titre: Support Explained in 3 Minutes - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: in this video we go over what makes a champion a support how to be a good support and how supports can convert their early game into mid and late game victories so what makes a champion a good support well supports usually fall into one of three roles a tank an enchanter or a bully tank supports will have large amounts of CC which they can use to engage and even with far less gold than your average tank they will still be tanky enough to get off all their CC in a team fight next enchanters bring utility to keep their carries alive they have abilities to Shield heal and importantly disengage fights they don't want to be in allowing the support and their carry to be pickier in fights they want to take finally we have the bullies these are champions that want to kill the opposition to get ahead in Lane these Champions tend to bring far less engage or disengage but are able to bring large amounts of damage while still on low income without many items during the laning phase there are three main aspects to focus on firstly trading this is understanding that most fights that either side start means losing some health and dealing some damage understanding how to take good trades where you deal more damage than you take is important to know if a trade is good or not you need to consider attack range spells cooldowns minions and if their spells will hit through minions this will course change depending on each matchup and each scenario but Windows to look out for are if your opponent uses their spells on minions or simply miss their spells if your opponent's a lower Mana or if you have a minion advantage in addition asset support you are also in the lane with a bot laner this means when considering trading you need to make sure your carries also in range so they can trade and there's also double the opponents to keep track of this can make trading far riskier as there's two lots of cooldowns coming towards one of you but of course that works both ways so you also have two lots of cooldowns to go towards one of their Champions understanding your matchup with what you do your ally does and what your opponents can do to you it's all crucial in deciding if a trade is good or not your second Focus will be on warding to keep you and your carry safe junglers love ganking and botslane this is because twice the potential money for them and setting two Champions back rather than just one is incredibly efficient in addition you're also on the bottom side of the map which is where the dragon is so killing a box lane or simply chasing them out of Lane means pretty easy and guaranteed dragons but thankfully as supports you have tons of War to protect yourself and your carry with on the blue side you always want to control wood in your try brush following this make sure you have a normal ward in either the riverbrush the area between Dragon pit and their jungle entrance or over the wall in this area and a good place for your second control Ward is either in your other bot side jungle entrance in the river brush if you can defend it or in their jungle entrance in this brush if you want to be cheeky on the red side it's often a good idea to have control ward in your jungle entrance and then the second control Ward can go in the river brush if you can defend it and then you want normal Wards in their try brush and then either in the river brush or the area between dragon and your jungle entrance the final aspect to focus on is roaming as a support you do not always need to be in Lane if the enemy isn't there or the wave is crashing into your turret often the best thing to do is to let your carry have the solo experience and this frees up time for you to influence other parts of the map where you often don't bring large amounts of damage the utility and CC you provide when ganking a mid laner or invading with your jungler is often enough to secure kills entering the mid game you're looking to take the bot Tower with your carry and then roam with their mid this places the two of you in the center of the map which allows for fast rotations to objectives or any fights looking like they might start from here you either want to set up aggressive Wards in their jungle entrances so you can see isolated Champions to pick off and convert into objectives or set up defensive Wards your team can Farm safely and Siege Towers also before an objective comes up make sure you've got Wards in their jungle entrances and all their Visions swept out

titre: How To Win More League of Legends Games (No, Seriously)
transcription: today I'm going to help you win more League of Legends games so uh yeah I mean you read the title this year I hit Grandmaster with a pretty solid win rate posted some nice scores on a multitude of Champions and styles drop some nukes for fun tactical nuke incoming use some unique pocket picks like Lilia mid to get some cool solo kills oh my and even had a few good Smurf account win rates playing every role in the game although I will say I really want to do a support only account soon but you know the purpose of me showing you this is not to inflate my ego it's not for me to tell you that I think I'm the perfect player and that I never make mistakes I'm not competing for a rank one Challenger and I'm certainly not a pro I can get better I can improve and I do make are not all weighted equally they range from extremely basic stuff that only a new player wouldn't understand to things that might even help high low players so let's start with some of the general likely that you're using far too many spells in the early game on the wave when you should be using it on Champions at high levels of play you'll see both players understand that in order to create an there is a huge difference between fighting somebody who has tabies and somebody who doesn't even if somebody is low and looks like a free kill if at any time the enemy is two or or three levels up on this is why levels matter so much on all of the Champions that you play you have to learn how to wave clear properly if you take a collie for example if you don't take the time to learn how to line up passive to Auto the cannon minion to clear the wave faster then you're playing silus wrong if you're wasting needless amounts of mana cooldowns and Time by wave clearing incorrectly then you are at a massive disadvantage forget fancy combos in the practice tool forget get items in runes none of those things are more important than learning how to wave clear correctly so spend the time to learn how to do it efficiently on the Champions that you play as a laner you need to think about jungle timings like getting ganked at 3 minutes and 30 seconds more or less all of the viable meta junglers will finish full clearing at 3:30 so if you haven't seen the jungler yet on the map and you can infer where they started based on who leashed you know the only thing the jungler can do during that time is gank on the opposite side of the map all of their camps are gone so if they're able to get a successful gank on you during this time the chances are likely that you just made a stupid mistake because this is when it's very obvious don't die to it jungling is the single highest impact role in the game but it comes at the cost of being very frustrating to play in the most mentally taxing if you don't understand waves which lanes are gankable and if you're constantly full clearing without watching the lane States then you are not jungling you're just a spectator if you can't clear your jungle efficiently then you need to keep improving because because every second as a jungler counts you should practice a little bit every day you can't be actively looking at your character jungling and watching yourself use the abilities because then you're not soaking in any information on your screen you will see that all highlevel junglers gank early and gank often the idea is that you want to start with a lead and then use that advantage to bleed out your opponent and force plays in the enemy jungle I see a lot of lower rated players make the mistake of thinking that jungle during the early game is formulaic they come up with the plan like I'm going to farm here clear then and then I'll gank bot and then I'll reset and then go here and then gank here Etc what makes jungle so difficult to learn is that you can't think like this you have to be able to adapt on the Fly and understand so much more about League of Legends than a lot of the other roles in this game you don't want to hard Farm early unless it's a very specific type of game State such as playing hecm or carus and clearly none of your lanes are gankable you want to start out the game strong and give a few camps if you can gank because then with the lead you can cycle perfectly and farm quickly if bot Lane is ever gankable during the early parts of the game you better believe a Korean jungler is going straight there they're not even going to finish their Camp first if they can get a kill they will once again Dobby has a great video showing Canyon this time in an absolutely perfect situation to explain what I mean about understanding the lane States the best junglers in the world know how to find ganks all over the map and keep up great Tempo because they know how laning phase actually works in this example the Nar really needs to shove out this terrible Lane State both him and the gragas are low so Nar needs to look to push out in recall because the idea is that a good player knows he can't really just sit here on one HP and no punishment is going to come the jungle rooll exists and we have to respect it even if Nar dies solo to this Gras if he can push out the whole wave into the turret the following waves will come back to him and he won't lose any Farm Canyon is looking all over the map and looking at the lanes while clearing his camps and realizes that the Nars wave state is extremely screwed over and he needs to base right now so Canyon just shows up and ruins Top Lane for the rest of the game it sounds so simple when you put it like this when I show such a good example it makes it seem obvious but trust me league is a complicated video game and the amount of ganks that a low low player will either over force or not even see as an opportunity because they don't move their camera around enough is costing them games it's really hard to have good map awareness it takes a lot of brain power and mechanics but you can practice it as for helping the lanes if your laners have a frozen wave on them and they can't play the game you need to hover for them so they can fix it the jungler doesn't necessarily always have to either be farming or ganking sometimes hovering is your best bet you don't need to think about jungle like a stealth assassin even if you are quite literally playing a stealth assassin it is okay if the enemy knows where you are sometimes if you end up having to show in the Top Lane to help your laner fix an extremely bad Lane state that is okay it's important to understand as a lowy low player for both the laner and the jungler this is not taxing nobody is taxing your wave to fix a horrible Lane State like this you would lose so much more experience in Gold if the wave wasn't fixed the final section of this video is all about mental which is just as important as gameplay and it's safe to say that the league Community is uh let's just say a different breed I don't know I'm pretty sure 90% of solo Cube players belong in a special facility where they can be monitored but H that's neither here nor there either way be the change you want to see in the world and I am officially from this point forward making a vow to always be a good teammate always try my best and have a good mental mute all is a powerful tool and so is the deafen function flaming getting frustrated and losing your ment Al helps nothing and solves nothing if this video game ends up making you so upset that you're raging out of your mind either take a break or don't play at all I'm manad enough to admit that I haven't been the best version of myself playing this game before and I'm going to try really hard to bring actual positivity back into this extremely toxic game it starts with me and I'm going to do it other than just pure toxicity let's talk about how you should approach the game from a mental standpoint as well the best Elden ring player is Gino machino and he has accomplished things in this game that nobody else is even close to matching yet and when asked by his chat about how he does what he does he responds with a very simple philosophy that he likes to live by learn first and then win and league is no different from this at all you must first learn before you can win if you are hyperfocused fixated and obsessed with your current LP and the result of your very next game then you are looking at the improvement process incorrectly you cannot get better if you're worried about the the result of your next five games you need to be concerned about the result of your next 100 LP will go up and LP will go down win streaks and lose streaks will happen both but what determines a good player is their success over the long term it's about performing and consistently having a high impact over your games over the course of a whole season in Solo qu you have to carry if you don't win your lane or get ahead help the team secure crucial objectives not feed your 1K shutdown if you miss all of your thres hooks if you're not a massive reason why the team is winning then you will not win period the reality is that your team is going to feed your team is going to make mistakes you will play against strong champions in the meta and none of that should be the determining Factor on whether or not you climb it is your job to perform and play well and carry the game but you can do it you can learn you can improve and even if you don't the final thing I have to say is that as long as you're having fun and playing because you enjoy the video game for what it is then you are winning in my book be 1K games hardstuck be an a casual play Norms with your friends who cares it's a game enjoy it and I hope you enjoyed this video thank you so much for watching and let me know what you thought in the comments down below

titre: The WORST League Advice EVERYONE Tells You To Follow!
transcription: League educational content is filled with advice that sounds super insightful and helpful, but it's not at all how high ELO players actually win games. For example, how many times have you heard something like, "Good players don't chase random kills, they prioritize objectives [music] instead." Well, here is a game from a rank one player called Davemon. His Talon looked to push mid looked to push mid and get two towers while he was fighting a random skirmish in bot lane. Why do they not just walk down and get four kills? I don't get it. But yes, I understand two two towers, but like we can get the kills and the towers. We get the kills as a team and then we do this play as a team. It's just funny to hear a rank one player complain that his teammates are over prioritizing objectives instead of rotating to a random fight over nothing. It's the exact opposite of what you'd expect from a top tier player and I kind of agree with him, especially because I've seen firsthand what this type of advice does to the player base. My favorite low ELO moment to this day is a game where I got a quadra kill by not rotating to dragon and my Master Yi had a mental breakdown. He basically just rage quit and AFK'd the whole game because I got a quadra kill instead of helping him get a single dragon. It's genuinely insane. If someone ever gets a quadra kill in high ELO, you usually just want to FF the game. It's not black and white like coaches or guides make it out to be. Sometimes kills are really valuable and sometimes objectives matter more. It just depends. If there's a Kha'Zix running around with 10 kills one shotting your entire team, getting two dragons is probably not going to stop him. And that's what I dislike the most. There's so much random advice out there that sounds good on paper like play for objectives, but doesn't actually translate to helping you win more games and in fact could be hurting the way you actually view League and your performance. So, I want to cover the advice I hear the most often that I really disagree with and strong side versus weak side is one of the worst things I've ever heard. It's a pretty straightforward concept. Coaches or guides like even our own videos in the past will tell you that playing around strong members on your team is good. If you make a play around someone who is actually strong, your play is more likely to work. Likewise, if you group around your weak teammates, your play is more likely to fail, so you should stay away from them. Basically, you should always try to be on the side of the map with your strong teammates. I'm not saying this is 100% wrong. There is some truth to it, but it's definitely not a rule high ELO players follow that regularly. In fact, here's the clip that actually inspired me to make this video. I was watching Nattinat, a rank one jungler, and his Fiora just died again to the Quinn 1v1. When he presses tab and looks at the scoreboard, you would think, "Okay, we should stay the hell away from Fiora, play around Jhin, or maybe Orianna. She's also doing okay-ish. So, maybe we should position on the bot side of the map and play around Drake around these two laners instead." Then Fiora types, "Guys, let's just kill Quinn." And then both Nattinat and his Leona as jungle support, the playmaking roles in high ELO, are both top side. They're actively playing around the feeding Fiora. Challenger players mostly just try to go to the area of the map where they think they can make a play. It doesn't matter who's strong or weak, it just depends. And this advice is even worse in low ELO because it makes even less sense. You shouldn't avoid pathing to the fed Draven lane. If I'm a mid laner and I see my own bot lane is losing and the enemy Draven has five kills, I'm still probably going to go down there because that's the easiest lane to roam to ever. That Draven is going to mindlessly shove every single wave no matter what and he is the freest shut down. Why would I avoid going down there? Now, what you should actually avoid is one, awkward transitions like this one, and two, a really famous quote by Dopa. Dopa, unless you've lived under a rock your whole life, is one of the most revered solo queue players of all time. And he has this really famous quote that people I coach often remind me about. There is no such thing as an aggressive or defensive player, there is only good and bad players. This sounds like a wisdomy old man giving great insight type of quote until you think about it for more than five seconds. Players often bring this up to me when trying to figure out what the correct play in a certain situation is, but I find this to be pointless as there's just so many different ways to play League. So, for example, Davemon, a player we just looked at before, is one of the most aggressive players I've ever laned against. He plays Pyke mid, Akshan, Qiyana, and he's always looking to play aggressive in lane, look for solo kills, and go crazy on the map with high kill count games. On the other hand, this is Qipik. If I had to describe him, he is a gathering storm enthusiast. He's a control mage or a Sona type of player. He avoids volatile situations, always runs a greedy scaling setup, and is a very consistent low death count type of player. Both of them have hit rank one on the ladder around the same season despite having very, very different approaches to the game. My opinion is that everyone tends to have a preferred style of gameplay that they're better at and that they also enjoy more. And this might be a crazy thing to say these days, but players tend to perform better when they're actually having fun and playing the way they like to. I am personally a very control oriented type of player. And if you see me at the Magic the Gathering table, I'll be the guy playing a counter spell every single turn. So, I tend to perform much better when I play a very slow controlling style where I slowly choke the life out of my opponent, in game of course. So, my main champions tend to reflect that. I main Malzahar, Twisted Fate, and Lulu. That's right, I'm a terrible person. If I tried to teach my style to a Yasuo or Talon player, even if it technically works, they would likely hate it, get bored, and play much worse. That's why I really like encouraging my students to learn their own style. And the key detail here is to learn the difference between objective fundamentals versus play style. An objective fundamental is something you will never hear any top player argue against. Examples of this would be when and how to slow push, cross mapping, fighting on item spikes, and stuff like that. These are fundamentals you cannot argue against. They're just objectively kind of true. On the other hand, let's say your rise and baron is spawning in 1 minute. You could group with your team to 5v5 or you can split and let them fend for themselves 4v5. I don't really care what the theoretically correct answer here is. It is much more effective for climbing to do the thing that you are good at. So, if you're good at causing chaos and like playing that way, go split. Or if you think you're better in coordinated fights, go team fight. Both are completely fine options. There is no correct way to play League yet because the game is too complex and random. So, the simplest way to improve is first, learn the objective fundamentals, then find a high ELO creator that seems to think and play in a way that you prefer. They do not have to be the best, just find high ELO players that think very similarly to you and copy them. For example, I do not like how Faker plays. I don't care that he's won six world championships and he's the goat. I just dislike his play style. It is definitely not my vibe. So, I tend to perform much better when I copy players like Chovy or even Nemesis who play closer to how I prefer to play. That's why we also make sure to have multiple different perspectives for climbing efficiently. For example, we partnered with Coach Chippys, one of the most respected top lane coaches out there, but we also have our own top lane course for players who prefer a different play style. It's the same for ADC. We have our standard course, but we also have a course where I coach the player to diamond, which is mostly focused on split pushing as an ADC, which is a perspective you're not really going to get anywhere else besides me, I think. We also have a huge variety of jungle courses and we've also partnered with Coach Rogue for a macro course while also having our own perspective on macro. That's all while also having some of the top challenger players showing you how to carry in your own ELO, uploading pure educational commentaries weekly. That's why we're positive we are by far the best educational platform on the internet for improving at League simply because we have the most resources possible for players to improve, which is important because like I said, every player is different and finding content that fits your style is critical for your success. We're so confident that we have the right tools to help you improve that we offer a money back guarantee if you don't actively climb while using our service. If we somehow have nothing to offer you, that's completely fine. You take absolutely no risk, you get your money back, and you can then go find another educational source that might be more beneficial to you. So, if you want to start climbing fast, use the discount link below to sign up to Skill Capped. Moving on, the next piece of advice that I think makes very little sense is using your F keys. This is especially true for junglers because you would always hear stuff like, "As you do camps, you need to cycle your F keys to get intel on every single lane so you know how to adapt your route to impact the map or something." What's funny though is that if you tune into any top tier jungler, for example, here's Canyon, possibly the greatest jungler of all time, he is just staring at his own camps, not scouting around at all. there's quite a few reasons for this and I'd actually advise most players to not use F keys all that often. First, the positives do not outweigh the negatives. The biggest positive of using your F keys is information gathering, but not all information is useful. Sometimes your bot side and you scout your top laner. Now what? 99% of the time, the information you gather isn't going to change what you were going to do anyway. So, there is a slight positive to getting information, but now let's talk downsides. First and foremost, it is super tryhard and can quickly burn you out. It is so much effort to constantly pan your camera everywhere. The best way to improve at League is simply to play a lot and you're going to burn out quicker and play far less if you're spamming F keys omega tryharding every single game. Secondly, it is important to focus on what's happening in front of you most of the time. So, as a top laner, you're not going to randomly F key in the middle of lane to randomly look somewhere else, right? And even as a jungler, the number one skill to being a really good jungler is often just killing your camps quite efficiently, which is why high ELO junglers are hyper focused on themselves rather than looking around. Being 10 seconds faster on my full clear is going to matter way more than getting random information on the map. And finally, you can get 99% of the benefits of F keys by just looking at your mini map. If I see my teammate's icon on top of the enemy icon and my teammate is losing HP, I know I should pan my camera over there because there's probably something going on. That's what Nattinat did here in that earlier example. He had his camera on himself farming, then the second he sees Fiora fighting, he pans his camera over. The next thing to look out for is, let's say I'm Twisted Fate mid, and I see Ezreal's icon randomly shift. That probably means he's used his E, and I have a 20-second window to kill him with old. Here's a great example of that. Catavolt, another rank one player, looks to push top lane, and I know for a fact he is glaring at his mini map because the second that Alister's icon appears to dash, he's already moving his camera to the fight. Then he realizes his Karma is likely fine, so he positions his camera back to himself and focuses on pushing. Then the moment that Kai'Sa's icon comes in dashing, he instantly moves his camera again and rotates to the fight. Basically, the way most top tier players actually gather information is just through the mini map. They often just keep their screens on what they're doing, and if they see action on the map, then they might use their F key or mouse over there. You definitely don't need to use F keys to be good at this game as most pros don't use them all that much in the first place. Besides Faker, of course, but like he's Faker, okay? He makes no sense. And in the same way that F keys are overhyped, so is players telling you that you need 10 CS per minute to be successful. I see this problem all the time in low elo, players trying to copy high elo players from YouTube videos. Wow, he has 10 CS per minute and has a ton of kills. I need to do that too if I want to climb. And I just watch so many low elo players not understand what's happening, and so they try to get to the 10 CS per minute mark, and they're just AFK farming the entire match doing nothing. If we look at Faker's account, here's a realistic view of what your CS score should look like. He averages like eight to nine CS per game, sometimes dipping to a seven average. Here's what's actually going on. Proper league macro is about making plays based on pushing side waves most of the time. So you push side, then you make a play. When you're winning, you can do that all of the time because when your teammates are doing well, you can push side lanes safely because you know they're likely fine without you. And if you're catching every single side lane wave plus a jungle camp here and there, you will probably have 10 to 11 CS per minute. But when your team is either even or losing, you can't afford to leave them alone for too long. You're going to have to sack wave every now and then to join them, so you probably won't get perfect CS. This is usually what I try to teach players. Don't focus too much on the actual amount of CS you get. Instead, focus on putting pressure on the map by constantly pushing minion waves, but make sure to also react to map plays when needed. If you average around seven to nine CS per game, and you still feel like you're a part of a lot of the action on the map, this is usually a good indication that you're playing pretty well. But you're probably not performing well every single game. Sometimes you're running it down, and you're just feeding kills over and over. Here is one of the worst pieces of advice that actually doesn't come from other high elo players, but low elo players trying to help each other out instead. It goes something like this. One of the best skills to learn is how to get carried when you're losing. You won't be the main character every game. Control your ego and know when to play safe and win off your teammates. I absolutely detest this advice for two different reasons. Let's start with the first, mentality. You are not going to climb to emerald or diamond or whatever elo you want by winning a single game and getting 20 LP because you play safe and got carried. You're going to climb by being better than everyone in your elo, and you do that by constantly limit testing and playing to improve. Having no faith in yourself and giving up an entire game of practice because you don't want to get flamed for feeding is not how you get better. Secondly, and more importantly, it's not even correct to play safe. When you are behind, dying matters less. You give less gold, less experience, and you have no shutdown, so you shouldn't be afraid of taking risks because if things go wrong, it's not like they get that much worse. It's your teammates who are ahead who are the ones who need to be playing safer. This doesn't mean that you just run into your opponents and die on repeat, of course, but if you think there's a 15 to 30% chance of a play working, you shouldn't be afraid to take those odds. This is actually exactly how TheBaus plays most of the time. He will completely into lane, giving his opponent multiple kills, but he's not afraid to keep taking fights he thinks he can maybe win because it gives him a chance to come back. The plays keep going wrong because they had a low chance of working, and sure, he falls further and further behind, but eventually, he might get lucky and randomly outplay his opponent, and now he's back in the game. That's how most good players actually play when they're behind. You take risks to come back into the game because you're already behind anyway, so who cares if you keep dying? And now for a quick final tip that I really hate. I don't care how many videos or coaches tell you that mid tier one tower is super important for opening up the map or whatever other nonsense, you should stop randomly grouping and sieging the tower, risking the entire game for no reason. Mid tier one matters, it is nice when it goes down, but the game isn't going to magically dramatically change when you take it, so don't lose your mind trying to break it. Anyway, remember at Skillcapped, we have by far the highest variety in content to help you improve. If you want to climb fast, use the discount link below. See you next time.

titre: EVERYTHING you NEED to KNOW to become a VISION MASTER! - League of Legends
transcription:
Vision control in League of Legends isn't
the hottest topic that gets players excited, but it's certainly one of the most powerful
ways to win the game for you and your team. The beauty of mastering it is that your vision
success is largely in your control regardless of your teammates. It also helps everyone on your team at all
times, not just yourself. The better your vision, the less likely your
allies get picked off. Not only does it reduce the likelihood of
your team handing over kills, but it enables them to more consistently net their own kills
AND secure objectives. While these perks may sound obvious now that
we're highlighting it, they're massively important. You should always remember that learning vision
control is going to help you improve significantly - more so than mastering the most efficient
Zed combo, or whatever. Vision is something players immediately associate
with supports, and while it's true that they will have more agency when it comes to placing
and denying vision, anyone can apply the concepts we'll be going over here in just a second. Okay, all that being said, you should be sold
that mastering vision will rock your world, so let's dive into today's guide where we're
going to teach you the most important concepts for mastering vision in League of Legends. Then at the end, we'll be sharing our favorite
warding tips and tricks that most players don't know exist. Let's get started! The most important battle between two teams
should be around deep vision. If you watch lol-esports, then you've definitely
heard this term tossed around constantly. Analysts are always going on about who has
deep vision, how well they're controlling it, how they are or aren't playing around
it, etc. It's by far one of the most discussed topics
in any competitive game. Simply put, deep vision is exactly what you
know or think it is. It's just having wards deeper on the enemy's
side of the map, rather than in the river or your own side of the jungle. The benefits are massive, so let's cover why
teams do it first, and then give you some guidelines to help you establish it yourself
in your own games. Honestly, the biggest upside is really simple. You get to see everything your opponent does
earlier than otherwise. How many times have you been ganked playing
aggressively, even though you had a ward and spotted the enemy jungler? Any vision is good, but shallow vision doesn't
give you that much time to react to things. Even if you see a play coming, you could be
in too deep to respond anyways. Deep vision on the other hand would give you
plenty of time to not only finish your aggressive trade, but back off before being ganked. One of the biggest wastes we see in regards
to deep vision is in objective control. Over the years, players are generally getting
smarter and smarter and know to show up to objectives before they spawn to establish
vision control. Oftentimes though, players in solo queue just
lounge around and only get vision on the actual objective itself, rather than invading for
more control. This lack of threat makes it incredibly easy
for the enemy team to walk into them, which usually just ends up being a coinflip fight. Ideally, what you want to do is arrive at
the objective, quite a bit before it spawns. Then, see if you can move into the enemy's
jungle and place some deep wards. The advantages you gain from doing this are
massive. For starters, you can see exactly how the
enemy team is collapsing to the objective. Are they all together? Is their approach disjointed? All these questions are answered faster for
you, and you can potentially take advantage of any flaws the enemy team may have in their
approach to the fight. Not only that, but for skill-shot champions
in particular, establishing deep vision before an objective spawns is one of the most brutal
things you can do to your opponents. They *have to* walk into you, and if they're
walking in blind, it makes landing your spells that much easier. Look how impossible it is for the enemy team
to even think about trying to fight over the Baron here. Walking into Morgana, Ashe and a Lux from
the fog of war is just devastating. And one of the best advantages of fighting
over deep vision when it comes to objectives is the fact that you're denying vision from
the objective itself. Let's show you what we mean. The Khazix this game is heading towards the
dragon, since his team is sitting on soul point, therefore a fight is sure to erupt
around it considering how important an objective that is. Getting there early, Khazix moves in leading
his team into the enemy's jungle to get some forward control before the dragon spawns. As they approach, he fights to keep them away
from the dragon itself, since he doesn't want them to have any vision on it whatsoever. The confrontation continues until a tragedy
happens. Senna gets hooked while Zed blinks onto some
traps. Both carries messing up like this is usually
awful, but thanks to his prior work, Khazix still salvage the situation. He knows that the enemy team never got a ward
on the dragon itself, so he leaps over and begins sneaking it while his entire team collapses. Before they realize what's going on, Kha has
nearly solo'd the dragon and can easily secure the smite with his Q, getting the soul and
proceeding to skitter away. Looking back, yes, the enemy team could have
potentially seen Khazix leap over and start the buff. But in a chaotic situation, it can be hard
to deduce exactly what's going on and react appropriately. Had they had an actual ward on the objective,
they would have reacted instantly to the sneak, and salvaging this situation would not have
been possible. It's good to know how great deep vision can
be in helping you achieve your goals, but there's probably a problem you're already
thinking about when it comes to applying it in your own games. Most of us aren't playing in either Challenger
elo or a competitive team, which means your teammates are definitely not going to help
you establish it. While it's true that the lower elo brackets
care less and less about vision, it doesn't mean that you'll never receive help whether
it's intentional or not. Take this game as an example. Leona was rotating towards bottom to help
her team in a small skirmish. She doesn't get there in time, but makes use
of the fact that the Khazix was also in the area at the time. It's not that Khazix was purposefully here
to help Leona establish deep vision. He just happened to be in the area, and Leona
makes use of that to ensure her own safety as she places some wards in the enemy jungle. While you should be looking to use the random
times your teammates happen to be in an area, that is definitely not the most consistent
approach you'll be taking to getting deep vision. The more reliable way is to actually scout
out the map, and find windows of opportunity where you know you'll be safe, regardless
of being alone. As an example, Morgana was going for a very
safe ward over the dragon pit, since she had no idea where the enemy jungler was. But when the Sejuani is spotted ganking top
lane, Morgana immediately shifts gears and invades completely alone. This is how you'll be looking to establish
deep vision in solo queue. Your opponents will eventually show themselves
on the map, and when you know where everyone is you can use those opportunities to invade
their jungle and place some wards. Her efforts bear fruit a minute later, when
she and her team are chasing the enemy Tristana for a kill. The vision gives Cassiopeia the confidence
to flash forward knowing that Tristana is completely alone, and there's no one behind
her in the jungle. Maybe Cassio does that anyways, sure. But this way, her safety is guaranteed, rather
than being a massive question mark. There's another method you'll want to employ,
which we'll be referring to as leapfrog warding. The idea is pretty simple. When your team has a set of wards already
placed on the map, you can then use those wards to move forward a bit further and place
slightly deeper vision. Then you repeat the process until you have
the vision you ideally want. It's not completely without risk, but leapfrog
warding is relatively safe, since you're only moving past your own team's vision line a
little bit each time you do it. Let's look at an actual example from a Challenger
game. At the start of the clip, Rell is fighting
over some vision control around Baron, since it will be spawning in about a minute or so. But then she notices her team likely isn't
going to be playing for Baron. Nidalee is bot side, and Tristana is moving
to split push the bottom lane. Thus, the vision she'll want to place should
be to enable her Tristana to push bot safely. With 2 wards already placed in the enemy jungle
from before, Rell and Kalista feel relatively safe walking a bit further to ward the brush
near the raptor camp. Kalista knows that the dangerous part is over,
and leaves to push mid. Rell proceeds to use her vision to move a
bit further in and place a ward here. At this point Rell knows from the minimap
that 3 enemies are ganking her Kennen in top. Although her team isn't super close, since
she's playing so close to her own vision, it makes her feel safe enough to jump into
the last brush and place another ward deeper into enemy territory. That was super simple and not fancy at all,
and it's not meant to be. Leapfrogging your vision should always feel
safe, as you're not walking that far away from the area you control in the first place. And don't think this is something only supports
can do with a pocket full of wards. Looking back at the start, Rell was mostly
playing off her team's already established vision. She then placed 1 control ward, a single regular
ward, and then another control ward at the end. That's an amount of vision anyone can carry
in their bags. Okay, with the core fundamentals of deep vision
covered, let's go over some more general tips that you can immediately implement in solo
queue that will really amp up your vision game. First up is the cheesy control wards that
you always find in your own jungle. These are incredibly useful and something
that you want to be placing nearly every single game. There's 2 main reasons why. One, they can spot a lot of enemy rotations,
as these are high traffic areas that players are constantly pathing around. And two, players tend to be quite lazy when
it comes to checking for them. Even though they're a bit cheesy, these wards
are incredibly valued by high elo players. During this Challenger game, we even see two
at the same time placed in the enemy's blue side jungle granting a ton of information
about what's going on in that area. During that same game, we see the enemy Nautilus
just walking right on by one of the brushes that had a control ward in it. That's why they're so good. It's not that he doesn't know about these
wards, every challenger players does, but it can be very easy to be on auto-pilot and
forget to check these brushes every single time you walk past them. Moving on, when it comes to deep vision, you
very often want to set it up and stay in the area playing around it and defending your
wards, but that's not always going to be possible. Often times, you're going to be placing your
vision and immediately backing off. What can be frustrating is having your wards
immediately cleared after you leave, and you're left wondering what the point even was in
trying to get vision. This is a tip you're not always going to use,
but you should depending on how well the enemy team is coordinating their anti-warding efforts. These are some very common defensive ward
placements the enemy team will utilize to deny your deep vision. Knowing these yourself is obviously useful
as well, when you're the one playing defensively. As a counter to these wards, you should potentially
look to place these less efficient, but sneakier wards instead. They won't grant you the same value in terms
of how much vision they grant, but it's better to have some vision rather than none. Often times, with a full set of control wards
your opponents may feel safe in their own jungle thinking their rotations are unspotted,
when in reality you know exactly what's going on thanks to these sneaky wards. As for our final tip, we'll just show you
some warding tricks you should definitely try to learn, so you can always obtain the
best vision possible. Some of these, especially this first one are
a bit difficult to consistently pull off, so you'll need to practice them in the training
tool when you get the chance. Of course, you don't have to learn all of
these, in fact, most players don't know them all. That being said, you should certainly care
for the ones that are specific to your own lane at the very least. Not only those, but these baron and dragon
ward tricks are crucial if you want the highest chance of securing those objectives. There are times you can't walk into the enemy
jungle for vision, and knowing how to place these can potentially be game-winning. When it comes to warding, remember that following
guidelines is important, and you'll never go wrong placing the wards that a guide has
encouraged you to place. That being said, you should practice your
vision control each game and begin to tailor what wards will help you the most based on
the specific circumstances you encounter in each game. Ideal vision changes based on who is winning,
what the team comps are and a ton of other factors. Alright, that's going to wrap up this guide. We hope you enjoyed and we'll see you next
time. Cheers!

interaction you want to follow this general rule if you have no threat of being invaded you actually want to Smite a little bit before the camp dies for example at the start of the game Smite does 600
