use that ability and look at the damage it does then add that to your Smite damage to get an exact number you're going to be smiting at all right next let's talk about the mechanics you need to speed up how fast you actually damage your camps first for any Champion that has an ability that locks them out of Auto attacking you should weave these in between the natural rhythm of your auto attacks for example Auto ability Auto on many champions will lead to nice smooth Camp clears without losing any DPS from Auto attacks any ability that interrupts your auto attack should be used in this way secondly abilities that reset your auto attack come into play when your Champion has an empowered Auto attack these will directly change the Rhythm that you attack in because they will alter the start and end points of your animations make sure when using these that you allow your auto attack to finish releasing before using the ability even if your ability comes up at the start of your auto attack it's going to be more efficient to wait just to get one more Auto in as most of your damage will be coming from your basic attacks the next thing to be on the lookout for are abilities that have animations you can combine together this reduces the amount of time they're stuck in their cast animation and fit multiple abilities between Auto attacks for example on kha'zix you'd want to use q and W together during the camp clear to increase DPS if we simply use one ability between each Auto attack we would have to do something like Auto q auto W Auto this means that our W would be up for longer not being used which causes us to lose DPS and clear slower instead we can cancel our W animation wind down with q and fit both within our Auto attack window meaning we can do Auto w q auto getting more spells within the same amount of time you should also be aware of Animation cancels on Mobility spells the most famous example is cane where if you cue Point Blank into a wall it's going to cast way faster than if you use it regularly a more advanced example of this is with Graves where with the right angle you can basically completely cancel the E animation while still getting the auto reset the last spell optimization to be aware of is to not treat your jungle clear as individual Camp speedruns sometimes you need to make a decision that will slow down the time it takes to kill one camp but leads to an overall faster clear because it saves more time on a future Camp if you can't pull the next Camp towards you you're going to have to walk the entire distance which can really slow you down obviously going over terrain saves a lot of walking time and because of this you can sacrifice DPS on one Camp to make up for it with movement efficiency getting to the next for example even though jarvin could e here to increase damage on his red buff it will be more efficient to have his EQ up to dash to kugs to save the hassle of walking around if he just used e right away he would not have it up after killing red meaning any DPS he gains Now by using it he simply loses and more by having to walk to kugs for Champions with high cooldown abilities with low damage it can mean not using that ability at all on a camp even though it's up just to save more time by using it to get to the next Camp instead now this entire time we've been focused on optimizing how we clear camps that's only one part of the game a huge skill junglers have to master is reading the map and knowing what Lanes they should be ganking this is where the mechanic of fkey cycling comes in if you watch any pro player or Challenger stream you'll notice them quickly cycle their camera across their teammates to get insane map vision super fast without fkey cycling you'll have to first look at the mini map then identify where your teammates actually are then move your cursor very accurately to click on them and if at any point you miss slightly you'll have to take time to adjust the camera and in the end it just takes an insane amount of time now there's actually a secret to setting this up like the pros go to settings H keys and camera control rebind select cect Ally 1 2 3 and 4 to the respective F Keys as shown you have to do this because by default F1 is bound to yourself which is what you already use your spacebar for now here's the thing the F keys on your keyboard are notoriously awkward and difficult to hit a lot of players just find them too uncomfortable especially if they have smaller hands if you have this problem we highly recommend trying out rebinding them to Z x c and V the nice thing about this is that if you rest your pinky finger on your shift key like a lot of players do it's it's really easy to just slide your pinky over to the Z key put the rest of your fingers on X C and V and then just hit them in sequence with your thumb hitting space bar at the end to return to yourself now here's the thing some people find fkey cycling to make check-in Lanes easier some find it awkward and prefer using their Mouse at the end of the day either way is fine the most important thing is you're actually checking your lanes here's how to check your lanes whether you're fkey cycling or using your mouse in a real game the first step is you'll be checking nearby Lanes in between camps so once we finish red we can input a movement command to our next camp kugs we no longer need to watch our champion so now we check both top and mid since these are the lanes closest to us this is a habit you want to develop for each Camp as soon as you're done clearing click to the next Camp then check your nearby Lanes either with fkey cycling or your mouse now the next step is checking lanes while doing camps this is tougher however there's a trick to it you want to time checking your lanes right after you've put all your abilities on cool down for example here we get to Raptors we Auto e for the auto reset and now with no abilities needed to be cast we check our nearby Lanes before jumping back to ourselves shortly after our q and E are coming up so we q auto e to Auto reset now we can check nearby Lanes again in this case only checking mid if we have a transition gank available before heading to our wolves and now that we're moving between camps we can check Lanes since we have nothing else to do you should also be aware another trick is to pay attention to health bars above the map often when one of your teammates is low in health it means the enemy will be too and can be a signal to check that lane if it's gankable addition Ally always be aware of player icons overlapping on the mini map when this happens 99% of the time a trade or Allin is happening and so it's a signal to check that lane to see if it's worth peeling off a camp or just heading straight for that lane after you're done with the current one and lastly you need to know how to count jungle CS each jungle Camp is worth four CS whether it's a grump or a crug camp clearing a camp adds four to your CS total on the scoreboard what this means is that if you spot an enemy jungler you can hit Tab and check their CS this is something you should do the first time you see the enemy jungler to confirm their route in this case it's obvious shyvana is level four which means she had to have full cleared but just to show you how it works we hit tab see she has 24 CS each Camp is worth 4 CS so when you add up all six camps of a full clear it equals 24 the reason this is useful is let's say we saw shyvana earlier on this Ward and she was still level three meaning she didn't full clear if we hit Tab and saw she had 20 CS well that means she's cleared five camps with her one Camp left likely being the kug camp the kug camp is super far off the map so not exactly easy for us to steal however if we hit Tab and saw she had 16 CS then she only cleared four camps and the two camps left could be crugs and Raptors this would be useful to know since her Raptors could be a good camp to try and attack once we finish our clear as it's easy to access counting CS is usually most useful though when junglers are pathing opposite to each other for example let's say I start red and the enemy is lein and starts at his red let's say I get to my top side I see Leon execute a really early gank bot side move my camera and see as Buffs then hit Tab and check his CS he only has 12 well since he has double Buffs that means he's killed his red and blue that's 8 Cs and so he's only killed one more camp this guarantees either his Raptors or kugs are still alive possibly both so since we pathed opposite sides after I finish my full clear I can move into his jungle looking to steal one of these camps before he's able to get there in time to defend it okay now let's pivot our attention to talking about some important mechanics around epic monsters like Baron Rift Herald and dragon the only thing you need to know for Baron is that it has a passive called Baron's gaze Baron Nasher takes 50% reduced damage for 8 seconds from the unit that it has most recently hit with a basic attack being effective only against one unit at a time this means you never want High DPS Champions tanking Baron's Auto attacks I see this mistake all the time instead your lowest damage and ideally decently tanky Champion should be most in front to aggro Baron's basic attacks and take the debuff for the rift Herald there's a few different mechanics you need to know firstly Rift Herald also has a gaze deu where the unit she most recently attacked will deal 50% reduced damage to it secondly when Rift Herald's eye is closed it lasts for 10 seconds but will be reduced by 2.5 seconds every time she's struck by a Champion's basic attack the eye cannot open more than once every 3 seconds though this means doing Rift Herald by yourself is always going to take significantly longer and thirdly hitting the eye in the back does 12% of the rift Herald's Max Health as true damage usually this will deal around 1,200 damage to the herald so if you don't have Smite and are worried about a being stolen you can actually purposely keep the eye alive and then hit it at 1200 HP to prevent being Smite stolen you can also use our previous tip about combining Smite by then adding your Smite damage on top of the 1200i damage typically your Smite will be dealing 900 damage when the herald is alive so when the eye opens you can leave it until around 2100 Health then hit the eye and smite it at the same time to secure it now our last two mechanics are quick simple and effective firstly as you send movement commands around the map you need to be switching that to an attack hack move command instead this is because as a jungler you're going to be the one most often running past control Wards I can't even begin to tell you how common it is for junglers to be running through brushes they move their camera to check a lane only to run past a control Ward not realizing they were just spotted you can even use this trick with your sweeping lens too where you activate it then simply send an attack move command and if you move your camera you'll still clear any wards that you spot and finally there's a mechanic you absolutely must know called residual Vision when a yellow Ward is cleared Vision in the area lasts for an entire 2 seconds much longer than players realize this allows you to see the direction of the enemy after they clear your ward but disinformation is one of the best ways to win a war so you should actively look to trick your opponents make it seem like you're going one way when in reality you've gone the complete opposite way once the residual vision is over you should also be aware that this 2cond window of vision persists even if it was revealed by a control Ward and speaking of control Wards this 2 seconds of residual vision is not a thing when it comes to control Wards when a control Ward is cleared Vision deactivates immediately this is also true for farsight Wards the blue ones so just remember control Ward equals no residual Vision when cleared and yellow Ward equals 2 seconds of residual vision and lastly if a ward simply expires when an enemy is nearby instead of being cleared then there's no residual Vision at all a yellow Ward needs to actually be cleared for that residual Vision to take place now if you've been stuck at your current rank as a jungler skill cap is what you've been looking for we just released the best jungle course on the entire internet literally it's five whole chapters teaching you everything you need to know to climb ranks fast as a jungler this is around 3 hours of content where you'll be walked through stepbystep how to carry as a jungler from the start of the game to the end pair that with our new Champion clear course that teaches you the fastest way to clear on your champion and trust me you're going to be climbing through the ranks in no time best part you can try all of this risk-free if you don't rank up while actively using skillcapped you'll get your money back no questions asked you can unlock all of this through the the exclusive discount you can only access through the link below so what are you waiting for click the link in the description below to get the rank you've always wanted all right and that will do it for this one we here at skillcapped want to thank you for watching and we'll catch you in the next one

titre: 250 CS at 20 MINUTES!? How YOU can FARM like the GOD CHOVY - League of Legends
transcription:
Chovy is without a doubt the King of Farming
all of League of Legends. Time and time again, he's out-farming every
single player he goes up against, regardless of what he's playing or what match-up he's
in. Absurd numbers like 250 CS at 20 minutes are
common for him for him in competitive play, and a big reason why he's regarded as one
of, if not the best mid laner in the world. Of course, we don't play in the LCK, but that
doesn't mean we can't learn from Chovy, since he gets insane CS numbers regularly even in
solo queue, where a ton of randomness can occur. This won't be a guide about being an AFK farmer
who never fights or helps his team. Chovy's goal in solo queue is to limit test,
and he definitely runs it down a lot during his games. And yet, he can have 5 deaths and he'll have
more CS than everyone in the game. He's in no way AFK in a side lane the whole
game doing nothing. He's constantly either grouping with his team
or looking for fights, while easily maintaining 10 cs per minute. There's no way we can teach you how to be
this good at League of Legends, but we can share some of the easy tips to apply that
Chovy utilizes all the time in order to get your CS numbers up. Let's get into it. it'd be weird to hear let's get into it and
then before getting into things Before getting into things though, here's
our question of the day. What's your most hated meta champion? Mine is definitely Qiyana. Ever since that one-trick demolished Faker
in a solo queue game, I've seen her so much more than before. There's nothing more frustrating than getting
rooted, into wall stunned, into an execute that one-shots you from 100% health. It feels like every little mistake is a death
sentence against her. But what do you guys hate playing against? What nerfs are you hoping for? Let us know in the comments below as we find
it very interesting to know what everyone hates playing against in their games. Moving on,
When it comes to CSing, the first thing to talk about is just scoring last hits. Unfortunately, this is something you have
to practice for yourself. Each champion is unique with their auto animations,
attack speeds, spell damage, etc. It's on you to learn how to farm as efficiently
as possible on each unique character you play in every match-up you go up against. That is not easy, and part of the reason why
Chovy is so successful. That being said, there are some tips we can
cover that Chovy always applies, which can help you come out with a bit more CS during
the lane, regardless of what you're playing, or your mastery on any champion. First up is something very basic, but if you're
not doing it, you'll immediately incrase how many last hits you score. When possible, you should simply try to kill
the enemy caster minions, before all the enemy melee minions die. Chovy does this quite frequently, as any good
mid laner should. By killing the caster minions first, you guarantee
that you're going to get the last hit on all of them, since your own minions are still
hitting the enemy melees. It's not something you should do all the time,
but if you're not under pressure in any way, then we suggest doing so. Secondly, Chovy loves playing incredibly aggressive
in lane. Now most professional players do this, since
solo queue is generally just a mode where they can limit test, but there is a neat advantage
when it comes to CSing by playing very far up in the lane. If you're playing very in your opponents face
near their tower, then the advantage you gain is that you can begin killing the wave before
it ever collides with your own. Just like the caster killing tip, it basically
guarantees that you score every CS in the wave since it will either only briefly touch
your own wave, or not get touched by it at all. This is just a really simple way of making
sure you don't miss any minions, since yours will never be in range to take them. Of course, there's obviously a downside to
doing so. By playing so aggressively, you're prone to
getting ganked, no? Absolutely, but as we said, that's a risk
Chovy is willing to take a lot of the time. He generally feels very comfortable outplaying
and evading ganks. But, even he's not perfect. Sometimes he makes mistakes, and gets caught
out. That being said, dying while you play aggressively,
is actually not that bad when it comes to keeping your farm up. We've touched on this before, but notice that
while he died, Chovy only missed a single wave to his tower. As he spawned, he managed to get back in time
to catch the next one. By playing as aggressively as this, you'll
never miss big chunks of minions, even when you do mess up and die. Despite that misplay, Chovy is still miles
ahead of anyone in CS, since he's scoring every single last hit possible. As quick reminder, if you want to improve
fast and get the rank you've always wanted then check out our hyper improvement system
at skill-capped.com. We have professional courses by top players,
smurf commentaries where a challenger player walks you through how to climb out of every
rank from iron to diamond, and we upload tons of new, exclusive guides to our website each
week. In fact, we're so confident you'll improve
using our system that if you don't climb at least 5 divisions while actively using skill-capped
you can claim a full refund, so there's no risk. What are you waiting for? check out skill-capped.com, and get the rank
you've always wanted, link in the description below. Alright, those previous early tips are gonna
help you CS a bit more during the early lane. But when it comes to getting 10 CS per minute,
your actions during the mid and late game matter a bit more than during the laning phase. One of the immediate things you want to do,
which many of us know to do, is simply to take your own junglers camps whenever possible. Of course, Chovy will have an inherent advantage
over you when it comes to this. He's Chovy. Imagine flaming Chovy. Any jungler who says anything to him is just
going to get flamed back by the rest of the team. Imagine taking both the blue buff and Gromp
in front of your jungler's face as they're pathing towards the area. Spam pinging will most definitely ensue a
lot of the time, if you're to try this. That's not how you take jungle camps from
your jungler away most of the time. You should just look for opportunities where
your jungler is on the other side of the map making a play and not pathing towards one
of the camps near you. For example, if you see your jungler committing
to a brush play in bottom, then they 100% don't care if you move on over to take their
raptors. They'll be up again by the time they get here
anyways. It's very easy to find windows of opportunity
for when you can do this. In this particular game, as Chovy chases for
a kill, his jungler isn't alive yet from the previous fight. It's fine to take every little camp you can
find when your jungler isn't going to get them anyways. It's very unlikely you get flamed doing it
this way. Okay, everything up until now is a basic of
accelerating your farm, but there's some less obvious strategies you'll want to incorporate
into your macro to farm like Chovy. Let's introduce this following concept as
a question. Imagine that there's 2 available plays for
you to make on the map. In bot lane, your Irelia is being shoved in
by a Jax, whom you could rotate over to kill. You also happen to see that the enemy jungler
seems to be power farming. They'll likely be making their way over to
the red side jungle soon. You could potentially set up in a brush and
kill them as they walk in. If the likelihood that both of these plays
will work is around the same, then which one would you choose? Okay, so what Chovy tries to do very often
is make decisions where his aggressive plays are going to lead him to more sources of farm. We believe that the jungle play might be a
little better in terms of getting you more CS. If you set up here and kill the enemy jungler,
then afterwards you're free to take their entire jungle for yourself, since you'll have
direct access to it by being in the area already. If you were to go bottom to kill Jax, then
you'll encounter an annoying problem. The Irelia is likely going to want to keep
pushing bottom. As the split pusher, she might get upset that
you're sticking around to take the waves. Let's show you what we mean in a real game. During this Tristana match, Chovy was finishing
pushing out his wave when he notices a skirmish going on in top. He makes his way there, taking the honeyfruit
on his path to restore some resources. He manages to get up here and clean up a kill
in return for his Senna's life. Now the point is that even if Senna lived,
Chovy would have priority for this farm, since he'd likely easily steal all of it from Senna,
as she's so terrible at last hitting. Regardless of what happened here, there was
an incoming source of farm for him to acquire, therefore he's both being proactive on the
map while maintaining his CS up. We see a similar situation a couple minutes
later. Renekton is playing very aggressively in bottom,
so Chovy rotates over to kill him. Remember our example from before? In this case, it would have been fine for
Chovy, even if Senna lived. She's going to go towards middle anyways,
as Tristana is obviously the champion who should stay down here split pushing. Regardless of the outcome, Chovy's rotation
allowed him to both make a play and acquire a new source of farm. He picked up countless waves in bottom and
the enemy tower, since he knew killing the enemy side laner would give him precious time
to farm alone down here. Our final tip for this guide is the something
Chovy is incredibly consistent about. He will almost always push out his side waves
before joining his team whenever possible. This isn't a new concept, but perhaps a different
way of looking at the situation. The problem a lot of people face is that they
don't trust their teammates enough to not run it down, while they push out a wave. It creates what we could call the "Solo Queue
Tax". If you're always responding to every little
thing going on and not trusting your team to fend for themselves, you're going to fall
more and more behind in CS. Sometimes it's best to just avoid paying your
taxes... in League of Legends. Most of the time Chovy treats solo queue as
if he has a competitive team. If he's able to, he *will* push out the side
wave before rotating over to join a fight. Now you may say, he can do this because it's
Korean Challenger solo queue. People are really smart there, I can't do
that. No, no they're not. They're just as blind and dumb as your own
teammates most of the time. Look, as Chovy is finishing pushing out the
wave his own teammates are dying defending a tower that wasn't defendable in mid. He finally teleports in, and thankfully his
Gnar lands a massive ultimate to turn the fight. Chovy scores some kills, and manages to get
some value out of his teleport. We chose this example to highlight something
critical to the way you view the game. As Chovy is pushing down here, we're not telling
you that letting you risk your team losing the game is worth pushing a single wave. If the enemy team is forcing Baron randomly,
then don't just sit bottom. You should be rotating over immediately. The better mindset is that its *your opponent's
risk* to aram in mid. Think about what the enemy team is actually
pressuring. In this case, they're just grouping to share
gold between 5 people in mid for a single tower. Yes, 30% of the time, their risk pays off
and your own team run right into them. But 70% of the time, their gamble is really
stupid and inefficient. That 70% chance that they don't score kills
is really really bad for the enemy team. Let's keep watching to see what Chovy does
to get an idea why. After the fight, he manages to pick up a Sion
kill, and if you look on the minimap it's easy to tell that the top wave is slow pushing
away from his team. That's bad, so he goes to fix it, since he
knows where the rest of the enemy team is so his health pool isn't a risk at the moment. Very briefly, if you're not aware, what we
mean by fixing waves is simply pushing it into your opponent's tower. That usually creates a bounce back slow push
towards your side of the map, which earns more overall farm for you in the long run. His commitment to fixing waves over the randomness
of solo queue is why he's so consistent at farming. Look at what he's set up. Both the bot lane and top lane waves are pushing
into his team. That's less farm for the enemy team, and more
for him and his teammates to pick. That's why it's not worth the risk for the
enemy team. In 70% of cases, they're falling drastically
behind for a gamble that isn't worth taking, and Chovy exploits this to the max. Let's be real. This guide won't get you remotely close to
being at Chovy's level. He's maintaining 10 cs per minute, and scoring
multiple kills against the best players in the world consistently. That being said, he does this by refining
these simple fundamentals to the max. Practice makes perfect, and if you ever want
to be as good as that, then this guide is a good place to start. And remember, if you want to improve fast
in general and get the rank you've always wanted then check out skill-capped.com, Link
in the description below. We here at skill-capped want to thank you
for watching and we'll catch you in the next one. Peace

titre: A comprehensive guide to crush mid game in League of Legends
transcription: The midgame is where dreams are lost. What once looked like promise of a clean game quickly goes down the drain, and it's probably Riot Freak's fault. We've all done too much trolling in the midgame, so if you want to step it up and make it a strong part of your play, this is the video for you. Part of why the mid game is hard is because League of Legends has changed a lot over the years, the main change being the bounty system. Back in the good old days, you could have a big lead out of lane, and it was often worth the risk to play recklessly as you'd usually win by stat checking. Even if you didn't, you'd just go from 5 kills ahead to 4. Dying now gives a lot of bonus gold and experience, and the game can become extremely difficult from dying even a single time. This is how it works now, and despite this reality, a lot of us are still running under that conditioning of "I'm fed, so let's fight all the time." I'm going over this first because staying alive is the most important foundation of the mid game, and all the concepts in the world will not matter if you have a bad death. What is the mid game? The mid game starts when plates fall. Plates are extremely valuable, making them a powerful incentive to stay in lane the first 14 minutes. Once they fall, the priorities shift on the map, and the lane assignments change. Bot laners go mid, and mid laners start playing side lanes. There are a couple key reasons for this. First, AD carries are really good at pressuring towers, and the tier 1 and tier 2 mid turrets are the most important defensive structures in the game. They help protect the jungle camps. Jungleers watching this know how helpless it feels to have your mid towers down, get permanently invaded, and lose all your camps. Mid towers are also really important defensively as mid is the most helpful lane to have waves pushed out in. It's in close proximity to every objective, and it's by far the shortest path to the nexus, so most games are ended through mid lane. Losing the towers means the opponent can shove out further, and get longer windows to roam and pressure objectives without bleeding farm. Additionally, the AD carry and support are usually going to be stronger in skirmishes than a mid laner. It's logical to have a strong duo central on the map to be available for objectives, while still allowing the farm hungry AD carry to consistently get minions. If mid towers are so important, and bot lane does well going mid lane, you may wonder why bot doesn't start there. It's because Riot said so. We've all run into scenarios where someone won't budge out of a lane they have no business being in. A lot of players want to keep playing in the lane they started in, so if this happens, Coach Cupcake's ace mentality will be really helpful. Acknowledge the best play, communicate it, and embrace reality. Try to communicate the lane assignment you want. Even be willing to give a wave for it, but if your teammate won't budge, you may just have to wait for a better window. Alternatively, you can assert dominance and come to the lane anyways. Just be sure you shove your previous lane out first, so that your tower isn't taken during the rotation. Usually people will move if you ask, and if they don't, they will often move once you show up and they see that juicy wave building up in the lane you left. Regardless of your lane assignments, League of Legends players want one thing. To push. This is because pushing is good. It gets us farm and tempo to do things without missing that farm, like roam, reset, or hit towers if no one's around. And don't get me wrong, you should push. A lot of people run around like headless chickens in the mid game, not getting any kind of farm or tempo before running to plays. In an ideal world, everyone on your team could push waves out all the time and just permanently have the first move around the map. Unfortunately the game is not this simple. Because pushing is the good default thing to do in a vacuum, this leads to every player, no matter what rank, overextending and getting caught. And then that thing I talked about earlier happens, where you throw the game disaster strikes, you crash out, and start thinking about other things you should be doing with your life. How do you push push push without trolling the game? What you need to do is follow the taped framework. Threats accounted for, pressure elsewhere. Map awareness for both teams is really crucial for playing the mid game out correctly. Murphy's Law stating that "anything that can go wrong will go wrong" is true as fudge for real on God in League of Legends, especially in the mid game. When you push out a lane, you're putting a target on your head. Players are hungry for some dopamine, so if they are nearby, they will come and kill you. This is why map awareness is so important. If you have every threat to you accounted for on the map, then you're of course safe to push. This is simple in concept, but it is actually hard to always mentally keep track of the enemy team, so executing on this takes a lot of intentionality and practice. The issue with pushing only with threats being accounted for is as soon as those enemies disappear on the map, you know they're coming for you, and have to back off. This is why having pressure elsewhere goes hand in hand with tracking threats. It serves as a security blanket. The opponents have multiple people to deal with, so they can't afford to allocate too many players to one lane, as they will be trading off other things on the map. For instance, I play a lot of Dr. Mundo, who really likes push push pushing. If I split while everyone else on my team is on the map pressuring, then even if I get three-manned, and they spend a lot of time chasing me, my team is in the position to capitalize elsewhere. But remember, dying is still really bad unless your team is getting a Baron, Atakhan, or third or fourth dragon for it. If you are leveraging your life for one of these key objectives, you need to communicate that clearly with your team and be darn sure that they are both in position and have the time to do it. Even then, still try to bait, waste time, and survive if you can, as it will accomplish the same thing and you don't die. Trading your life for anything less than Trading your life for anything less than a highly impactful objective is never worth it. For instance, a lot of top laners will die for a tier 2 side tower and think it's worth as they still get 700 gold for their efforts. It's not that simple. That's a tower you can eventually get without dying, and you're dead for a long time. You give a bunch of gold and experience, You give a bunch of gold and experience, lose time you could be farming up and getting resources, but most importantly, your team now has to play the map 4v5 for 30 seconds or so. In this time, they may compensate and lose a fight 4v5, lose an lose a fight 4v5, lose an lose a fight 4v5, lose an objective, or often both. When this happens, the whole game can be thrown within the span of a minute. That's on you. Getting caught before an important objective is trolling the game and you deserve to lose if you do this. Something to keep in mind is that League of Legends is an information based game. It is extremely important to play for vision, as everything is easier to do with more information. You have better chances of baiting opponents, surviving, and stalling. Additionally, it allows your team to capitalize much faster as they can see the opponents going for you ahead of time, giving more going for you ahead of time, giving more time to start taking the objective with a lower risk of getting contested. If you die really quickly, then the enemy If you die really quickly, then the enemy team will be able to contest afterwards, making the game a coin flip and a ship show. If you find yourself wanting to push but you don't know where anyone is, this is the perfect time to dip out of vision and poke your head in the jungle to try and get some wards down. This makes it so the enemy doesn't know where you are and gives you more information to play with so that you can push safely. Now that you are safely shoving your Now that you are safely shoving your waves out, how do you use that tempo? It's going to largely depend on both your champion's identity and your win conditions. Champion identity in the mid game is all about how does your character want to destroy the enemy nexus. They typically will either prefer to hit towers or team fight, and a lot of characters can do both. If you're not sure what your champion wants to do, look into it. Now before I even attempt to explain win conditions, here's an important disclaimer. The majority of the time people lose the mid game, it has nothing to do with the assessment of win conditions and everything to do with forcing fights that make no fluffin sense. The amount of times people are fed, feeling good, and decide to just send it even though they're hard outnumbered is crazy. I am by no means above this myself, there's just something about being fed that makes resisting bad engagements really hard. If you take anything from this video, it If you take anything from this video, it If you take anything from this video, it should be to follow the T.A.P.E framework so you never get picked for free and to avoid brainlessly fighting under bad circumstances. There should always be an obvious reason to fight, like numbers advantage, enemies wasting important cooldowns, being mispositioned, etc. Along that note, it's also best to avoid coin flipping 5v5s when there is no objective to get off them. There's always a chance someone misplays it, so it's more reliable to farm up during downtime and play to capitalize on mistakes rather than engaging and hoping it works out. Now that we have that out of the way, win conditions are still really good to play for. To figure out what they are, you need to assess the game in its current state and see both what objectives are important and what fed team members you should be playing around. It's hard to clearly define how to know your win conditions every game, but I'll try to give you some questions to be asking yourself to better assess a game's state. Is your team stronger 5v5? If the answer is yes, that is a huge indicator to play for dragon, Atakhan, or baron. You still want to shove waves out before the objective, and then use that tempo to set up the area with vision, get your team in position, and set up a kill bush. Pink words can be really helpful for Pink wards can be really helpful for forcing enemies to face check objectives and bushes. Always play for the fight first. This is extremely important for quite a few reasons. First, hitting the objective and getting it low allows the possibility of it being stolen. If you win the fight anyways, it's entirely pointless to provide them an opportunity to coin flip stealing. Second, starting the objective makes it harder for your team to be coordinated. Some members will keep hitting the objective, and others will play for the fight, which often throws both. Third, some objectives like Baron and Atakhan shred resistances and all deal damage, hurting your chances for no reason. Fourth, hitting objectives entices everyone to use all their cooldowns to damage it, meaning when the fight actually breaks out, there's a high chance the enemy team will have more abilities available, which makes a massive difference. Lastly, your team will be positioned poorly when hitting an objective. Being bunched up and cornered in a pit is not really ideal for anyone trying to play out a fight. As you can see, it is much better to use the objective to bait the weaker enemy team into fighting you. To do this, clear all enemy vision around it so they are forced to run into your team to contest. Do you have a fed split pusher that the enemy team has trouble dealing with? If so, we may not want to force fights and instead enable their pressure. Split pushers require the enemy team members to come deal with them, or they will open the base making the game volatile and dangerous for your opponents. They will create numbers advantage for your team to then get the objectives. Supports may want to help set up vision so they have more information to play around. The general way to play around split pushers is to match your pressure with how extended they are on the map. For instance, if they are way pushed out to hit a tier 2 tower, we should also be pushed and ready to hit a tower or an objective. We have to be ready for the enemy team to either go for the team fight or go for the split pusher. If they go for the split pusher, great, it's time to secure the objective and play with your numbers advantage. If they go for you, your goal is to bait their engage and get away. Waste time, and your split pusher will now be the one who capitalizes. If your split pusher has teleport, you can still play for the fight if it looks good, as if you're baiting their engage well, the enemy will often force and be in trouble once your fed member arrives. Is your team behind? If you are, then your goal is to stall the game and look for picks. A lot of people fall into the trap of accelerating the game when behind, because everyone is tilted and just hopes fighting the dragon works out. fighting the dragon works out. This actually ruins any chances of winning, so unless it's soul point or the enemy team trolls and gives you a numbers advantage, you should concede the objectives. Remember that people throw mid games all the time, so be patient, farm up, if you have any members who scale well, try to enable them to do so, and keep looking for mistakes. People are going to get cocky and mispositioned at some point, so be ready to get any picks you can, especially on people with shutdowns. Successfully getting a pick helps maintain the stall, as even a fed team usually won't want to fight with a numbers disadvantage, and if they do, disadvantage, and if they do, you have much better chances of getting some key shutdowns. If the game is even, you also want to play for picks, but stalling the game isn't as important unless you have a hard scaling champion that needs more time to hit their spikes. Extra tips for a smooth mid game. The importance of adaptability. Regardless of any mid game knowledge you have, League is a highly chaotic game. People on both teams are going to misposition, waste cooldowns, and just generally find ways to troll. One of the hardest and most important skills in League is being able to adapt to new circumstances quickly. Say you're mid team fight and you notice that both your fed members got caught. Even if your team is ahead, this team fight may not work out, so you need to be willing to adapt accordingly and play to live. This can go either way. You can also be avoiding a fight until you notice the fed enemy Yasuo decided to dive the fountain, and you now think you can win the fight. It's easy to get locked into a certain way of thinking. All I can say is try to remember that when circumstances change, try to update your response. Communication is key. It's really common for us to know how we should play out the mid game, and then our team has something different in mind. It's your job to communicate and make it easy for everyone to be on the same page. If you see your team about to overextend and force a 3v5, back ping them a bunch of times and ping where the missing members are so they can see why they shouldn't fight. If you want a split push but your team is pressuring while you're in the base, ping them off and ask them to wait for you to get onto the map. If you want to fight the next dragon as you think it's the win condition, tell them that condition, tell them that and try to caution ping anyone who might get caught ahead of time. You get the point. Pings are a really important part of the mid game, and most of the time your team wants to win. If you're giving good pings and have rationale behind your communication, people will often listen and agree. A lot of players know not to take bad fights and die before objectives, but get distracted. Pinging can make all the difference. Don't take inhibitors before baron. You only want to take inhibs once you have baron, because super minions plus baron buff allow you to siege the base and win the game smoothly. A lot of players think taking inhibs is always a good thing, but even with super minions ending the game isn't very easy unless you have a baron or elder dragon. So what happens is now you have made one of your lanes permanently push, preventing your team from safely farming that lane while simultaneously giving free and more valuable farm to the opponents. If you're in a later game scenario where you've won a team fight and may be able to end, just do the baron. end, just do the baron. end, just do the baron. People always want to go for the end after a won fight, which often results in taking inhibitors, the enemy stalling you out, your team overextending and dying, and now the opponent's getting the baron and winning the game. So unless you're 1000% sure you can end before the enemy respawns, it's always more consistent and reliable to play for baron, reset, and reliable to play for baron, reset, and then play to siege after. Lane phase sets up the mid game. The last thing I'll leave you with is that having a smooth lane phase sets you up to have agency and impact over the mid game. You can have all the knowledge in the world, but if you come into mid game behind and permanently losing priority, the game is obviously going to be really hard to play out. So if this does happen to you, even though it can feel like the game was lost in the mid game, it's always going to be more mid game, it's always going to be more valuable to solve the early game issues before working too much on mid game strategy. Thank you so much for watching the video. If you liked it, be sure to like and subscribe so I can keep doing this. If you have any feedback, I'd love to see it in the comments.

titre: How to Teamfight in League of Legends: ULTIMATE Team Fighting Guide!
transcription: welcome to our tenth of this year's collaborations with riot games for latin america if you want to catch these guides in spanish then be sure to check out their channel here when it comes to team fighting one of the biggest problems players face is how one dimensionally they approach them almost everyone just blindly fully commits presses all their buttons and just prays that things work out of course it's hard to blame people for doing this team fighting is insanely difficult so it's hard to see any other way to go about it thing is it doesn't have to be that way there are actually a ton of easy to implement tips that can immediately make you a much better team fighter with one of the first being a complete mentality shift around them let's try and compare how players approach team fighting to trading during the laning phase do you mindlessly just run at your enemy and hope that whatever you do works no of course not you generally try to think things through and take it a bit more slowly how many minions are there how do our champions interact with one another which cooldowns do they have available etc now you may not know exactly how things will play out every trade but if you have the correct approach you'll know that more often than not you'll come out ahead team fighting is essentially the same it's impossible to know how each fight is going to play out but with three fundamentals that we'll teach today you should be able to lead your team to victories far more often the first and most important concept to learn is simply learning how to create and play around space that you or your teammates create space is the term that players have come up with to describe when you're more able to freely damage your enemies the simplest example of this that everyone can understand would be having a tank in front of you as a carry being in front the tank provides space by absorbing incoming damage thus you the carry are able to deal damage safely it's not just tanks that can do this though anyone can provide space for their teammates and this is where a lot of the nuance from team fighting can come from the reason we brought up trading before is that you can think of team fighting in a different way it may sound incredibly obvious but your goal in a fight shouldn't be to just run at your opponent and hope that you kill them this is what most players do and why team fights end up being so chaotic you should make an active effort to both maximize the damage that your team is dealing while also minimizing how much damage your overall team is taking and this is where the concept of space comes in let's draw up an example to clarify imagine two teams fighting in the mid lane on the red team aesion charges in to engage while on the blue team the akali moves forward to fight she sets up her shroud while her teammates gang up on siam here's an interesting question even if you don't play akali what do you think she should do in this situation what scion is doing in this fight is atrociously bad because he's giving akali the option to do something very simple the answer we were looking for is literally do nothing but sit in shroud think about it as a tank scion doesn't do much outside of soaking damage and see scene but he's on his own so he's taking damage for free he's accomplishing absolutely nothing but why does this mean that akali should do nothing you may wonder shouldn't she try to assassinate a squishy target well she simply doesn't need to if she sits in her shroud in front of the enemy team they can't walk past her you don't want to flank yourself and walk past an assassin with four squishy members it's just not great which means that the four enemy members literally can't do anything and that's the reason why akali should not try to kill anyone if your opponent is taking free damage while their teammates can't do anything then why should you as a colleague give them the option of hitting you yes akali could potentially kill someone but there's no reason to her team already has a massive advantage by being able to hit scion right now while the enemy team is unable to hit anyone thus the assassin is in a way providing more space to their teammates than the actual tank is simply by the way they played the fight without a doubt this is by far one of the biggest issues that players have in regards to team fighting you don't need to be optimal in how you deal damage if you just keep this concept in mind you'll be a better team fighter than most players let's take a look at a game from masters elo where players are bound to be good at team fighting right the fight initiates with echo going over a wall to deal damage to jarvan this is exactly what the scion did in our previous example echo is giving the entire enemy team a free target to hit while not providing his team anything at all that's because all of the blue side team is too far to make use of ekko's distraction the fight is over as soon as it's begun but jarvan has greed in his eyes he sees the enemy team clump up gets a massive ultimate off and dies instantly hopefully you can see the comparison between this clip and the previous example that we went over and why we recommended that akali do nothing in the first example jarvan and his team got a complete freebie from the enemy echo diving face first into the middle of all of them the blue team could do absolutely nothing about the fact that their teammate did that jarvan unfortunately suffers from the tunnel vision of optimal cc and damage that is such a huge culprit in players throwing fights just because you can get off big crowd control doesn't mean anything if your teammates can't do anything off of it it's doubly worse if you're handing your opponent someone to hit for no reason jarvan could have just stood by his team and killed echo for free and the blue team would have been stuck doing nothing you always want to be asking yourself what can my teammates do off of the play that i'm making but at the same time what can my opponents do off of it as well you'll find that it becomes much clearer what you should be doing in fights this way we quickly want to address that you may be led to believe that this concept only applies to front-line champions and only they can provide space but no the ability to set up your teammates and deny plays from your opponents is applicable to all roles as an example while vygar is great for deleting squishy targets one of the main strengths is his ability to create space for his teammates to easily deal damage while zoning opponents with his cage try to keep in mind a visual representation such as this oftentimes optimal damage or cc comes at the cost of your team taking a lot of damage themselves it's better to do a bit less if it means that your opponents don't get to hit you freely once you start thinking about team fights this way you'll improve dramatically and win them way more consistently our second major fundamental is going to be about target selection another consistent thing that players get wrong when it comes to fighting in solo queue one of the most prominent things that you're ever going to hear is focus their carry guys come on without a doubt this is one of the biggest debates that makes players play out fights incredibly poorly so why is that well for this topic let's actually shift gears and try to envision the game in a simpler way let's break down league of legends champions into medieval units such as swordsmen and archers imagine that you're approaching your opponents in front they have a bunch of swordsmen guarding their archers in the back if you're a swordsman on the other side do you just ignore the enemy front line and run straight toward their archers of course not you just get sliced down the moment you tried to do that and yet this is one of the most recurring problems we see all the time from a bunch of players people go absolutely mental trying to reach an enemy carry it's not like your opponents are just going to let you do that for free all the time you need to wait for proper moments where you've either found a good opportunity or when they misposition so poorly that you can easily get on top of them to score a kill or even better say that you had some nice cavalry waiting in the sidelines aka an assassin ready to flank the enemy archers once the fight has begun lastly there's another consideration here for you to take in carries are only as good as the team that is supporting them one of the main reasons that players are so tunnel visioned on killing carries is because they're the most threatening enemy members right but you should try to think of enemy carries as a trebuchet think about a nice beautiful trebuchet tucked away safely behind some castle walls as you try to siege it's raining massive boulders at you making life miserable now consider that very same trebuchet but for some inexplicable reason placed outside of the castle walls not so threatening now is it it's completely exposed so it's going to get overrun instantly that concept translates perfectly over to league of legends while you would ideally like to kill the enemy carries from the start it's often just as good to kill the allies protecting them as it is to kill them directly a carry without peel and support is just an exposed trebuchet it's unlikely that they'll be able to do any further harm to you and will probably just retreat to safety to wait for their allies to respawn the final thing that you need to understand about fighting is how each type of team composition beats each other in rock paper scissors fashion generally we'd say that there are three types of champions when it comes to team fighting engage disengage and poke engaged champions are obviously characters that have powerful all-in capabilities such as camille ashe alistair etc disengaged champions are ones with great defensive or zone control types of abilities brahm while he can engage is one of the premier disengaged champions in the game he works best when the opponent is running into him and he can counter their play then there are less obvious ones such as anivia or victor with powerful zone control abilities making it incredibly difficult for the enemy team to engage without their lives being miserable or perhaps a champion like kindred with her ultimate she can turn the tides of an engage very quickly with an ability that literally prevents death hopefully you get the gist of what we're getting at any champion that generally dissuades their opponent from fully committing to a fight falls under the disengaged category lastly there are poke based champions who want to harass you from incredibly far away this is the most obvious of the bunch as we're sure you know that this includes stuff like ezreal zoe jace etc as we said these categories counter each other for the most part engage counters poke since champions with good all-in abilities will not give long-range champions the time to get poke damage off before a fight since engage is a poke comp's biggest weakness it should go without saying that disengaged comps are really punished by poke based champions without the tools to engage they will just get poked down before a fight begins every single time that weakness is made up by the fact that disengaged comps will thrive against engage comps since their entire purpose is to foil their opponent's engages as you can see each type of composition has its strengths and weaknesses which is what makes league such a compelling game okay so what's the point in covering this it's good to know but it's not realistic to solo cute right well the odds of having team comps filled with only one category of champion is certainly extremely low more often than not you'll get a mix of champions wanting to do different things that's essentially the point that we want to get at you shouldn't hope your teammates play fights appropriately it's on you to try the best to adapt and to work with them as well as you possibly can here's an example of a player two tunnel visioned on what their own champion wants to do that they don't care to think about what their teammates can accomplish this diana has a xerath and jace on her team meanwhile the enemy has almost zero reliable engage champions although diana is a powerful all-in champ she could give her poke teammates a bit of time to get some damage off and play to their strengths before fully committing to a fight as her xerath uses his ultimate to poke the enemy off the inhib she instead takes it as a sign to fully commit and die completely for free if she had just understood the rock paper scissors of team comps and adapted to what both teams could do she'd realize that the enemy team had almost zero answer to pre-fight pope she can easily hold off on engaging until the fight looks better from xerath's poke instead she gives away her life for free initiated a poor fight and instead of this being a game-winning push the game is just delayed further for no reason let's compare that to one of the best team fight adaptations we've ever seen from a professional player on an incredibly linear champion malphite the big rock is one of the premier engage champions in the game his ultimate is absolutely insane for getting on top of the enemy team and locking them down but cloud9's fudge made a great read on this teamfight is there something that you can notice about some of the enemy team's champions so all of them other than orianna are short-ranged engaged champions that like to fully commit right and what are engaged champions weak to disengage so instead of using his ultimate and forcing his teammates to go into his opponents fudge realizes that he can just hold it to peel instead for when someone on the enemy team inevitably commits and voila an easy one team fight adapting to every single game and coming up with the best team fighting strategy isn't easy but you should at least make a conscious effort to think about both what your own team wants to accomplish and what the enemy team wants to do as well given enough practice doing so you'll start seeing different strategies going into team fights and well the results will speak for themselves team fighting is without a doubt one of if not the hardest skills to truly master in this game we're not expecting you to become a team fighting master overnight but if you follow these three fundamentals from now on you'll definitely perform better in critical fights compared to most players who don't think about anything at all before engaging with their team and if you want to keep learning to do similar things to improve fast and get the rank that you've always wanted then check out skillcap.com link in the description below best of luck in your games thanks for watching and we'll see you next time

transcription: why play velos mid well he has insane range he brings tons of burst damage he is one of the best poke champions in the game and he kills both tanks and squishies alike however he is squishy and lacks any Mobility at all and he is purely skillshot Reliance so he can struggle against High Mobility velos is passive is his ability apply a stack of deconstruction to enemies hit for 7 Seconds Auto attacks and abilities will refresh the duration every third stack consumes all the stacks to deal a large amount of bonus true damage voses Q is a straight line skill shot which deals damage to and slows the first enemy hit this ability can be recast to stop early or upon maximum duration it then splits into two right angles with each of these abilities applying the same effect also whenever this kills an enemy velos regains a small amount of Mana velos is W is a rectangular StraightLine skill shot which after a short delay deals damage to enemy's hit after 75 seconds it then explodes again for even more damage and this ability works on an ammo system where velos can have up to two at one time velos is e a circular skill shot which deals damage to knocks up and stuns enemies hit this ability goes off faster the closer the ability is to velos and if the ability is used close to velos it also knocks the enemy back slightly vos's ultimate has a passive where after applying your three stacks of deconstruction to an enemy Champion it marks them as researched the active is a channeled ability which Roots Velar in place and fires out a StraightLine skill shot which deals damage to and slows all enemies hit in addition it keeps applying deconstruction to enemy hit and if the enemy has been researched and that they've already had this passive applied to them at least once they instead take true damage from this ability for combos your go-to is to start with E into W and Q following up with an auto attack into your second W and you're all in combos to Simply use e W and Q followed up with your ultimate for tons of damage and in occasionally helpful max range combos to throw your Q at an angle and just before it hits use ultimate to hit them from miles away for matchups velos does great into shorter range Mages so he can simply sit out of their range and Bully them all game and he can struggle against assassins who can easily jump on him and blow him up for runes take this Comet combined your Q slow makes you hit like a brick for build orders start ringing pots into LeAndre and Tier Two Boots after this a horizon focus and hat a great pickups before finishing a build with any of these as needed for skill orders start Q than W then E before maxing Q than W then e taking ult whenever you can for Summoner spells take flashing barrier teleport also works well if you're noted against assassins starting the game your main focuses are going to be on farming hard poking hard and looking for your explosive combos when farming you always want to be looking for opportunities to hit your opponent with w and pushing the wave as well as being mindful of being able to throw your que out at any angles to hit your opponent behind their minions this puts them in a tough position where they can stand in their minion wave and get hit by the W or stand behind their minions and risk getting hit by your Q after landing Q is a great opportunity to throw your e while they're slowed and if you're able to land this and throw out a w you have out massive damage with your passive and of course when you hit Level six you now have insane amounts of damage especially if you can land your e into a full combo and during the mid game your Bot Lane will usually want to come mid which puts your golden experience in a side lane push the wave out as far as you can safely before grouping up with your team for fights and objectives entering late game team fights you want to play the backline Mage between your LeAndre and your passive you deal tons of damage to both tanks and squishies alikee and Landing a queq on a back line will simply half Health them spam out all your tons of damage until you can land a full combo on someone to remove them from titre: 3 Minute Ezreal Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: why play Ezreal bot well he has brilliant poke he is a very safe laner his kit has very long range and he scales well into the mid and late game however he does have a weaker early game compared to other bot laners and he's not the best carry at bringing down tanks ezreal's passive is when he hits an enemy with an ability he gets granted bonus attack speed stacking up to five times ezreal's Q is his bread and butter it is a StraightLine skill shot which deals damage to the first enemy hit and if it hits an enemy it lowers the cool down of all of his abilities including this one by one and a half seconds ezreal's W is a straight line skill shot which marks the first enemy Champion monster or structure hit ezreal's next basic attack or ability against the target detonates this Mark to deal bonus damage and if the Mark was detonated by an ability ezriel gets a chunk of Mana back plus the Mana cost of that ability ezreal's e causes him to Blink in a direction he then fires a projectile at the nearest enemy which deals damage and reveals them this will always prioritize targets marked with his W and can also Target enemies he can't see so it's a really helpful way if an enemy's hid to in a bush and you don't have WS to reveal them e Real's ultimate is a global ability which fires out a straight line skill shot this will travel across the entire map and Deals damage to All Enemies hit for combos your basic trade is to attack into W and Q before using e as needed to chase them down and you roll in is to use ultimate into your w q and E before attacking away and if
