titre: How to PLAY FROM BEHIND as Jungle - League of Legends Guide
transcription: we should never plan to fall behind however it's inevitable that it's going to happen and you need to know what to do when it does in today's video we're going to be exploring strategies that you can use to come back in your games and showing how to salvage those games where your lanes or you are going 0-3 by 5 minutes it's not as doomed as it looks most of the time you just need to shift your perspective to play from behind without further ado let's get right into it fundamentally the game is different from behind no longer are you the aggressor able to brute force your way into the enemy jungle and take any 1v1 you see you have to be a little more calculating reactive and play around information we've selected the most important core concepts and takeaways that you should remember when you happen to mess up early the first of which is opposite side play essentially this just means avoiding the enemy jungler or the person who happens to be troublesome on the enemy team in this game i'm playing kindred and get caught in a 2v2 where i die obviously this is a mistake and i shouldn't have gone for the crab knowing that nocturne could be sitting in the bush however the game isn't doomed and now i'm going to look to take advantage of the fact that i know where he is now on the first clear i know that nocturne passed up went to crab and then came to the bottom crab this means that his second rotation of camps will respawn in the same order making him unlikely to be topside at this moment i decided to ignore camps right now and rush to top to make a play while the information that i have on him is still valid if i were to wait it gets harder and harder to figure out exactly what he's doing and i'll have to wait to see him on map again for more information notice the careful wards i place on the way in order to verify this information when i see that gromp is still down and he's not showing on these wards it makes me feel confident in my initial assumption we successfully dive top lane and with the new numbers advantage we're able to force another play i instantly go from getting double crabbed and killed to being ahead all through one single idea when you die it's obviously not great but the first question you should ask yourself is what advantage do you have that you can capitalize on the first is usually information and the other is usually tempo early on death timers are extremely short so even though the enemy may get gold for killing you it really doesn't do anything unless they base and for the most part you'll be able to go buy and be on map quicker since your death timer is shorter or about the same as a recall and it's unlikely that the enemy will immediately base to counteract your play this is a very short window that you can look to abuse whenever you fall behind just like i did in this game before we get on to our next example we just want to make sure you know where our guides come from check out our hyper improvement system at skillcap.com we have professional courses by the top players smurf commentaries where a challenger player walks you through how to climb out of every rank from iron to diamond and we upload tons of new exclusive guides to our website each week in fact we're so confident you'll improve using our system that if you don't climb at least five divisions while actively using skill caps you can claim a full refund so there's no risk what are you waiting for check out skillcap.com and get the rank you've always wanted link in the description below let's take a look at a game from pro jungler armal to see how he applies this same concept except for when his lanes are losing the enemy mid laner picks up two kills at level 1 and then another kill very early on into the game it's not expected at this point for rumble to have prio in his lanes so he's just going to avoid zoe as well as the enemy jungler like the plague instead of trying to contest the crab he runs all the way across the map to the other side and then looks to play onto bot lane initially he'd probably hope to contest top crab because jace would likely have prio but changing your game plan is critical when things don't go as expected from this point onwards he is never going to play around mid or even bother contesting a dragon that his team knows about zoe continues to pick up kills throughout the game but notice how he never goes around mid at all during the section he got his bot lane ahead earlier and he's just going to hug the lane that's strong and make sure they continue to be strong there's two main takeaways that we should get from this game 1. play around the lane that is winning it doesn't matter if your mid lane is 0-3 if your bot lane is winning just as hard trying to salvage a losing lane is one of the worst ways to play the game if you try to help them out it's likely you'll just get double killed and make the situation even worse additionally you stifle your own proactivity it's much easier to make aggressive plays when you're just not around the people on the enemy team who are strong or when you play around teammates that are ahead of the curve two learn to give up objectives you don't have to contest everything objectives exist for the enemy team to bait you into a fight that they win sure you might lose a dragon but it's better to lose the dragon and farm up than to lose the dragon your life and the ability to farm pro players are good but they aren't perfect and arma actually makes the exact mistake that he managed to avoid earlier thresh and armao try to rush a play onto the enemy viego to contest the rift herald but he barely manages to slip away and the monstrously strong zoe wanders over and the situation just gets worse this is a perfect example of how punishing it can really be when you don't avoid the strong members of the enemy team and it's easy to say in hindsight that he should have not gone for the contest at all a very good idea you can use is to just look to trade objectives obviously this last clip dragon was down but let's imagine a game where both objectives are up a key concept you need to realize are the goals of both teams let's say that the enemy team is ahead by 2000 gold early on into the game their job is to take that gold lead and snowball building upon it to expand the lead to 3000 4000 and so on until the lead is substantial enough to end the game the team that's behind wants to stabilize they don't need to actually win any fights early on they just need to stifle the enemy's ability to snowball theirs in a game where one team has eight thousand total gold and the enemy has ten thousand we can just say that one team has eighty percent of the other's gold if that gold lead doesn't snowball the percentage will continue to get closer and closer until you reach six items and it just doesn't matter anymore if the enemy team wants to take dragon avoid them give it up and trade it for rift herald you may not be able to use it proactively but that's besides the point you'll stop the enemy from snowballing as hard as you continue to turtle and close the gold lead it may stay at 2000 the entire time but percentage-wise you're gaining as the game drags out this puts all the pressure to win on the team that's currently ahead and from behind just going even means that you're winning for the inverse if the enemy team is taking rift taking dragon can delay the enemy's dragon soul by 5 minutes you may not get much use out of the dragon yourself but both of these plays are just to slow the game down and give you more time to neutralize the advantage resources on the map are limited there are only so many minions camps and objectives so denying things from the enemy is the main way to get a lead or stop the enemy from extending theirs let's just take a look at a platinum replay where our jungler's laner dies pretty early on vaya is busy farming wolves while nocturne kills nico while she does manage to get a return kill nico is obviously going to be behind from this interaction so we might run into some issues later down the line if mid lane gets out of control and obviously we would prefer if nico had just lived here however an incredibly useful tip for when your laners die is to just take their resources like we said earlier minions are limited there's a finite number of them in the game at any given time meaning that there's a specific amount of total gold available from having minions to farm if nico isn't able to get them you can at least take them for yourselves so your team doesn't start to lose out on overall gold you're just shifting resources from being more evenly spread to one person instead vai should have taken midway here to get gold for herself as well as an extremely large amount of experience you can really snowball a game with a level advantage from jungle as if laners get ahead they pretty much make one person's life miserable but a jungler can impact the entire map instead she takes the bottom crab that would have been there anyways for her to get if she had pushed in the wave and then gone to crab this is an extremely common mistake where junglers prioritize their own camps as well as crabs instead of getting the resources that are more finite camps remain until someone clears them waves will die on their own in order of importance waves will just come first before jungle camps 99 of the time this goes for later in the game as well and lower elos especially teams will often just a-ram mid and group up way more than they need to when you're behind you want to avoid fighting so going to a side lanes you literally just freeze away for five minutes even as a jungler is a perfectly viable strategy to catch back up again remember that minions are finite if you're freezing the enemy isn't getting resources from that wave and you're consistently getting two waves every minute when you map that out it's roughly a 240 gold increase every minute considering kills are worth 300 gold that's a pretty significant amount and it puts a lot of pressure on the enemy team to force something of equal value laner should obviously be doing this but more often than not they won't and will leave the resources for whoever wants to go pick it up don't be the player who ignores side lanes and lets 480 gold just disappear from the map every minute speaking of side lanes we have another extremely important concept weak side and strong side are terms that are used to delegate which side of the map has more strength at any given moment common examples would be like three people being top side while the enemy only has two or you're really fed laner 1v1 in the not so fed enemy later in general you need to identify which side is more likely for a play to work out on and play for that side this relates back to not playing around your losing lane but it also goes a bit beyond that since you should be looking at things like numbers advantages as well shuffling team members around the map to get a 4v3 or some advantaged fight can be huge for actually coming back in this platinum example game nocturne and most of his team haven't had the best start but his rise however is 3-1 being the only beacon of hope once he responds from this death you'll notice that he goes bot side towards his lane that's not doing too great and said he easily could have passed towards topside to help rise while he's attempting to push and bully out the enemy vladimir while ryze does end up actually outplaying it's not hard to see that it was extremely close and if noctron was here it was probably a guaranteed double kill morgana made the mistake of playing into her weak side and nocturne simultaneously made the exact same mistake getting nothing on the other side of the map this is in platinum and it's a mistake you'll see even higher up the ladder all the time identifying who you need to play around helps drastically when you're trying to come back from a deficit and oftentimes you'll have at least one person on your team that you can try to play around even in losing games it just may not feel like it sometimes because you've spent your time playing to the wrong lane these are just some core concepts that you can use to help recover from a deficit but remember that every game of league is going to be different and finding the solution that best fits your game is always going to be ideal build upon and improve these ideas to fit the needs of your own team and remember if you want to improve fast and get the rank you've always wanted then check out skillcap.com link in the description below otherwise you know the deal make sure to like subscribe and hit the bell icon to get more premium guides with one goal in mind helping you become a better player we here at skillcaps want to thank you for watching and we'll catch you in the next one

titre: 3 Minute Diana Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: why play Diana jungle well she has high burst damage she has great engage she is incredibly mobile and she can be versatile with her build however she is very Allin with little disengage afterwards and she is reliant on her ultimate for her Full Burst damage team fight combo Diana's passive as she gains bonus attack speed and after using an ability this bonus is tripled for 5 seconds her second passive is every third attack causes her to cleave near by enemies dealing additional damage Diana's Q is a curved skill shot which explodes in a circle at the end enemies hit it doubt damage and marked with moonlight Diana's W grants herself a shield and creates three orbs around her if enemies are hit by these orbs they doubt damage and if all three spheres detonate she gets the shield again Diana's ear is a point and click ability which causes her to dash to an enemy dealing damage to them if the target was marked by the Moonlight from her queue this cool down is basically reset Diana can also use any of her abilities while dashing Diana's ultimate pulls in all enemies in a large circle around her and slows them if this hits at least one enemy Champion she then deals damage in the circle after one second and the more Champions within this circle the more damage this ability deals for combos your go-to is to start with Q into e and w from here you can just keep attacking away using e to chase them down or burst them out as needed and your team fight combo is to use Q into e follow up with an auto attack and your ultimate and W and then spam away with more attacks for matchups Diana is great into junglers who she can easily burst out and she can struggle against pure Duelists who usually bring more sustained damage than she does for runes take this she procs conquer it incredibly quickly for build or start red monster and pot into a nashes in tier 2 boots after this aich Bane and Zas are great pickups before finishing a build with any of these as needed another incredibly viable build for Diana is her engage build if you notice that your team just simply needs more engage your build will look more like this with the core parts of the build being the rocket belt to get onto your opponents and your zous to keep you alive afterwards for skill orders start Q then W then E before maxing Q then W then e taking ult whenever you can for Summoner spells take Flash and smite starting the game Diana clears the jungle quickly and help l so you almost always want to go for a full jungle clear this will maximize your gold and experience and put you in the best possible position to fight for Scuttle crab from here you can simply back and do another full clear and this will make sure you're level six as fast as possible otherwise you can look for a gank when ganking always consider the three main points of what cc does your team bring what escapes does the enemy have and where is the lane pushed to when ganking before level six you don't bring any CC at all but you do bring a ton of damage and great Mobility the most important thing will be landing your queue as this gives you two dashes and of course when you hit Level six your ganks now become devastating with tons more damage and lockdown entering the mid game as the jungler your main focus is going to be on objectives you have insane burst damage so keep up aggressive Wards burst out an overextended enemy and convert into objectives entering late game team fights you can play as your teams engage Landing your que and then going in with a massive ultimate otherwise play the Assassin role wait for the fight to start then Flank In on their backline and blow up a carry and that's how to play Diana jungle if this video helped you leave it a like and comment below who you want to see next next

titre: The Difference Between GOOD and BAD Teamfighting as ADC
transcription: if there is one thing that most low elo adc players don't criticize themselves enough for it is their team fighting as a marksman it's so easy to shrug things off to either hey my team didn't peel for me or the enemy team's champs are too broken adc in current year etc the truth of the matter is though that if most low elo adcs were actually good at team fighting that they would climb instantly it's literally what the role is built for so if you're not succeeding in low elo team fights then you're obviously doing things disastrously wrong in this guide we're going to put you up to the test we'll show you a bunch of examples of team fighting adcs if you can accurately tell whether you're watching a good or bad adc and what they're doing right or wrong then we'll give you a premium skill capped pass to use the previous excuses in your game it doesn't get any better than that so let's jump right into things in this first game we have a very fed kaiser looking to carry the game and the following skirmish that occurs is pretty ideal for that to come true kane tries to pick off kaiser but due to her mobility she easily turns things around and kills him nearly instantly afterwards kaiser yumi and yone look to push into the enemy base watch what happens and see if you can accurately assess whether this is a good or bad adc and what you may or may not have done differently in this situation so you should have very clearly been able to identify that this was a low elo example let's break down why and let us know whether you were able to identify all the things that kai'sa did wrong as you watched for starters although this isn't a macro guide the fact that kaiser went toward mid lane where the inhibitor was already down instead of going to take a free baron should have instantly been a low elo giveaway as for her skirmishing her first mistake was before she even got off her first auto attack while pushing kaiser notices scion coming into his base normally when someone randomly appears behind you you should definitely be hesitant about being flanked by multiple people but for starters kaiser already knew where most of the enemy team was kane was dead meanwhile corki and zyra were visible in the base although twitch was still hiding the main issue here is kaiser failing to read sion's body language as he entered the base look at this massive drift to the left we really want to emphasize how important that is very rarely will players in league of legends mind game with their body language during skirmishes and team fights the fact that cyan did that was a clear giveaway that he was alone and not a threat at all and yet it took kaisa about three seconds to finally realize how vulnerable he was if she wasn't nearly as fed as she was that dps loss could have caused big issues later on right after that when she turns to zyra she makes another big mistake look it's good to know combos and how to use them but players tend to get too eager to be fancy for no reason if you noticed at the moment kaiser flashed zyra wasn't even in an animation to cast any spell it's not like kaiser is trying to dodge her root or anything with the flash not only that but she could have easily caught up with her e if she just waited two seconds the only purpose in this flash was to look cool or something i guess then she's not punished for her mistake at all because for some reason this twitch thinks he's a melee champion correct us if we're wrong but twitch has the highest auto attack range in the game with his ultimate up if he had just played remotely properly kaiser dies instantly because she would have no way to close the distance between them and finally her death to corki she could have maybe won this if she kited up instead of down and back into the package she did have plenty of time to see corki's package angle and react appropriately but there's no way to expect that from a lower elo player so this was an understandable error we really want to emphasize that although you may be hard carrying your games and winning fights that it doesn't mean you're doing everything perfectly if you put in actually good adc into a room and had him go through a million replays of challenger and gold elo adcs they'd be able to accurately assess whether they're good or bad 99 of the time regardless of what their in-game scores are all right let's move on to our next example again see if you can tell whether this is a good or bad adc before getting into what actually happens take a moment to look at the compositions and see how strong everyone is alright so at the start of it all jinx and her team are pushing mid it's at this time that they notice the enemy threatening to do baron as they approach the fight jinx splits off from her team and does a massive loop around the red buff wall eventually she's able to join up with her crew and they manage to kill jax things get dicey for a second there as lee sin almost takes down jynx but overall things went well for the blue team alright so based on what you saw do you think this was a good high elo adc or a bad low elo one alright this one may have been a bit trickier than the last one but it should have been clear that this is a fairly decent player in fact it's t1's gumiyusi you know decent let's break down the simple fundamentals that he followed in this fight we showed you everyone's items because we wanted you to be aware of the lethality lee sin on the enemy team that build is basically the same as if you're playing against a one-shot assassin the problem that he faced in this fight is that he and his team were running blindly into the enemy team's vision when you're walking into fog that is when assassins are generally at their strongest because it allows them to set up onto you with an ideal combo for example leblanc in full vision in a mid-fight has to maybe w then flash rqe to reach a high priority target this isn't her ideal damage rotation and it still has a chance of being interrupted by the enemy team whereas a leblanc hiding in a bush can look for the perfect eq rw combo which maximizes her damage with zero counter play in return this is precisely why gumiyusi took the long way around his team may not have had baron vision but they did have random wards in the enemy jungle trying to fight on your own vision is a simple fundamental when it comes to skirmishing and team fighting yes he is losing out on damage by doing this but he knows lee is near barren and this way he's completely safe for the moment and what we just discussed is precisely what happened as well lee not having clean access to jinx has to perform a mediocre combo instead of maximizing the execute damage from his q he's forced to use it as a gap closer to try and get on top of jinx this results in him barely not having enough damage to finish jinx off and the fight is completely lost for the red team and not playing around vision or where your opponents may potentially be is something we see a lot of low elo adcs fail at constantly here's a nearly platinum level gin failing miserably at this task as he's approaching mid he very clearly sees the enemy fiddle sticks in front of him who goes into the bottom side of the river with that knowledge five seconds later he goes aggressively onto azrael and even begins channeling his ultimate imperfect range to get fiddle ulted from this brush thankfully this gin rightfully gets punished even if it wasn't by fiddle fighting around your own vision and being aware of where your opponents are may not be the most exciting concept when it comes to team fighting but it's definitely one of the first things most top adcs focus on as you just saw from the previous example all right let's move on to another example for this one we'll go ahead and tell you that it's a good adc gen g's ruler let's do something different this time we'll let you watch the team fight first as it's playing we'll to pick up as you watched the fight the first one was a bit tricky but it's a very important team fighting concept whenever you're engaging with the enemy team you should always be asking yourself should i even be here at the moment and could i instead be doing something better what happened here is that ruler and his team had already established vision control in this area of the river the enemy team seemed to be walking away which meant that ruler had an opportunity to instead pressure the incoming wave that way they'd both have control of the river and mid at the same time getting mid prio is something we talk about a lot in our videos it's not so much that getting a single wave into your opponent's tower is insane but it's good in that it gives you more options for what you can do for example if the enemy team is over committing to the dragon and it's being stalled out you could rotate over to mid with the wave and take their inhibitor tower for free that was his intention in the fight but the second the enemy team randomly decided to try and come back this way ruler turned around and joined his team to drive them away from this choke point the second big moment that you should have paid attention to is right afterwards if the enemy team can't go through this choke point then they'll obviously come in through the only other entrance to the river while it is caitlyn specific to trap that other entrance the important point here is understanding your opponent's options keeping track of where from and how the enemy team will approach the fight is pivotal for any adc repositioning based on that information can oftentimes be the deciding factor in whether you win or lose an encounter sadly what happens next is the reality of soloq just like you ruler has to deal with incompetent players due to his traps he and echo wanted to burst the dragon while the enemy team is forced to awkwardly enter the river again he may be on caitlyn but this is the equivalent of abusing a tempo advantage on any other adc to quickly secure an objective unfortunately his leblanc and lux were completely clueless as to what was going on this meant that the enemy team had a much easier time of getting in to contest the dragon there's nothing ruler could have done about that but he did try his best to make up for their mistakes when faced with danger a lot of bad adc players tend to run away in fear but ruler puts on his big boy pants and does what any good adc would instead of leaving echo defend for himself he positions very aggressively to zone the talent all the while he's making sure to try and dodge as many gin alts as he can as an adc he couldn't do much more than force talon to have to use his ultimate to get into the pit but that is definitely more than most other adcs would have forced here which would have been useful if his echo pressed his alt and that's the tragedy that ends up losing the blue team this fight toward the end we see one last ditch effort from ruler to clutch things out pat yourself on the back if you got this one he was trying to play around fog of war to surprise the jinn with the pink ward in the brush and gragas not actually going into it ruler knew that the enemy team had no vision of him here he also spotted jin walking around this way a couple of seconds ago since jyn was running predator which is the meta at the time just how much high level adcs are thinking about in fights compared to lower level players they're making so many subtle movements and choices all the time to increase their chances of winning as much titre: 3 Minute Anivia Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: why play a Nivea mid well she brings strong wave clear she puts out tons of AOE damage she has tons of CC and she scales incredibly well however she is Mana hungry especially early and she is immobile with no additional Mobility ania's passive is once every 4 minutes upon taking fatal damage she becomes an egg while an egg she gains additional armor and Magic resist and after 6 seconds she'll come back alive with her current health however this egg can be attacked by enemies anivia's Q has two parts to it firstly when used it as a straight line skill shot which deals damage to all enemies that passes through and slows them when this ability is recast or upon hitting maximum distance it then explodes dealing damage in a circle stunning all enemies hit so it can deal damage when passing over the enemy and damage again when exploding aia's W places terrain on the floor which knocks away all units from it the more this ability is leveled up the longer the terrain becomes Ania e has a passive where enem is hit by her Q or fully formed ultimate become chilled the active is a point and click ability which deals damage to the enemy hit and if they're chilled this damage is is doubled AIA ultimate places a circle on the floor which expands to its full size enemies within take constant damage and are slowed and when it's at its full size it deals massively more damage and increases the slow while this ability is active it will constantly drain a Nia's mana and if a Nia's hard CCD or goes out of range the ultimate will stop for combos an easy go-to is to use q and make sure it passes over the Target first before detonating it for the second chunk of damage follow up with an auto into e to proc electrocute a more advanced one is to use q and then W behind your target to Bunch them into it before following up with an auto attack into e and your all-in combo is to use ultimate into your q and W following up with your e for matchups anivia is great into Champions who she can easily disengage from and Zone all game and she can struggle against mid-range Mages who will be able to apply heavy pressure for runes take this electrocute makes your short trades brilliant for build orders start ringing pots into Rod of Ages and Tier Two Boots after this and Archangels and LeAndre are great pickups before finishing a build with any of these as needed for skill order start Q then e then take a second point in E before taking W after this Max E then Q then W taking alt whenever you can for summon the Spells take Flash and teleport the start of the game is anivia's weakest point of the match so your main focuses are going to be on farming hard to scale well poking your opponent and taking good trades and if you can ever poke your opponent by using Q over the wave before detonating it on them it can be great value and once you hit Level six you can now push waves incredibly quickly so use your ultimate and Q to push waves in and either get free backs or influence other lanes entering the mid game your Bot Lane will usually come mid which puts your golden experience in a side lane push these side Lanes out as far as you can safely before grouping up with your team for picks fights and objectives entering late game team fights you want to play as the backline Mage you can easily Zone enemies by using your W and your ultimate to stop them going certain places or forcing them to stay certain places other than this use your queue to start anything going for you or to pick off any Target as this easily goes through their front line and detonates on their back line and once to Target is Shield spam away with your ease

titre: Stupidly SIMPLE Tricks To Climb In League
transcription: League of Legends is a complicated game. Hell, I've made a career out of simply teaching it. And it can feel like rocket science just figuring out where to go and what to do. And that's all before you even start fighting the enemy. And boy, once you actually get to a team fight, it does not get any better. Just look at this absolute mess. I've played the game for over 15 years. Even I can't tell what the hell is going on right now. So, instead of giving you yet another complex guide you're just going to forget about in an hour from now, let's cut through all of the chaos. save you the time of having to watch 5,000 different tutorial videos and instead I'll give you my stupidly simple tips you need to begin climbing right away. My name's Rogue and I played League of Legends professionally for seven years now. I'm a full-time content creator and coach for all ranks iron through the challenger. So, if you want more information of what a coaching session with me would look like, you can find more information by following the link in the video description below. And while you're in the description, you'll also find the link to my Patreon, where I post all of the recordings of my best coaching sessions in full. With almost a thousand hours of content currently available, all sorted by role and rank, including sessions where I coach for professional level teams, you can get a glimpse of what prolevel league looks like from the inside. That's why whatever you're looking for, you'll easily be able to find something that's going to help you improve, no matter your rank. So, you're in the mid game and dragon's spawning in just under a minute. So, you recall, buy items, and run out of base in order to get there in time. You managed to arrive in time. In fact, you even get there 20 seconds before it spawns. Meaning you even got there early, right? Great job. Well, too bad. Your jungler walked into the enemy raptor camp, got caught out, and died. And now you have to give the dragon because you can't fight 4v5 without your jungler. Pretty annoying situation that you've probably faced before, right? Well, here's some news for you. You weren't on time after all. You are, in fact, about 20 seconds late, and the fact that your teammate died, while not entirely your fault, is still your fault in your way, or at least somewhat preventable. Recently, I came up with a really simple to understand rule, and everyone I've coached since making this rule has some pretty good success with it. At every point in the game, you want to try and keep track of how long a death timer is. In this game, about a minute or so before all this happened, we see the enemy Hecrim died. And when the Hecrim died, he died for about 35 seconds. Now that we know a death timer is 35 seconds long, we can create a very simple rule out of it. When a neutral objective is within 35 seconds of spawning, kills now matter. Someone who dies after this point will be dead for the spawn of the neutral objective, which gives you a massive advantage going into it. So, simply put, when the dragon is 35 seconds away from spawning, you need to be grouped with your team hunting for kills because any kills you get will now have an impact on the outcome of that neutral objective contest. Furthermore, you need to be grouped with your team because deaths that you and your teammates have now also matter in the context of the neutral objective. Now you can start to see the mistake that our Trindir player made in the intro. He left his teammates alone on the map at a point where kills and deaths now matter. Now that the duration of the death timer is longer than the spawn timer of the next neutral objective. Early on this year, the game underwent some major changes. And since then, from what I've noticed, the pace of the game has sped up and the average kills in a game has ballooned to become a massive amount. In a game that ends with almost 100 kills in total, can you even remember what kill number 23 was? Because most of the games I play, I can't. Most kills are just a meaningless exchange of gold and XP that will likely just be donated back to the other side in later kills anyway. But in a game with 100 plus kills, you absolutely will remember that time that your master died and gifted the enemy team a dragon because in that game, there were probably only five or six dragons taken in total. My point here is that there are a lot of kills in a game of League that just simply don't matter. But there are very small periods in between all of the chaos where a death timer aligns with a neutral objective spawn, at which point kills really do have a purpose in deciding the outcome of a game. I want to put a big extra emphasis on two important objectives in particular, Dragon and Adakan. Dragon and Adakan are objectively broken, especially in solo Q games and especially in lower ELO where games tend to be much longer. A gold lead can be thrown. A Baron buff can be lost when you die. But a dragon or an Adacon Taken is a permanent buff that you and your team cannot lose, and you will absolutely feel the effect of it when you're in those late game situations where the game is truly being decided. The fastest possible dragon soul that you can obtain can be taken at about 22 or 23 minutes in. if you take all of them on spawn. The average game duration in a solo queue game is over half an hour long. Most games these days are decided by a dragon soul. So, prioritize the kills that make you get it. An addakon, well, I made a video recently where I talked about the power of the Attakon buff, and you would not believe how powerful it is. An addakon taken shortly after its spawn grants each member of that team about nine attack damage or 14 ability power on average, depending on what they're building. This means each member of their team gains about 300 gold worth of adaptive force. When spread across your entire team, each member gains on average about half a level. So you're getting about 2 and a half champion levels worth of XP by killing Attakon. So whilst the instant value of taking Adacon shortly after its spawn is worth about 3,000 gold or slightly more plus the buff, the value it has scales with the game time as your team gains more kills and thus more flowers that are amplified by that 25% buff that you now permanently have. So with dragon and adac having such incredible value in the chaos of long high kill games that you commonly get now, it's really important to make sure that you don't get caught up in the chaos when it doesn't matter. instead take care of everything you need to early on so that when the death timers align with the spawn time of one of these neutral objectives, you are already grouped with your team in order to simply hunt enemies in order to try and stagger death timers into the spawn timer of that objective, whilst at the same time babysitting your own teammates so that they don't become victim of the exact same thing. Moving on, here's an easy rule to follow because it's visually very easy to see. At any moment in game, take a look at where your minion waves are in the lanes adjacent to you. Then draw a line diagonally down the map on each of them. This is a very similar idea to the tempo lines concept I came up with about 2 years ago, but this time instead of using champions, we're going to be using minion waves as our reference point. With a few exceptions, you generally don't want to be going deeper into the enemy jungle than what these lines are indicating to you. mainly because you won't be receiving much of a reward anymore for any kills that you find because your waves are so far away. In this instance, I timed it in practice tool and even with swiftness boots and three items, it would take this Darius player a minimum of 40 seconds to walk all the way down and get the wave and push it all the way back up to the enemy tower. In 40 seconds, whatever enemy you manage to kill will literally just respawn and make it back in time to defend anyway, making the kill completely pointless. This is why we end up with an average game duration of over half an hour. If it takes you over 40 seconds just to go get the minion wave, well, in order to get the tower, you need your enemy's death timer to be over 40 seconds long, which is what you get at the half an hour mark into the game, which is why games end at this time specifically. Now, using minion waves as a reference for how deep you can go on the map is super useful since it ensures you actually get a reward for any kills that you find. So, if you ever see your teammates doing dumb things, charging the enemy jungle far beyond how far your waves are pushed, don't go join in. Go to the lane and push the wave forward so that at least the random fighting they're doing, if it results in kills, will actually give you a reward. Now, this is more of a low mistake, as you probably guess, but a more similar and far more common mistake that I often have to correct in my own games in higher ranks is people who let newly arriving enemy minion waves walk past you. Look at our top laner who was smart enough to know that denying enemy jungle camps will help snowball his lead. Great job top laner. That's a good thing to do. The only problem though is that he was so obsessed with taking the enemy camp that he allowed the enemy wave to walk past him, which is something you simply never do. Just look at the difference between these two examples. Here in example one, I'm only 2 seconds late to intercepting the wave. And as a result, I have to run all the way back basically to the river in order to get the wave and then run all the way back up to where I am now, taking about 30 seconds. And in example two, I managed to intercept the wave on time before it walks past me. And just like that, it's cleared in about 2 seconds. The difference between intercepting the wave on time and being late to it is tiny. You cannot let the wave go past you in the parallel. Otherwise, you're going to have a very long run all the way back to go pick it up, potentially leaving your teammates further up on the map compared to where you are, and thus putting them at risk of dying while you're showing on vision all the way back at the river. This one absolutely kills me because even in my Grandmaster games, I have to make it a part-time job to go pick up random mini waves my teammates were just going to let walk past us and go all the way back to the river. That's an easy game. But what I'm going to do, because I want this game to end, is I'm going to start focusing on pushing side waves. Because if my teammates aren't pushing side waves, I'm going to have to. Cuz how do you make the game end? You make waves hit turrets. Not even kidding. That's how to win League of Legends. If a wave hits a turret, good things happen. I don't want to end up with an in a ram with my team just running up mid doing absolutely nothing. All right, just missed the Q. That matters. Good job, Jake. Just missed the one Q that matters. It was a good play, but I missed the one Q that mattered. Oh well. And we get the tower. Good job, boys. Eat their camps while we're here. Good luck with that, Twitch. Do the best you can, my friend. So, to make league simple, just follow these rules. Identify the moments where kills matter. This is simply when death time is aligned with the spawn of neutral objectives. And whenever this happen, it means it's time to group and just hunt for kills. In between these moments, the only way to get a reward as a result of senseless fighting is to just have a wave near you. So, make sure that you use your minion waves as a reference for how deep on the map you can go. making sure not to go too much deeper than however far pushed your adjacent lanes are. As well as making sure not to let newly arrived enemy minion wave walk past you, as this is just simply a shorefire way to either end up deeper in the enemy jungle than what your waves are pushed or to just waste half a minute having to go back and pick up your wave from the center of the map. If you want to learn more, as a reminder, there are links in the video description to both my Patreon where you can watch the recordings of my coaching sessions or to my Metaphy page where you can book in your own one-on-one coaching session with me. titre: 3 Minute Seraphine Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: why Play seraphine support well she has very long range she scales well into the late game she brings lock down Shields and heals and she is a massive AOE team fight threat however she is remarkably squishy with limited mobility and she is Mana hungry especially early seraphine's first passive is every third ability she casts cast twice instead her second passive is she gains a note stack every time she casts an ability per nearby Allied Champion for each note stack she has her next basic attack gains increased range and Deals increased damage sah Q throws out a skill shot which upon Landing expands into a large Circle dealing damage to All Enemies within and this does increased damage based on their missing Health sarapen W grants a shield to herself and all nearby allies in addition she grants herself a large amount of bonus movement speed and her allies A reduced amount if seraphine was already shielded at the time of the cast after a short delay there will be a pulse of healing which heals all nearby allies and this heal is increased for each Ally affected seraphine's e is a StraightLine skill shot which deals damage to All Enemies hit and slows them enemies that are already slowed to become rooted for the same duration as the slow and enemies that are already immobilized are also stunned for the same duration seraphine's ultimate is a longrange rectangular skill shot which deals damage to enemy hit and charms them the distance of this ability is reset when it hits an ally or enemy Champion so as long as it keeps passing through Champions it will go for a very long distance in addition if she hits an Allied Champion with it they instantly gain four notes from her passive for combos start with Al if available into your doubled and powerered E following up with q and W however choosing your doubled ability is your skill expression wh often double Q for damage or double w for heals and shields for matchup she can struggle into longrange supports who can abuse her weaker early game and she works well into engaged Focus supports disengaging from them and punishing them as they Retreat for Rons take this Airy gives you even more damage and even more utility for build order start World Atlas and pots into echo of Helia tier 2 boots and upgrade your item into a Dream Maker after this a moonstone and Redemption a great pickups before finishing a build with any of these as needed for skill order start Q then e then W before maxing Q than W then e taking ult whenever you can for Summoner spells take Flash and heal for synergies seraphine works amazingly well with longer range bot laners who bring a slow or hard CC starting the lane your main goals are to harass the enemy while protecting your carry you'll be wanting to throw out multiple cues whenever your enemy goes up to last hit as it's incredibly difficult to dodge throw out your ease when you can get more damage or when your opponent tries to engage on you and when fighting an empowered W is insane value for easy trades your q and E easily pokes through minion waves so you'll often find yourself pushing the wav so make sure keep up deep Wards to spot any ganks when you hit Level six not only does your engage become amazing but your disengage also becomes amazing too so if your opponent ever engages charm them and deal massive damage entering the mid game take your Tower and rotate mid with your carry this puts two strong champions in the middle of the map to Siege and rotate for objectives entering late game team fights you want to play as the backline Mage support your main goal here is land a massive alt Charming the entire enemy team and then following up your e and Q for massive CC and damage and you almost always want to use your double w for massive heals and shields

titre: How To Play Jungle - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: This video will go over how to play jungle and what you can expect while playing this role. So, what makes a champion a jungler? The jungle is almost a wild card position where massive variety of champions can be played in a variety of different ways. And on a very simple level, this comes down to champions who either enjoy ganking or champions who enjoy farming. On the ganking end, we have champions like Ivern, Rammus, and Zac who don't need as much gold to be effective. And they're also very effective at quickly running into a lane and locking down an opponent. More towards the middle, we have champions like J4 and Vi who love ganking but also do need high gold income to be effective. And on the farming end, we have champions like Master Yi and Bel'Veth who will likely be farming all game and ganking rarely as they need so much guaranteed money. As a jungler, instead of going to a lane, you go to the camps in between. On the first clear of your jungle, you have two main options. The first and most consistent is just a full clear your jungle. If you start on the red side of the map, you can kill the camps in this order. And if you start on the blue side of the map, you can clear the camps in this order. The second way to do your first clear is clear one half of your jungle and go for a gank while you're level three and the opponent's level two. If your champion brings very strong ganks, this can be a great way to catch your opponent off guard. And from here, you do just clear the other half of the jungle and go for Scuttle Crab. At 2 minutes 55 on the game clock, Rift Scuttles appear in the river. These provide a ton of gold and experience. You'll often have to fight for them. So, you normally only want to use one Smite while clearing your jungle and save the second Smite to secure this. From here, either back and keep farming or go for a gank. Ganking is essentially running into one of your lanes to try and kill or damage the enemy laner. When ganking, always consider the three main points of where is the lane pushed to, what escapes does the enemy have, and what CC does your team bring. If you bring a lot of the lockdown yourself, it can be a good idea to go for lanes that bring the damage. And if you only have damage, it's going to be way more successful ganking a lane that brings the CC for you. And an important tip is if you're playing a jungler with a dash, don't just use it immediately. If you're ever able to simply walk up to the enemy champion, just do it. This can put them into a dreadful situation where you force them into a fight. And then, if they do flash out of it, you can simply use your Amumu Q or Lee Sin Q or whatever you have to run them down. Otherwise, as a jungler, just keep farming and looking for ganks while you can. When you back, you always want to trade in your ward for a sweeper. Clearing out enemy vision is just a fundamental part of getting good ganks in as well as taking objectives easier. One of your main duties in the game are all the big neutral objectives. These include all dragons, grubs, heralds, and barons. These objectives simply win games. And you have the Smite summoner spell which allows you to deal massive damage to secure them. So, your basic game plan every game will be gank a lane, try and get a kill, or push them out of lane, then convert that into killing an objective while they can't contest. Lastly, as a jungler, you start the game by buying a pet. The red pet grants damage and a slow. The green pet makes you tankier and the blue pet makes you faster. And you can't go too wrong with which one you choose. By killing jungle monsters, you can upgrade your pet twice. And with each upgrade, your Smite deals additional damage and you get some other bonuses. Starting out as a jungler can be daunting. You have the ability from the start of the game to impact all of your laners, which often means you have your teammates blaming you for their mistakes. But, don't sweat it. Just mute them, and as long as you're farming hard where you can, ganking where you can, and making sure to get to objectives just before they spawn, you're doing a great job.

So, open your textbooks and let's get started with the first part of the Lux player's brain. This is the binding impulse, and this is the part of their brain responsible for their Q ability. Because Lux players don't have to be creative with the use of their Q, because as long as it lands their enemies die either way. So, whether they're securing kills in the mid lane or stealing kills in the bot lane, the Lux players know what they bring to the team. And that's how they feel for just hitting one Q. Typically, we see dopamine responses like this in combo characters like Irelia and Lee Sin, because they work really hard to pull off something great. But Lux players can exceed these levels just by hitting their Q. And the feeling never fades for them. Whether it's their first Q or their hundred thousandth Q, the Lux players always pride themselves on it. Because Lux players don't need a lot to be happy. So, they can enjoy the game with their simple character while everyone else gets mad at the game with their characters. And all they need to do is hit a few Qs. And that is the result of the first part of their brain. And now we can move on to the second part of the Lux player's brain. This is the detonation dome, and this part of their brain is responsible for their E ability, Lucent Singularity. And when Lux players cast it, it creates a circular area on the ground that slows people. And they can recast it at any time to ensure the damage goes off. The problem is when Lux players get greedy with it, and they wait way too long before detonating it. Contrary to popular belief, the damage doesn't go up the longer it sits. So, if the slow isn't providing value, then there's really no point in waiting. But, the Lux players tell themselves that they should. And if they do choose to wait out the ability, then they almost always wait too long. And by the time they finally detonate it, the enemy has already stepped off of it. But, it's not the Lux player's fault. It's the fault of Riot Games who gave the Lux players an ability that wasn't point and click. Because high brain power doesn't run in the Crown Guard family. And we can see that in the case of Garen players as well. But, after their first 100 games, and the Lux players realize they can recast their E manually, that's the point where they usually do. And then they realize they can poke the entire lane phase without trying. So, beware a Lux player who knows they can recast their E. [laughter] And all of it starts by just learning how to recast their E. And that is the function of the detonation dome. >> [music] >> And now we can move on to the third part of the Lux player's brain. This is known as the personal space cortex. >> [music] >> And this is the part of the brain that keeps the Lux player alive. Because Lux is what's known as an artillery mage. She has some of the longest range poke in the entire game. And this is how Lux players like it to be. Because they want to provide all of the damage with none of the risk. But, Lux players often die if an enemy can close the distance on them. And as a survival mechanism, they've developed this part of their brain around it. And this part of their brain ensures that nobody will ever enter their personal space if they can help it. So, if an enemy runs towards the Lux player, their first instinct is to throw their Q. Because that can stop them in place and let them get a free kill. But, if that doesn't work, the Lux players run around and don't look back. They want to get as far away from the danger as possible. If you enter a Lux player's personal space, they become extremely defensive. And this is why they also bring barrier and hold their W just in case. Because the fun comes to a stop if you actually kill them. And they're fine with backing up as long as it means they can poke you from off the screen. And so, that's exactly what they do. Trying to chase a Lux is like a cat chasing a laser pointer. You can try all you want, but you're never actually going to catch her. And after the enemy has wasted all of their time chasing her and dodging her first Q, she gets another. And by the time the second Q hits, they scream Demacia, and you die. And this above all may be the reason Lux players are so hated, because they get to have their way and do whatever they want to you. But the second it's your turn to hit them, they run for the hills. At least until the time they turn around and kill you anyway. But just remember, it's not personal from the Lux players. It's not like they want to have this defensive play style. You should just stop trying to kill them. That way they can poke you from a normal distance instead. But unfortunately for the Lux players, they have to develop this part of their brain to feel safe, which is also unfortunate for anyone who ever wanted to kill a Lux. So the next time you see a Lux player running for the hills, you'll see this part of their brain at work. And then you can marvel at the science you've learned here today. And if you're enjoying this breakdown of the Lux player's brain, then let us know who we should brain study next. The fourth region of the Lux player's brain is known as the spark reflex, and this is the largest part of their brain. And it opens up at level six when they unlock their ultimate. And just like most of Lux's kit, her ultimate is very simple. It does big damage in a big line. And unlike most ultimates in the game, it's not on an ultimate cool down. This is one of the shortest cool down ultimates in the game, and it only gets shorter as the game goes on. So Lux players don't have to manage it very well. Lux players pretty much use their ultimate anytime they want to. This could include just killing a wave or aiming it at a random enemy, or trying to steal an objective. Because whether it works or not, it'll be back in 15 seconds. But their favorite time to use their ultimate is when they land their Q. And this is where Lux players are known for their one shots. Because if the Lux player lands their Q and you're rooted, they can just ult on top of you. And if that's not enough to kill you outright, then they can always throw in their E for good measure. And so they get all of that massive payoff just for hitting a Q. And that's why this is labeled as a reflex, because every time they hit a queue, they instinctively press R, even if they're not even level six or it's still on cool down. titre: The Ultimate Season 12 SPLIT PUSH GUIDE  | League of Legends
transcription:
You’ve probably heard of this thing called ‘split 
pushing’ a thousand times before right? You know   what the general gist is, but when you sit 
down in front of your overpriced gaming PC   and boot up League, you’re completely lost? Well, 
today we’re going to help you figure out when,   why and how to properly split push. 
So sit back, relax and let us do the   talking because we’re going to quickly brush 
over how to split push in League of Legends! 
  First of all, let’s quickly go over what Split 
Pushing even is. It refers to a champion moving   into a weak or undefended lane to apply pressure 
by pushing the wave in attempts to gain a tower   advantage. Most players think of top laners 
doing this, but mid laners and bot laners can   also be split pushers later on into the game. 
Usually the top and bot lane are the ones being   pushed. Why? Because they’re longer which means 
it’ll take the opponent longer to get to you. 
  So, when can you actually split push? This 
is probably the hardest thing to figure out.   First of all, it has to be late enough 
in the game that you’re strong enough   to go 1-vs-1 in case an opponent shows up. 
You should also make sure your team is able to   4v5 or 4v4 teamfights, so if you’re behind 1000-0 
on kills and not even objective bounties can help,   just forget about it. Stick to your team. 
You should also have teleport available.   You might be off clobbering down the top lane 
tower, but your team starts a fight at dragon.   You have to be there quickly to get that 
objective, so make sure to have teleport,   or some form of global that 
can get you there quickly. 
  Another important aspect to have 
when split pushing are escape tools.   So champions with escape abilities will be 
much better at split pushing than others. 
  You also need enough ward coverage all around 
the map. Make sure that the jungle isn’t dark.   You need to be able to know whether the enemy team 
is heading your way, ready to collapse on to you.   So keep an eye on the mini map at all times! 
Another important aspect is to have a wave   available and it’s best to stack your minion 
waves. How can you do this? Slow. Pushing.   To do this- you kill the back caster minions. 
This will make the wave slowly push and allow   the wave to stack. So, you take your stacked 
wave and march ahead towards the enemy tower. 
  So, now that we have the basics down, 
when is the perfect time to split push?   Usually you’ll want to split push, while the 
rest of your team is putting pressure elsewhere.   This means the enemy team needs to decide whether 
to stop you from pushing, or to stop your team   from getting an objective. Make sure this is 
done cross-map, creating global map pressure! 
  Now, we’ve already touched upon which champions 
can split push, but let’s go over a few examples.   One of the best split pushers is Jax. He’s able to 
take down turrets quickly and affectively, while   also being tanky enough not to get one-shot in 
case the enemy team tries to counter your play. 
  Another top lane menace who is an 
insanely strong split pusher is Camille.   She has damage and can play quite 
safe thanks to her innate shield.   She’s also got her hookshot in case 
she needs to bail from any situation,   which means she can safely push and escape. 
Nasus is another split pushing monster. He   just has to gain passive stacks and he’ll 
become unkillable eventually. Even if he   gets ganked he won’t go down and he’ll be able 
to take the objective he is looking to take. 
  Hopefully we could help you a little on your 
split pushing adventure, make sure to keep   practicing – and communicating since this is 
key when split pushing. Until next time!

titre: The SECRET to COUNTERING Assassins as ADC - League of Legends
transcription: by far the biggest issue that we see from low ELO adcs is consistently their inability to deal with enemy assassins it always feels like the enemy Zed can get the sloppiest of combos on you but you'll die anyway due to how much absurd damage there is in the game nowadays and this all feels the worst as an ADC since you're the biggest recipient of every assassin's attention you're the squishy Target that's a free 300 gold for them so you have to endure the worst of it all not to worry though in this guide we will be covering the top ways of dealing with assassins on the enemy team plus a secret tip that not even professional players are abusing that will make your games 10 times easier if you use it properly so let's get right into it all right the most important thing to remember when it comes to dealing with assassins as an ADC is doing everything within your power to avoid the endless cycle of death that most players fall into we know that you can't do anything about your team feeding opponents but the problem is that if you take part in the process then there's really nothing that you're going to be able to do to survive later on anyway if you begin dying then the problem is that not only are you giving the enemy free gold but you are also falling behind especially in levels level ups are a huge source of defensive stats in the game if you're down a ton of levels to the enemy assassin they will be able to One-Shot you with even the most mediocre of combos it's much harder to kill you when you're actually close to even on levels with them so this is an absolute priority but that's of course all easier said than done how do you actually go about avoiding dying in the first place well there are a lot of easy to follow tips that you can Implement that should drastically increase your chances of avoiding death the first is the simplest of all your most important skill to master versus assassins is just having better map awareness you're an ADC so of course assassins can easily kill you that's literally their job that's why they're in the game what this means is that you need to be much more Vigilant of where everyone on the map is at all times if you don't know where the enemy is lurking then you need to be far more careful of where you stand in lane or how you rotate around the map for example this Zia is way too greedy to get to mid lane quickly to farm the wave when she could have easily taken a far safer route to guarantee her safety okay but that may leave you thinking that you're not going to be able to farm for most of the game right if you're always hugging your Towers or teammates then you literally can't do what we recommend that you do in almost every single skillcapped guide which is to power Farm like crazy and yes unfortunately you won't be able to just walk into lanes and mindlessly farm all game you'll need to be incredibly aware of where the enemy assassin is on the map at all times and look for Windows of opportunity where you can farm for example this High ELO Jinx knew aalii had been dead from a previous fight that meant that she was safe to go farm out the side wave while aalii had to get back out onto the map but once she sees her in the river Jinx knows that she has just enough time to farm out one more side wave before retreating to safety but even if you can't do that it's honestly not a big deal a huge problem players generally face in this game is that they often forget to see things from their opponent's perspective for a moment instead of seeing things as an ADC versus assassins imagine that you're in the mid lane one side has a cidan on it and the other a Renekton let's say that in this matchup things are going completely even during the lane phase they're both csing similarly and neither is gaining a significant lead over the other here's a question how do both of these players feel based on their circumstances in the game well it's pretty obvious that Renekton should be incredibly depressed whereas the cidan is ecstatic that he hasn't died five times already the reason being that it's renekton's duty to shut down cidan in that lane otherwise he's going to get outscaled to Oblivion and although the ADC versus Assassin and dynamic isn't quite that extreme you can think of things very similarly as you go about your game it's okay to not Farm every wave and jungle Camp efficiently as long as you're denying giving the enemy assassin kills they are balanced around scoring a ton of kills so if they don't snowball then they naturally fall behind in farm most of them generally have weak wave clear and begin to be useless in fights compared to what other Champions can do now the most common piece of advice that you'll hear from any player on how to deal with assassin is to just group with your teammates if you're paired up with Champions that have hard CC then the previous point that we made about how there's so much damage in the game turns in your favor instead should the Assassin tank any CC whatsoever they should be instantly deleted before they can kill you unfortunately as we're sure you're all aware of being surrounded by your teammates doesn't guarantee that you won't die with the billions of ways that they have to get in and out of fights assassins can still easily kill you even when you're close to your allies but the better way of playing around your teammates versus assassins is more often than not using them as bait rather than working together with them keep in mind that assassins are combo oriented and thus cool down Reliant by Nature what this means is that one of the best times to fight back versus them is after they've attacked one of your teammates and are out of gas as seen here the enemy LeBlanc used all of her burst on the recx ey although Caitlyn gets Sona aled it literally doesn't matter since there's no damage left from LeBlanc so they both get obliterated by Kate's sustained DPS on the opposite side of things notice how during this team fight that the enemy Kiana has not tried to enter The Fray that's because she doesn't want to commit any damage to the beefier targets on jyn's team although jyn isn't doing a lot in this fight the fact that neither he nor Senna have mispositioned means that the enemy Kiana isn't doing anything either but here comes the problem Jinn is upset that he isn't doing anything and literally walks right up to the Kiana to get comboed and die if the Assassin is very obviously saving all of their spells for you then keep in mind that it's often completely fine for you to play the way Jin was at the start of it all it's sometimes literally correct to do nothing and just wait for your opponents to use their spells before you go in all right moving on another big issue that we see from all players not just adcs is wasting precious cooldowns when you're dead anyway not always but usually if you're already in an Assassin's range then blowing your flash or other defensive tools will not help you get away especially if you're flashing away from a rangar who is next to a brush just saying it's insane how consistent of a problem this is as a player you're going to inevitably mess up that is fine but you need to learn how to build the discipline to not press every button on your keyboard every time you do so recognize that you're dead and save your CDs more often than not we see so many situations where a player like this Ash has blown their Flash from earlier pointlessly and proceeds to die again when it would have actually saved her later on this all ties back to our earlier point on the cycle of death that adc's face you die once and it just gets easier to kill you again and again especially when you die and waste your defensives now we're finally at our secret tip to completely shutting down assassins as an ADC while everything prior to this moment is an important skill to master for dealing with them there is a much easier and simpler solution that players do not abuse nearly enough when it comes to shutting them down there is a really really easy build that most adcs can pivot to to increase your survivability by a massive amount we know there are a lot of players out there who simply copycat every Pro build that they see you know who you are you run magical Footwear and biscuits in your Rune tree as they do then there's the enlightened gigachad adcs who run absolute focus and Gathering storm because they developed the unique skill known as reading comprehension regardless of which type of player you are if during the draft phase you see a ton of high burst Champions on the enemy team then we'd recommend that you adapt your secondary Rune tree to resolve to instead take conditioning and overgrowth not only that but if you learn to manage your Mana slightly better you can add overheal on top of that from the Precision tree if you pair those runes with Shield bow as your Mythic item then it becomes infinitely harder for Assassins to kill you let's take a look at an incredible unrealistic situation Misfortune here will allow the enemy Fizz to land a max range ultimate on her into a full combo plus ignite she is literally standing still tanking one of the highest burst combos from any assassin in the game and did not die thanks to this Rune setup the reason this is so strong versus assassins is that if you think about it conditioning on its own nearly nullifies one of the penetration items that assassins tend to get and that's without building any extra resistances in your items not only that but since you're taking less damage in general that means that all health and shields on you are also even more effective that's why we advise you to take overgrowth overheal and shield bow along with it don't get us wrong this tip shouldn't replace everything else that we've covered in this guide all the other tips to dealing with assassins do come first but learning viable builds in the current meta that allow you to itemize defensively without conceding too many damage stats will make your life infinitely easier as an example example let's take a look at this cogma he's gone the red tree for his secondary runes and rageblade as his second item if he had the previous setup that we mentioned and knew that kgma could pivot to wht's end as his second item then this ensuing duel would have gone so much better for him he would be able to literally press W and stand still and destroy this aali we do have to mention that yes there are certain adcs like Ezreal and lethality Baris who can't build this way but almost any ADC that can go Shield as their first item can go this defensive route and abuse how broken it is at shutting down assassins now what you find here on YouTube is just the tip of the iceberg if you want to unlock your true potential then you need to dive into skillcap tocom we have the largest catalog of League of Legends guides in the entire world with over, 15500 guides and 350 unique courses you get brand new guides every week exclusive to our website along with our Smurf commentaries where our Challenger experts walk you through how to carry out of the exact rank you're stuck in still unsure well you can have all your questions answered by those same Challenger experts need one-on-one coaching we got you covered with handpick coaches trained to the highest standard don't have time for that use direct Pro pick a past game you played and within 24 hours get a personalized video from a top 100 Challenger player breaking down exactly what you can do better the best part all of this comes with a rank Improvement guarantee if you don't climb at least five divisions while actively using skillcap you can claim a refund no questions asked so what are you waiting for head to skill cap.com and get the rank you've always wanted Link in the description below all right everyone that's going to wrap it up for today we hope that those tips help you deal with assassins in your own games thanks so much for watching and we'll see you next time

titre: How I Get Every Last Hit in League of Legends
transcription: CSing efficiently is the most consistent way to get gold in carry games in League of Legends. A high elo player can simply lean on their impeccable CSing skills to create a tangible advantage in any low elo game and come out on top. Let me tell you the secret to CSing that midlaners like Faker use and how it can be utilized by any champion in any role to get more CS instantly. I've been playing a lot of mages in bot lane lately. ADC is constantly being nerfed and adjusted. Yet somehow mages like Carus and Swain keep reappearing with 53% win rates when played in the bot lane. Riot seems to be completely blind to this. If a marksman has that win rate, it's nerfing time. If you're a seasoned mid laner, you may know this trick. Because you had to learn it the hard way. Why do you think Faker always has perfect CS? Mid laners are some of the hardest champions to CS with because they have auto attacks with really slow wind up animations and low damage. A good mid laner knows they can't waste their cooldowns to farm in the early levels or they will be punished heavily by a good opponent. If you've ever tried to CS on someone like Synra, Annie, or Vaggar without using your abilities, you know exactly what I'm talking about. The secret trick is positioning your last hits correctly. When you auto attack, you are subject to two timebased waiting periods. Your auto attack windup animation, and the time it takes your auto attack to travel from your champion to the target. The secret to CSing perfectly without using your abilities is shockingly simple if you think about it, but most people don't notice it for years. You can't eliminate the auto attack windup animation, but you can eliminate the travel distance. The secret is standing right up on top of the minion you're trying to last hit. This gives your auto attack no travel time and means you only have to time your auto attack animation to the last hit. Since your auto attack windup animation is always the same duration, unlike travel distance, which is variable depending on position, you get used to it pretty quickly, which takes the guesswork out of CSing. This small adjustment to how you position when CSing, will have a huge impact over an entire game. you'll find yourself missing those close call last hits less often and feel more in control of your farming prowess. Now, get out there and try this CS trick in your games. Let me know in the comments below if this secret helps you out. And if you want to learn another League of Legends secret that will help you win more games and climb, check out this video. deploy.

all the enemies it will reset all the directions of your q this is because every enemy will cover one q direction since you are standing in the middle when your w hits your w will still go off even when you get cc there are two ways your w can tell you if an enemy is hiding in a bush or not first your w will make a different sound when you hit an enemy the second way is to queue in the direction increased lifesteal each slash strikes the lowest health enemy and the amount of slasher skills with her attack speed this ability looks like aurelia's w on steroids there is no enemy cc that can stop it all that enemy cc does is stop the attacks for the whole cc duration but the damage negation part will still continue if she gets suppressed she will do it in her e even polymorph will not stop the damage reduction while charmed she will stop her attacks but fierce will do nothing thoughts and berserks will redirect her damage to another target and while blinded you will miss all your attacks your evil even attack invisible enemies and the spikes will point to the enemy's direction so also enemies that are hiding in a bush when two or more enemies have the same percentage hp the closest enemy will be targeted by the attacks while in tamkensha's ultimate you will still attack the enemies around you while in your e there is a 0.75 seconds timer before you can reactivate it and stop stack your lethal tempo with just one single e when tower diving with an ally that isn't tanky be sure to aggro some tower shots because your e can easily tank two of them before it wears off you can infinitely takedowns against an epic monster or champion leaves behind a void coral you can then consume a void coral and it will explode slowing and dealing true damage afterwards velvet changes into her true form for 1 minute while in her true form she gains extra stats consuming a void coral from baron or herald will grant you a true form for 3 minutes and it will cause minions that die nearby
