shoot range projectiles at nearby enemies prioritizing those that Zyra has attacked zyra's W places a seed on the floor and she can store up to two of these at one time killing an enemy massively reduces the cooldown and killing an enemy Champion or large monster fully resets one seed back to you zyra's e is a StraightLine skill shot which sends out a wave of vines which deal damage to and root enemy's hit this also has a much larger hit box and if seed within it it will grow a plant the plants these grow are much shorter range but their attacks will slow your enemies zyrus ultimate places down a massive Circle which deals damage to enemies as it expands and after 2 seconds it then knocks up all enemies within plants within this ultimate gain 50% Max Health gain increased in size titre: Main in Minutes - Zeri ADC Guide for League of Legends
transcription: Zer is one of those champions that feels completely different from every other ADC. Her her movement is a little bit different in her fights revolve around rhythm more than raw mechanics. But when she used to flow, she's an incredibly rewarding champion with a high skill ceiling. Part marksman, part mobility mage, and she's not the type to sit back and just simply right click. She's all about repositioning, weaving damage, and outplaying opponents through movement. Let's quickly go over some of her strengths and weaknesses. Starting out with her strengths, Zuri shines against teams that rely heavily on extended fights. Her ability to adapt to both AD and AP builds makes her one of the more flexible ADCs in the game. Although most players prefer sticking to her AD play style for consistent DPS, one of her biggest strengths is her escape potential. Her spark surge, her E ability lets her slide over walls and out of danger, giving her more safety than most immobile ADCs. In team fights, once she stacks her ultimate and starts zipping around, she becomes almost impossible to lock down. However, she's not without her challenges. Starting out some of her weaknesses. Zer's burst fire. Her Q is her main damage source, but it behaves differently from normal ADC's autos, meaning that there is a learning curve in controlling her spacing and timing. She's also very dependent on items to hit her power spikes. If she falls behind, catching up can feel painful. And while her E gives her mobility, it's also her only escape tool. And early on in the game, it does come with a pretty long cooldown. Once it's burned, she becomes very vulnerable to hard engage or all-ins. Let's discuss some of her abilities now. So, Zer's kit rewards fluidity and consistency. Her passive, living battery. Whenever she attacks, she will store up energy in her spark pack. When fully charged, her next attack will deal bonus damage. Up next, we have her cute ability, Burst Fire. This is going to be the core of her gameplay, and it replaces her traditional auto attacks, firing a burst of shots that count as onhit effects. Mastering her spacing and movement while constantly firing her cues is what separates the adversary from the great ones. Up next, we have her W ultra shock laser. This is a long range poke and slow tool that becomes much deadlier when fired through walls as it gains extra range and width. Up next, we have her E Spark Surge. This is going to be her dash. This is a wall ride that resets on champion hits and allows her to reposition or escape. Then finally, we have her ultimate, lightning crash. This is her power moment where she unleashes a massive AoE burst and gains overcharge, supercharging her Q attacks and making her faster and harder to catch. Your skill order is going to be QEW, putting points into R whenever possible. Let's now quickly discuss her combo. So, she'll typically start her fights with her ultimate, lightning crash, then go into a burst fire with her Q to build charge. Up next, she'll be using your E spark surge midfight to reposition, chase, or kite, then continue spamming your Q. Once you built up enough momentum, you can finish the kill with your ultra shock laser, your W to secure kills or poke through walls if enemies try to retreat. So, her typical combo will be ultimate into a Q. Then you'll use that overcharge from your ultimate into a auto attack E auto attack and then into three Q's to build more charge into an auto attack W. For her runes and summoners, you'll be taking flash and barrier. And we'll be running lethal tempo as our keystone backed by triumph legend elacrity and last stand in the precision tree. For your secondary runes, pick conditioning and overgrowth from the resolve tree. This will give you extra survivability as the game scales. Rune shards will be attack speed, adaptive force, and scaling, but feel free to adjust based on the matchup. Your itemization, you'll be starting with Wild Arrows for consistent poke and energy. Then build into Berserker's Greaves and Reance Hurricane to spread your Q damage during fights. Follow up with Infinity Edge for your major crit spike. Then Lord Dominic Regards for armor penetration. And then finally, finish off with a blood thirstster for sustain and durability. And this build currently holds a 53% win rate across over 25,000 matches. For your early game, everything's going to be about patience. Your range and damage early are not super great, and her Q takes time to get comfortable with in terms of spacing and accuracy. So, focus on farming safely and managing your wave while weaving Q hits whenever the enemy ADC steps up too close. Also, try to angle yourselves near walls so you can use your E defensively if you get engaged on. Micro wise, go ahead and prioritize spacing. Your Q has slightly more range than most ADC's autos, so use that to your advantage. And simply harass when possible, and don't overextend unless your support has strong peel or setup. You want to be playing for wave control and safety until that very first item spike with wild arrows. Once that is complete, your poke and DPS improve dramatically. Moving on to her mid and late game. Once you do get wild arrows and run hurricane, you will become a serious threat in extended fights. Now, your mid game is going to revolve around grouping with your team, contesting objectives, and using your mobility to kite out fights. With lethal tempo stacked, your attack speed and movement speed make you very slippery, allowing you to constantly reposition while dealing consistent damage. You want to keep your Q firing almost constantly while maintaining movement, and you want to plan your E usage. It's both a chase and escape tool, but using aggressively without vision will get you caught very easily. Zeri does not s sideelane as efficiently as a lot of other ADCs. So you're going to focus around playing objectives like dragon and herold where her AOE and mobility really shine. Moving a little bit later into late game once you get IE once you get bloodthirst your sustain and burst reach your very high levels and you just simply want to sit back, wait for an engagement to happen and you want to drop your lightning crash your ultimate and start shredding. While you are overcharged, you are very hard to pin down. Your cues will fire faster. Your movement speed will skyrocket, leading you kite or chase with ease. Her real power comes from cleaning up team fights. To sum everything up here, Zary is at her best when she's moving constantly, firing, dashing, repositioning, and never standing still. She rewards awareness, timing, and consistency more than brute force. If you enjoy champions who reward mechanics, Zer would be a great one to pick up. Once you get comfortable with her rhythm, you'll find yourself both unpredictable and unstoppable. If you guys enjoyed the video, please consider liking and subscribing to the channel. See you guys in the next one. [Music]

titre: ALL NEW CHANGES for PATCH 26.05 - League of Legends
transcription: 26.5 is going to be a pro-focused patch with nerfs to Orianna, Azir, and Neeko. Well, we've got some buffs to Lee Sin and Mel. Now, we're going to cover everything that you need to know heading into this patch in today's video as we can expect 26.5 to hit live servers this upcoming Wednesday, March 4th. So, starting with the item changes, Hubris has been a pretty forgotten item as of late, rarely purchased as a core item on many champions. So, it's going to have its cost lowered for this patch going from 3,000 to 2,800 gold. Kayn, Qiyana, and Draven are a couple of champs to keep your eye on with Hubris for 26.5 as they had liked the item in the past. So, we're going to have to see if it makes a comeback on any of them. Now, Locket is going to receive a buff with its shield going from 200 scaling to 360 with the scaling starting at level one to now 290 scaling to 360 with the scaling starting at level eight. So, for reference here, level eight shield of Locket in 26.4 is 260. So, this means you're going to be getting 30 extra shielding from Locket at level eight in 26.5 as it's going to go up to 290. Banshee's Veil has continued to remain the better purchase over Locket in most cases in recent patches. So, the Locket buffs should bring the items closer in value. So, onto the champion changes now. Riot has a couple of buffs planned for Garen with his Q movement speed duration being increased from one scaling to 3.6 seconds to 1.4 scaling to 3.6 seconds. E damage total AD ratio is going up as well from 38 scaling to 50% to 40 scaling to 52%. Lee Sin is going to receive a damage buff to his Q going from 60 scaling to 180 to 65 scaling to 185. Now, this is both for Q1 and Q2. So, technically, it's a 10 damage increase instead of five. A few Lillia adjustments are on the way with her passive monster heal AP ratio being increased from 9 to 15%. Q AP ratio is going up from 30 to 35%. R base damage is going down by 50 at all ranks while the AP ratio drops from 45 to 40%. R cooldown is being increased by 10 seconds at all ranks. So, Riot does have these changes under the buff category. However, we'd be pretty surprised if they landed significantly net positive. The R nerfs are definitely pretty big, but maybe we're underestimating the passives and the Q buffs. Regardless though, Lillia shouldn't really be gaining much power at all for 26.5 and we honestly wouldn't be surprised if she actually lost power due to these R changes. After settling out around a 47% win rate since her mini rework, Malzahar is getting buffed for this patch with her Q damage per additional bolt is going from five scaling to nine to five scaling to 13. Her base damage is being increased from 100 scaling to 200 to 125 scaling to 275. And then passive minion mod is going to be reduced from 60 to 50%. Nocturne is going to be issued two buffs with his passive cool down being lowered from 13 to 12 seconds and then Q movement speed is going up from 15 scaling to 35% to 20 scaling to 40%. Volibear is buffed for this patch so that AD builds become more viable on him as his Q AD ratio is going up from 140 to 160%. W empowered bonus AD ratio is going up from 15 to 25%. [music] W mana cost is going to be dropping from 30 scaling to 50 to 20 scaling to 40 and then E mana cost is going to be reduced from 60 to 50. First up on the nerf list for this patch is Kha'Zix as his Q bonus AD ratio takes a hit going from 110 to 105%. Base movement speed is being nerfed as well going from 350 to 345. We've got a nerf on the way for Neeko as her Q cool down is going to now start on W clone expiration. Orianna is going to be tagged with a nerf as her Q cool down is going to be going up from six scaling to three seconds to seven scaling to three seconds. Taliyah is going to be losing some damage on her Q as the base is dropping from 55 scaling to 125 to 50 scaling to 120. Q bonus monster damage is going up however from 20 scaling to 40 to 23 scaling to 43. So less of a blow to jungle Taliyah compared to mid. And then with this being a pro focused patch, not a surprise at all to see Azir being nerfed with his health growth going down from 119 to 108. Varus is going to be adjusted in a few different ways with his Q max damage going down from 80 scaling to 400 to 80 scaling to 360. Q bonus 80 ratio is being changed from 130 scaling to 170% to 150 at all ranks. And W on hit damage is now going to have a 15% bonus 80 ratio added. So, some more changes here to try and combat lethality poke Varus while boosting the relative power of on hit Varus. Okay, guys, one last thing. Our rank up guarantee is absolutely insane. It's kind of like signing up for the gym and then just getting a refund if you don't get ripped. That's how confident we are in Skillcapped. We obsess over making the best guides with the top players, the rigorous testing, and top-tier video editing to make your climb as easy as possible. If you're ready to level up, just visit skillcapped.com and literally see the difference. So, there you have it, everyone. This patch is super light compared to the previous two that we've had. It's right. It doesn't want to shake up the meta very much right now with the first big pro tournament coming up soon called First Strike. Thank you all so much for watching, and we'll see you in the next one.

titre: Why People STILL Don't Play Aurelion Sol | League Of Legends
transcription: 2015 to 2017 was the notable period in League's history for marking a definitive turning point between the old cookie cutter champion designs of the early days and the more contemporary if not unusual designs we see now. 2016 in particular featured a most unusual lineup of cast members whose kits and play styles could not be more unorthodox even if they tried Jin Talia Cled Ivvern and Dorellian Soul. Empirically speaking results were hit or miss. On one hand, we had a very successful design in Jyn, quite possibly the most well-received in history. But on the other hand, people weren't all that interested or receptive towards the remaining. Aelion Soul, in particular, was especially hard to wrap our heads around, being the only champion in the game who demanded spacing in the most literal sense of the word, more often than not in an unproductive way. He was so unpopular that Ryan had to reimagine his gameplay to nearly the same extent as a full VGU, where the solely today scarcely resembles that of his past iteration. Though his play rate has indeed reached a more gainful number in comparison, he's back to a low relevant state despite having a measurably more practical kit. As their two episodes on the champion wasn't enough, I feel like it's necessary to give him a third since we're essentially discussing a totally different character. So, in today's episode of Vinyl and Place, we'll talk about how the Star Fortress has been struggling to maintain consistent presence even post gameplay update. The factor is responsible for his decline in relevance can be split into three separate parts, and I feel like it's important to discuss all of them at length, as each of them contributed to his loss of presence. Like with most reworks of gameplay updates, the initial spike of a champion's popularity is considerable, as players are interested in checking out their new gameplay and how strong they might be. For Aelian Soul, though, that jump was nothing short of unbelievable. From a sub 1% play rate that skyrocketed up to over 15%, he was an overnight sensation to say the least, and quite competent, too. Much of that can be written off as having overtuned numbers and that definitely played a part. But pragmatically and objectively, Riot succeeded at reimagining a Soulskit in a way that made him more usable for a broader audience. However, in order for them to have done this, they had to essentially strip away a significant part of his identity that while unquestionably a detriment to his popularity, housed most of his unique strengths that few of any other mages could offer, establishing the first big part of his current unpopularity. Aurelion Soul is too easily comparable to other mages that it becomes a contest of numbers as to who you should pick. And that's a contest that a soul's currently losing. The advantage of his old passive was that it fundamentally made him a consistent threat to the enemy team. While Star Search and Voice of Light were powerful tools when used optimally, especially the former after they made it so you could generate a star with a large enough mass to take up the literal entire screen. Where his DPS really came from was of course center of the universe causing an orbit of three stars to damage enemies that came into contact with them. These stars would be perpetually active with the exception of being under crowd control making it easy for him to damage enemies without even having to give his undivided attention to them like other mages do. In a sense had some of the best AoE pressure in the game of which you could exert an on the enemy team even as soon as level two. a single cast of his W could simultaneously push the wave at a rate far quicker than anyone else can match and harass the enemy laner. Not too late after, thanks to his comet of legend having a semi- global radius while being a basic ability, Asol could roam around the map and get stuff done very early in the game. A star player Huhi had a reputation for exploiting that privilege to gain a sizable belief for his team even before the 5-minute mark. Then during the mental late game, his stars would continuously grow in strength while at the same time keeping their inconspicuous nature. Throughout a team fight, ASOL would be racking up an insane amount of DPS on the enemy team. But it would also be more difficult for them to know or see where it's coming from given how small stars were in comparison to all the explosions and stuff happening. More importantly, his star surge could single-handedly win his team the game when prepared properly. And in general, his focus was about control. Unlike other battle mages who typically wanted to stay in a certain spot, ASOL was also quite hard to fight without either speed or lock down as he was one of the few mages in League who could continuously attack you while moving around, making him very good at kiting and spacing. Ultimately, yes, his unconventional way of attacking made him one of the most, if not the most unpopular modern champion in the game. But for those willing to pick him up, he carried a repertoire no other mage could even hope to match. After being updated, Asil became much more of a generic battle mage, someone who could attack him in range and continuously apply DPS while also establishing control of an area. But in turn, he was brought in line with other battle mages, pitting his numbers and performance against theirs. Now, that's not to say he no longer has the explosion factor he once had. Falling Star, while not the highest damaging ultimate in the game, makes its presence known, that's for sure, having one of the largest effect radi of any attack in the game, even among other ultimates. But from a base kit point of view, he became more, I suppose, generic, if we're putting it bluntly. For sure, it made him more popular initially, but again, that was only because Asil had overinflated numbers. Numbers that were severely and repeatedly put down to prevent him from over dominating the metag game, which he unsurprisingly did post rework. Starting from 13.13 when he got his comprehensive gameplay update, ESOL received 13 numbers adjustments dating to June of this year. Of those 13, want to guess how many of them were nerfs? 10 of them. Of the remaining three, which one was the direct buff? Only one. The other two were just power budget swaps that weren't intended to be a net positive. Asil's new kit was so good at being a battle mage that he would almost consistently overpower most other battle mages. He was out DPSing Rumble, Swain, Talia, Victor, Vladimir, Anivvia, Carthus, Alzahar by a considerable margin, especially given his infinite scaling and extreme area control. For example, Breath of Light was for all intents and purposes a better Rumble queue as he could cast and recast in at a much faster rate while also having it active for as long as he could pay the mana cost. The range of the attack also capped at 920, far exceeding most battle mage ranges and giving him one of the best neutral games out of the entire cast. Now that Astral Flight was also made into a combat oriented spell, he could additionally move while attacking, using it to chase enemies or running away while attacking them, giving him an even wider thread radius and the ability to act as cover fire for his team. As he grew singularity, it would essentially be an Invia ultimate on a basic ability, giving him tons of area control when used in tandem with Rise, becoming a living nightmare for any champion without a dash ability. Asil's team fight pressure had around the same extent as his previous iteration. The only difference was that it was a lot easier to apply, which only made the sheer power he had more conspicuous. The reason they had to nerf him over and over and then keep nerfing him even after he felt so weak was because ASL was really just that strong. bunch of the roster straight up had no way to deal with him on their own, causing an infinite scaling champion to essentially get a free pass to scale and then keep scaling. He was basically Smolder if Smolder had double his current cast range and double the wave clear. Asil didn't have to risk anything to scale because of his neutral game and linear margin for error. So, they had to push him into a gutted state to avoid dealing with it. Putting it simply, Ail is a balanced nightmare. Believe it or not, old Asol was extremely overtuned. We just didn't see it because one, you'd run into him once every maybe 200 games, and two, he was a lot harder to play given how conditional every part of his kit was, including his DPS. Now that he's way more consistent, it became apparent that each of his abilities are individually so strong that he would just outclass every other battle mage and leak. He could control way more area than Anivia, Swain, Carthus, and Rumble, champions notorious for impacting entire battlefields at once. He had more range than Cassia, Rise, and Talia, other machine gun champions. He also had more front loader burst pressure than Victor and Vladimir, especially with a primed to falling star. Battle mages typically hang around the 650 to 800 range. Asol could attack around 900 plus. Astral flight could extend it even further, letting him challenge artillery mages at a distance while also permanent cutting immo burst mages like Brand Varian Twist of Fate who can never get close enough to one-shot him even if they did have the means to. Under no circumstances would I say he has no weaknesses. We're going to get to that in just a moment. What I am saying is that a byproduct of his new design was that he became too good of a champion that the only quote unquote safe place to put him is in an underpowered state. Orelian Soul is a classic example of a champion with the concept so good that a mountain of restrictions had to be put on him that ultimately rendered the potential of that concept irrelevant. As he got weaker, so too did the player base's desire to play him. But due to his design, he can never get stronger beyond a certain point or else he becomes unfair to play against. If that sounds familiar to you, it's because a number of champions already have that problem. Like Annie, a stat check mage who cannot be truly allowed to 100 to zero target without being super fed on account of her burst rotation being practically guaranteed. But since all she can do is burst, if she isn't allowed to, then what's the point? Though it may not look like it, Asol is also a stat checker who doesn't have many ways to outplay his opponent while conversely not giving his opponent many ways to outplay him. Despite not having a single point andclick ability in his skin, all of Ail's attacks are impossible to dodge by just moving away in time. Breath of Light has an instant cast time, meaning the time it takes for a space vomit to go from him to you is zero. He presses it, you take damage. Singularity and Falling Star, their area coverage is too big for anyone to realistically sidestep it in time. This implies the only way you can deal with him is if he has some way to interrupt him, get out of the way immediately, or just try to kill him faster than he can kill you. A sizable portion of the roster just doesn't have an answer against Asil. emphasizing why they have to keep him underpowered. Once he buys ri spam on you, chipping away your health while you struggle to catch up to him unless you have a dash or lock down of some kind. The inverse is what brings us to the second factor affecting his diminishing play rate. Orion also can't really do anything against the champions who do have the means to shut him down. Much like his py version, he's best at home when playing the neutral game, not really fully committing to attacking, but also not being forced to defend. The more he can free fire on you with Q, the better. So if he's caught in a situation where he needs to act fast, he might be even worse at that than his past version. At least old days had Voice of Light to use as a get off me tool against opponents who tried to engage on him. New Soul has literally nothing except astral flight which is too slow and too vulnerable to any champion that can rush him down quickly or hard CC him out of it. All of his worst matchups happen to comprise champions who could do one or both. Yas by far his worst enemy. Wow, shocker. Because he can chase him down to the ends of the earth while permacy seeing him all throughout. Then we have champions like Ekko who can also rush him down and stun him with W. Any who, if she does manage to get in range, nukes the hell out of him before he gets to set up. Diana, who can rush him down, then use Moonfall's brief interruption to stop him from flying away. Castin, who can also do the same. Said, who can chase him down forever and blast him quickly. Ireelia, who can rush him down and keep rushing him down. Katarina, who blinks around all over the place, preventing Asil from marinating her with this cube. Basically, Asil's a menace against tactic cast in light of them having no way to respond against his superior range or astral flight. But in turn, Ail completely folds against the other half of the cast who can blitz him down at a moment's notice and never let him breathe, literally. Match up volatility to that extent just doesn't reflect on a champion's widespread popularity that well. There needs to be a degree of consistency and agency in handling opposition. And at his current power level, ASIL just doesn't have it. But as I elaborated, they can't really give him any more latitude so as not to shut down the other half of the cast with no answer against him. He's a mage who's also a stat checker, not a good combination for balance. Final point pertains to his current situation. In addition to being a stat checker, Ail's an infinite scaler who infinitely scales in more ways than one. The more stardust he generates, the more% max health damage his Q does, the farther he can travel with his W, the bigger and more deadly his E becomes, and his ultimate can turn into a humongous AoE knockup. Past infinite scalers like Nasus and Vagar are kept in check because only one part of them gets stronger indefinitely. They still keep their inherent weaknesses from start to finish. ASOL not only does more damage, but he gets a bigger attack radius and longer travel distance, making it much easier to apply said increased damage. To counter this, ASOL has one of the most unproductive early games of any champion. While he's not as helpless as like Anasis or Kale, he also can't really do a whole lot. For the first 10 to 20 minutes of a game, Ails to kind of just sit in lane and play neutral as much as he can, making it for all intents and purposes a non-existent presence. While it can sit far back and chill by just pushing waves, lane opponents can run around the map and punish you for sitting back. You have no choice in the matter though because of how easy it is to destroy you just like a Kale Castan or Nassis. It's a damned if you do, damned if you don't situation, unless of course you get camped. But you have a better chance of winning the lottery than your jungler babysitting you for more than two games in a row. The higher up you go, the faster games get. The faster games get, the less time you have to scale. People would much rather play someone who can accomplish things immediately. Ironically, that was old as strength. Current Asil just doesn't deliver on that. He may have back when his numbers were high enough to get him to full power by Midame, but they made it take a lot longer. Now, to reiterate one final time, Asil is stuck in a catch22 situation where all positive aspects of him get canceled out by the negative consequences of those aspects. He needs to be slightly better than he is now for people to want to play him, but he can't be slightly better on account of being a stat checker. Other stat checkers get more leeway because they're inherently kept in line by circumstances like usually being melee. A souls ranged with some of the best area coverage in the game, making it that much harder for people to be okay with piss pressure than that of a Garand Darius or Morichiser. Finally, of course, a non-negligible portion of the player base finds his new design to be not that interesting to play. While he was even less popular back in the day, he was certainly more unique than how he is now, where you just Q spam non-stop and that's basically it. He feels too much like just another battle mage. And matchup wise, he's a very inconsistent one despite battle mages being the most consistent of the three subcategories. Personally, I'm still of the opinion that a soul could have kept his old passive and W if they just brought in the new Astral Flight, Singularity, and Falling Star. Each of those abilities makes Center of the Universe a lot easier to apply than Star Search Surge and Voice of Light ever could. That seems to be the case for a lot of people. I think his Q is just really boring gameplay. More practical, yes, no one can deny that. But there had to have been a way for him to preserve his old niche while modernizing him as opposed to giving him one of the most one-dimensional gameplay loops of any mage I've ever seen. But what do you guys think? Do you agree or disagree with my points on his unpopularity? Let me know in the comments down below. That's it for today. So, if you enjoyed the video, a like and subscribe would be much appreciated. Don't forget to follow me on Twitter, Paris Varum. Join my Discord server and check out my other W and place episodes if you haven't yet. Until next time, thanks so much for watching and I look forward to seeing you again soon. Take care. [Music] [Music]

titre: ALL NEW CHANGES for PATCH 26.06 - League of Legends
transcription: We finally got some nerfs to Ahri on the way. Shen is going to be nerfed as well after becoming the best jungler, while we've got a bunch of buffs to struggling picks like Skarner, Tryndamere, and Azir. So, as always, we're here to break everything down for you guys in as quick and condensed of a manner as possible, so you're completely caught up to speed with what you need to know for when 12.6 hits live servers this upcoming Wednesday, March 18th. So, starting off with all the item changes, we've got a very minor buff to Chemtech Chainsaw with cost of the item being lowered from 3,100 to 3,000 gold. newer to League, then Chemtech is probably an item you haven't even seen in any of your games, and for good reason, because it's super niche being that is the grievous wounds purchase for fighter champions. We don't see this buff doing anything to shift the priority of Chemtech, so it should continue to remain niche for 12.6. Sunfire Aegis is getting a quality of life change so that a Ruby Crystal is added to the build path. So, now it's going to be a bit more efficient on your path to completing the item as you'll no longer have to save up 1,000 gold before fully completing the item. After completing your Chain Vest and Bramble Vest, you would then need 1,000 gold to fully finish Sunfire. So, now if you end up having to base before you have 1,000 gold, you'll be able to snag an extra Ruby Crystal. Supports across the board are getting buffed for this patch with the pre-five minute kills allowed before you get penalized going up from seven to 24. Post-five minute kills allowed per minute are going up as well from four to six. So, if your teammates aren't taking farm throughout the mid game, or if your ADC dies early on and a massive wave crashes under your tower, you'll now be able to pick up that farm without getting penalized. As if Azir wasn't gutted enough for solo queue, his win rate dropped to 42% due to his 12.5 nerf, so Riot's giving him a few buffs for 12.6. Q AP ratio is going up from 35 to 55%. W damage is being increased with the per-level base damage going up from zero scaling to 45 to zero scaling to 72, while AP ratio is up as well from 32.5 scaling to 62.5% to 35 scaling to 65%. Lissandra is going to receive two changes with her Q cooldown dropping by one second at all ranks, while armor growth is going down from 4.9 to 4.2. One second may not seem like a big deal, but for a spell you can spam off cooldown and for a spell that was already at a pretty low cooldown before, this Q buff is really nice for Liss. A few buffs are on the way for Olaf with his base attack speed going up from 0.694 to 0.72. Q slow is being changed from 25 scaling to 45% to 30 scaling to 50%. However, slow duration is adjusted as well, going from 1.5 scaling to 2.5 seconds to 1 scaling to 3 seconds. And then E self damage is going up from 30 to 40% of damage dealt. Riot has a couple changes lined up for Skarner with his Q mana cost dropping from 45 to 30 and then Q max health damage is going up from 9 to 11%. Tryndamere is going to be issued a buff to his attack speed ratio as it's going up from 0.694 to 0.725. On to the champion nerfs now, we've got a change to Ahri with her Q base damage per hit dropping from 40 scaling to 140 to 35 scaling to 135. Pretty small nerf overall, so Ahri should remain a top tier mid for yet another patch. Pyke will be losing some durability for this patch with his base armor going down from 43 to 37. Shen has absolutely popped off as a jungler over the past patch, so Riot won't be wasting any time in nerfing him with Q monster damage dropping from 120 scaling to 200 to 100 scaling to 200. So, nothing too major with this one and Shen should still remain a really good jungle pick up for 26.6. Zac has taken over as one of the best junglers in recent patches, so Riot's going to be nerfing him in a few different ways. E monster damage is getting hit with damage dropping from 75 to 50. W base damage is dropping from 40 scaling to 120 to 30 scaling to 110. And then W bonus AD ratio is down as well from 50 to 30%. All right, guys, one last thing, our rank up guarantee is insane. It's like signing up for the gym and getting a refund if you don't get ripped. That's how confident we are in Skill-capped. We obsess over making the best guides with top players, rigorous testing, and top tier video editing to make your climb easy. If you're ready to level up, visit skillcap.com and see the difference. So, there you have it, everyone. Those are all the changes that we can expect to hit live servers for this upcoming Wednesday, March 18th. Thank you all so much for watching, and we'll see you in the next one.

titre: 3 Minute Vi Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: Why play Vi Jungle? Well, she brings great burst and constant damage. Her ganks are truly amazing. She also brings great crowd control and she easily dives the enemy backline. However, she's very reliant on landing her Q to engage and she does need a team to help her dive the backline. Vice's passive is periodically her next ability hit will grant her a shield. The cooldown of this passive is reduced every time she fully stacks her W. Vice's Q causes her to start charging which slows herself and increases the range, speed, and damage of the ability. Upon release, Vi dashes in a direction, dealing damage to all enemies hit and stopping when she hits an enemy champion. And when she hits an enemy champion, she deals damage to them and knocks them back. Vi's W is just a passive where her basic attacks on hit apply a stack onto the target. Upon applying the third stack, all are consumed to deal bonus damage and also reduce the target's armor. And after applying the third stack, Vi gains a large amount of attack speed. Vice E is an auto attack reset which can store up to two uses of this ability at one time. It empowers her next attack to have bonus range and deal damage in a cone behind that target. Vice's ultimate is one of the best guaranteed lockdowns in the game. It is a point-and-click ability used on an enemy champion, which causes Vi to dash to them. On her way to that target, if she dashes through anyone, she deals a good amount of damage to them and knocks them to the side. And then upon reaching her target, she knocks them in the air and deals a large amount of damage. For combos, your go-to is to use Q into an auto attack and E. Follow up with another attack into E before alting. If you need to guaranteed lock someone down, use ultimate into an attack and tap your Q. Attack into E before attacking into E again and attacking away. And to engage from basically anywhere, you can Q and flash into your ultimate before using attacks and E. For matchups, V is great into junglers who often rely on their mobility as she has guaranteed ways of locking them down. However, she can struggle against champions who can easily interrupt her Q or simply outduel her. For runes, take this. She procs conqueror with ease. For build order, start blue monster and pot into tier 2 boots and a sununded sky. From here, a black cleaver and sterax are great pickups before finishing a build with any of these as needed. For skill order, start W, then E, then Q before maxing Q, then E, then W, taking alt whenever you can. For summoner spells, take flash and smite. Starting the game, you have a few different options. The safest will always be to do a full jungle clear and then fight for scuttle crab if you have to into a gank. However, vice ganks from level three are also incredibly strong, so it's a very viable strategy to simply clear one half of your jungle and look for the gank, especially if the enemies are immobile or your team brings tons of lockdown. Keep full clearing as much as you can and your priority is to look for these high impact ganks. And once you have level six, you now have access to some guaranteed lockdown. So use it as early as you can possible to secure a kill and get an objective. Enter in the mid game. Having one or two items is where Vi is incredibly strong. Keep up aggressive wards. Use your mobility and CC for picks and skirmishes and then convert all of these into objectives. Entering late game team fights, you want to play as your team's engage diving onto their backline. Landing a Q or ultimate onto a backline carry is just a great way to start any fight. And as long as your team can follow up, there's not always many ways to stop you. Otherwise, if the other team is diving your backline, you're also great at peeling.
