titre: 3 Minute Ekko Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: Why play Echo Jungle? Well, he brings very high burst damage. He's tricky and difficult to kill. He has a massive AoE stun, and his ganks are pretty solid. However, if locked down, he can't ultimate and is much squishier. And he does need both time and items to scale. Ekko's passive are his attacks and damaging abilities apply a stack onto the target. Upon applying the third stack, all consume to deal a burst of damage and also grant Ekko a burst of movement speed. Ekko's Q is a straight line skill shot which starts out by going fast and then slows down when hitting a certain distance or hitting an enemy champion. When it slows down, it travels a small amount further and slows nearby enemies before returning back to Ekko, dealing damage to all enemies hit. And as this deals damage on the way out and on the way back, it can apply two stacks of his passive. Ekko's W has a passive where his basic attacks deal bonus damage to enemies below 30% health. Ekko's W places down a circle on the floor and after 2 seconds, enemies can see the circle. After another 1.25 25 seconds, enemies within are slowed, and if Ekko enters inside the circle, it detonates, granting himself a shield and stunning all enemies within. Ekko's E causes him to dash in a direction, which empowers his next basic attack. This empowered attack has bonus attack range and causes him to blink to the target to deal bonus damage, and this works as an auto attack reset. Ekko's ultimate has a passive where an image of himself 4 seconds where he was earlier follows him. The active causes him to enter stasis and heal himself, which scales with a portion of the damage recently received. And then he blinks back to this image following him, dealing damage in a circle around him. For combos, start by throwing out W, then using E to dash. Follow up with Q before attacking to dash onto your opponent before spamming away with more attacks. And a great combo if you're being chased is to throw down W, then ult back into it for a massive damage and stun. From here, attack into Q, then use E for more damage or to chase your opponent. For matchups, Ekko is great into junglers where he can use his ultimate to negate their burst. However, he can struggle against champions that can easily outjul him. For runes, take this. Dark harvest is great extra damage. For build order, start blue monster and pot into a Nash's tooth and tier two boots. From here, a lichbane and hat are great pickups before finishing a build with any of these as needed. For skill order, start W, then Q, then E before maxing Q, then E, then W, taking alt whenever you can. For summoner spells, take flash and smite. Starting the game, you don't have the fastest jungle clear. However, you are very reliant on levels and gold. This means your main go-to will almost always be to do a full clear into fighting for scuttle crab. However, if you do just want to clear one side of the jungle and go for the level three cheese gank, it is fairly strong as long as you land your stun. After getting scuttle, you want to look for a gank as always considering the three main points of what CC does your team bring, what escapes to the enemy have, and where is the lane push to. Your ganks are incredible if your W stun lands. However, it's difficult to make sure enemies stay within this, so it's a good idea to gank laners that bring the CC for you. Entering the mid game, it's all about looking for those pigs and getting objectives. You are very mobile and can quickly burst out targets and you're very safe at doing this as your ultimate easily gets you out of danger. Entering late game team fights, you want to play as the assassin. Lurk on the sides and throw your W to where the fight is going to start. Your E, allowing you to jump over terrain allows you easy flanks onto fights. And your entire kit is perfect for this as you can jump on a carry, blow them up and ult out.

titre: How to Stop Tilting in League of Legends (By a Former Tilter)
transcription: Have you ever had one of these days in solo Q? First game of the day, you queue up, you play out of your mind, but unfortunately you still lose. And look, your teammates, it happens. No problem. Next game, same story. You play great, but still another loss. Game three, you're winning. It's going well until you hear >> a summoner has disconnected. >> The end of the night, nine straight losses, 200 LP gone, and Tilt has completely taken over. This used to be me for years. I was very toxic. Heartstock diamond. But the moment I learned how to master tilt, I finally climbed the challenger. So, in this video, I'll show you exactly how to stop tilt from ruining your climb, and by the end, you'll know exactly what tilt actually is, what the three different types of tilt are that every player falls into, how to rewire your brain to break free from tilting, and finally, I'll share my personal anti-tilt toolkit that allows me to stay calm consistently. So, what actually is tilt? Psychologists call tilt an emotional reaction to losing control. Studies show that people get way more stressed when they think they can't influence an outcome. In League, that's when a teammates ends, you get camped, or Baron gets stolen. Tilt happens when you see something as harmful, but you believe you don't have the tools to fix it. Now if you add in a little bit of ego where your rank and your skill feels attacked plus you got loss aversion where people hate losing more than they actually feel winning. You as a natural tendency in yourself want to avoid pain rather than chase good feelings. So losses feel twice as bad as wins feel good. And then your brain just spirals out of control. So tilt isn't just anger. It's your brain stressing because it thinks your actions don't matter anymore. Now there's three main types of tilt. Number one, not having control over the outcome of the game. Of course, it's a 5v5 game. It's a team game, and there's going to be situations where your teammates don't perform, go AFK, or lose the game. You can't control every single game because it's a team game. Another big reason of tilting, expectations versus reality. And this is what I see with a lot of players in my coaching program is that they set really high, sometimes toxic expectations of themselves, leading to get very mad at themselves if they made one small mistake in the lane, exploding into tilt, ruining the game for themselves. Another one, threat to ego. A lot of players, admit it or not, right, have tied some piece of their selfworth towards their rank. And this is something I've done a lot in the past. We're going to explore all three of these topics because I've struggled with every type of tilt myself. Once again, I was a toxic [ __ ] back in the day. Feeling like you have no control over the outcome of the game is the number one reason that players tilt. And I see this constantly in my coaching program as well where I've worked with thousands of players. And the reality is it's true. You don't always have control over the outcome of the game. And this is a problem because you queue up rank with the intention I want to climb. I want to improve in rank and what happens is that you lose and you don't see the immediate rewards. Matter of fact, you actually lose LP. You feel stuck. You don't climb. You're further away from your goal every time you do it. So, this sparks an emotional reaction of course. Now, it's listed very well. You don't have control over the outcome of the game. And it's true, but the truth is that we shouldn't focus on the outcome of the game. The way you solve this is by actually not looking at the outcome, but actually looking at the inputs. And this is the link that a lot of players need to make. And I really struggled with this in the past as well. Every win or every loss would spike dopamine or stress response in my body because this was the only thing I was thinking about the LP. That's was why I queued up. I queued up because of the LP and we can't control the LP. It will always be up and down. What we can control is the process. Trust the process. The what we actually put in and this is a tough pill to swallow for a lot of people because they don't understand how to focus on that. So, everyone's focusing on the outcome because it's easy. It's the visual thing, right? You show the rank. You go up and down. You lose, you play like ass. You win, you play well. even though that might have been your best your worst game in a while. And the way that I want you guys to view this is that we want to start shifting our focus towards the input instead of the outcome. Now, easier said than done. How do we actually do that? Well, I'm going to frame my story. So, back in the day, I was incredibly toxic and I've been playing the game like six, seven years at this point and I was dying. Stuck for a couple years. And what happened is that I got invited to join in a really low-level league amateur team. And that team had a coach and when I was working with this coach, he was doing live game uh reviews with me and he would just sit in Discord. We would chat together and we would look at my game. And I remember I lost a few games in a row and I was having a really tough game like game three, four, I was having a really tough game, 40-minute banger. And what happened is that at the end my teammate made a mistake and we lost the game 40 minute back and I crashed out. I totally crashed out. I got so unbelievably mad at that game because I was fully focusing on the outcome of course and what happened is that my coach after the game he allowed me to to to wind down a little bit and he said this was the best game that I've seen you play ever. And I didn't understand that because I was so focused on the outcome. I lost. So I was disgusting. I was a piece of [ __ ] How could I lose this game? My teammates, they ran it down. They trolled me. I'm the victim. That was my mindset right here. But he said, "No, no, no. Doesn't matter. You played really well and you actually did something that I've not seen you done before." Because we had some learning goals that we were working back then. I was young. I also didn't understand what he said to me. And that review after that game was said, "Let's look at this point in your game play. What you did right here, could you have done this two weeks ago?" And I was thinking, I was like, "No, I I learned this while working with you, right? So, you've gotten better." And that's the moment when it started to click for me because improvement, everyone knows deep down that if you get better, you'll climb. But it's just not visual. So, it doesn't instantly show. And climbing a league is not linear. So what happens is that if you get better, it doesn't instantly show in rank. It takes a little bit of time. But everyone deep down knows that if you get better, you'll climb. This was my missing link on how I was starting to focus on the input instead of the outcome. And it took a little bit more practice. That was my my awakening, so to say. And what started to happen is that the more I worked with the coach, the more he actually kept me focusing on the input and the things that I was learning and improving upon. And without thinking, of course, it took some time, but a few months later, I was climbing steadily, more consistently, improving as a player and my rank climbed with it as well. And that's the mindset that I really want you guys to understand is that we need to start focusing on the things that we can learn and improve. Now, how do we actually fix the tilt part of this? Okay, because this is where a lot of people are still going to struggle when they see the loss in LP that they're still going to they know, okay, I've maybe done something better, but I'm stilling mad that I lost this game. And I totally understand that because it's normal. The way you want to frame this is you want to start rewiring your brain when it comes to how you reward yourself and also what you look at. So this LP, this outcome in the end actually doesn't matter. You have got to have set specific learning goals in game that you work towards, that you focus on. A very good example is timing your abilities with the enemy last hits. It's one of my favorite learning objectives in the early game. It's very clear to see if you're improving on that or not over a couple of days, weeks in a row because it's just going to be more consistent and your abilities are just going to look better. So compare your gameplay a few weeks later to your gameplay a few weeks before and look at the stark difference and then ask yourself the question, could I have done this a few weeks ago? And the answer will always be no. You'll always be improving. And if you do this with three, four or five things in a row, what will happen is that your rank will slowly start to creep up because you're actually becoming better and you're focusing on the input. So focus on what you can improve. Focus on what you can learn and reward yourself based upon things that you do well. If you've completed a learning goal, get yourself a pizza. I love food. I love pizza. He always said, "Celebrate your wins." That was what my coach said. And I always got food when I I did something well. So I always got myself a pizza or I went to a sandwich bar. I got myself a sandwich. And I started emotionally regulating my wins. not my game wins but my improvement wins with good things. And what started to happen is that my brain started to rewire to focus on the inputs instead of the outputs. And this solved 90% of my tilt issues. So the next root cause of tilt is having expectations versus the raw reality of league. And expectations is this big word because expectations can mean different many different things. can mean expectations upon your own level of play, expectations of your teammates, expectations if you compare yourself to others. There's many different types of expectations and there's just a few that I want to really highlight and no doubt because a lot of people especially when it comes to tilt set toxic expectations expectations that are not realistic either indeed by comparing themselves towards other players by thinking they should climb faster than that they actually should because of things that they've seen before or either that they should learn faster than they should actually shoot. There's also players in my coaching program that follow the process and they are just really mad at themselves that they're still working on a learning objective that they've gotten three weeks ago and they haven't cracked it yet. And what evidence is that you get really frustrated at yourself. It's a different type of tilt than blaming your teammates. And that's another thing to note is that there's inwards tilt and there's outwards tilt. Some people reflect and deflect tilt upon themselves and are very self- toxic. Other players are very outwards deflecting and they actually blame their teammates even though they actually feel [ __ ] about themselves. Hidden secret guys, if someone flames you, they're actually tilted at themselves because they're not happy with how they are playing mind hack here. So let's talk about some of these expectations and how you can fix that. Now first things first, right? Like I said in the previous example is that you need to compare yourself to yourself a few weeks ago. Don't compare yourself to towards others because you're going to set unrealistic expectations of yourself compared to for example running a marathon. I'm training to run half a marathon right now. And what happens is that you got to get realistic on what you're able to do. You can't just start say and say tomorrow I'm going to run the marathon. You can't do it. You got to take it step by step by step. You got to take baby steps. If you're going to attempt to run the marathon while you haven't trained, what's going to happen is you're going to completely kill your body. And you just got to take it step by step. Set a nice little goal. Okay, the first thing I'm going to work towards is this 5 km. And if I can do that consistently, we can make it seven and we can make it 10. You take it step by step by step. And then you compare yourself with yourself. So don't set unrealistic toxic goals. Set smaller goals that are achievable and this will also lower your expectations of yourself. Game changer. Now another thing um expectations of yourself in game. What I used to do is I used to really struggle when I wanted when I was actually there was 600 LP. I was a little bit later in my journey but I was very self-toxic at that point because I was a coach already and I was working on my lane and I really want to break through from 600 to challenger consistently. And what happened is that I got really mad at myself if I made one small mistake in the lane because of the fact that I'm a coach. I've I've practiced this. I preach this. I do this 247 hours per day. How comes that I'm not good enough to consistently execute a lane? How comes I'm [ __ ] this up? I'm I'm so disgusting for [ __ ] this up and my entire game got ruined. Now, why is that the case? Well, first things first, right? I set really unrealistic expectations to have it perfect every single game. And I became rigid in that way. And if something didn't play out exactly how I pre-planned it, I would completely lose my [ __ ] Now, why is that the case? Well, I had unrealistic expectations of myself. I thought I should do it perfectly and that this should go in a certain way. And if it didn't go in that way, what happened is that I got frustrated. Lower the bar for yourself, right? Lower be okay with something not going right. Matter of fact, it's good that something doesn't go right because you can learn from it every single time. It doesn't have to go perfect. Now, there's of course also some external um expectations that that some people struggle with when it comes to like their, you know, their their friends or you're in a friend group and you're the worst player from the friend group and you you push yourself so hard that you feel like you got to play with them because they're all higher elo. You got to be careful with that, guys. Once again, compare yourself with yourself. That's my number one recommendation. Really look at yourself and set realistic goals. Set realistic expectations of yourself and also once again comparing this to input versus output. Don't set expectations on output. Set expectations on input because that's the thing that you can control not the output. You can't say even though it's might be realistic. If you if you're diamond two right now and you say I want to be master in four months even though that it might be doable. What happens if you don't reach it? You're going to feel [ __ ] about yourself. You're going to feel disgusted. How am I so trash that I can't reach climb 200 LP in in four months? Am I that bad at the game? Well, it's not something that we can always control. Maybe you just had an unlucky streak. You never know. You got to set expectations based off of your inputs. That's also the way I want you to frame it. You when when you ever you set goals, don't set output goals, set input goals. So, a good way to frame a positive expectation instead of a negative expectation is um I want to become the player that can reach master. So, I'm going to play my game on really high intensity. I'm going to play my game in blocks of three, which is going to allow me to learn the fastest. I'm only going to play one or two champions mark so I can focus on champion mastery and learn the game fundamentals through those champions. That is what a master player does. And if you're going to set yourself those expectations, you can actually get to that because it's something you can control and it's not an output. Now, a final root cause is still being deeply tied to your ego. And this is something that I really used to struggle with. Back in the days when I was heartstick diamond, I my life didn't go that well. I had a lot of struggles in school. some IRL situations did not go the way I wanted to and my escape was League and what happened is that League was the thing that I was pretty good at. I was diamond break of it. It was wasn't bad, you know. And um I was the best of my friend group. And what happened is that I really got tied to this LP number. And what happened is that if I lost the game, I would feel really bad because my LP was literally tied to how good I felt about myself because this was the thing I cared about. This was the thing I put so much time and effort into. And what happened is that whenever I lost or whenever something happened, uh my ego would just go to [ __ ] I would feel bad for for for that day if I just lost two to three games more than I won and I would just feel really bad and would go to bed feeling completely disgusted at myself. A lot of players, believe it or not, have their ego tied to their identity. And what happens when you do that is that you then get into a position where you're bound for failure because your rank is an output. It's not something you can control. And we go back to this first first topic we talk about, right? is that you're you're instantly screwed when that's the case. So, what's the solution to this? Right? How what how did I fix my rank being tied to my identity? It was by basically fixing the fact that league was the only thing I cared about. And this is something that I've had with a coaching client as well. Um I'm going to name it out there. I'm sure he's going to be fine with it. Zack. Now, Zack hit me up last Christmas and he said and he dropped all the way from master to diamond form. He was in the [ __ ] and he was spamming games. Stuff in his life wasn't perfectly lining up and he said, "Tim, I really need your help, man. I I I don't know what's going I really need your help." So, we had a chat and I asked him, "What are you what in your life is currently what are you doing outside of it? Like, what other hobbies do you have?" Because he got so frustrated with every single game and he says, "Yeah, I I used to do this and this and this, but I don't really do it anymore." So the is kind of the only thing I got right now because I had these own experiences myself in the past. So I said, "Okay, what we're going to do is we're going to not play the game for the next two to three weeks and I want you to fix all your IRL issues and not really issues but get new things going in your life. Clean your clean your house. Um, pick up a different hobby and a few months later randomly midmaster out of the blue. new peak, highest he's ever done by playing less and by fixing his life. Because what happens if league is not the only thing that you do? Your ego doesn't directly translate towards your rank. There's other things that matter in your life. You are confident. You feel self-confident that you don't have to fully focus on the rank as your source of confidence. And what happens then is that you don't really care if you actually lose. You feel okay with yourself. you don't feel disgusted at yourself when that happens. And that's also how I solved it myself. I uh eventually took a break from the game um for like half a year or something like that. And what happened is that I started going out with my friends more. At that point, I also got introduced to clubbing and alcohol. But you know, there was other stuff about besides league that got my interest and I actually felt more confident in myself. When I got back to League, it it there was still some form of that present. I still didn't properly solve it, but it was already a very good step in the in the right direction. And right now, it's not any type of issue I have anymore. Even as a coach, right? My rank is my ego in a way. It's my job. What happens if I'm what happens if I can't get out a master? You know, that would be that would be shame. No, as a coach, I'm teaching you guys the game, but I'm I'm hard to master. How crazy is that? you know, that's that's that's also fine in a way. It's it's okay. It's it's a it's not who I am only anymore. I'm not only this. I also have my my life. I got a beautiful girlfriend. I got an amazing life outside of I'm very happy with myself. I got other hobbies and passions that I do. So in the end you know getting secure and building and getting rid of um in the end becoming confident in yourself is the key and your rank shouldn't directly translate to your confidence. If if you feel like that's the case quit the game for a bit and start exploring other parts of your life. So, if you're going to apply all the things I talked about and you're going to focus on the input, you set reasonable expectations, you build self-confidence outside of league, there's still going to be times where you're going to get frustrated because we're humans and we're emotional creatures. And that's okay, right? The main thing that we got to do is we got to regulate them and we got to accept the fact that these emotions are going to be present and we got to find ways to deal with them. And that's why I have the toolkit that I use for my own emotional regulation. I sometimes get frustrated in my games. I don't get super tilted, but I just get frustrated. And I found ways to deal with that. So, here are a few key things that I do. Number one, the moment I recognize I get emotional, I quit because League is a rational game and I know that if I want to win, if I'm in my emotional brain state, I can't play rationally well. So the moment I get actually emotional, I quit the game. What do I do? I go outside. I take a walk. I let myself calm down and everything is going to be just fine after that. If I just not think about anything, not get any inputs and I walk outside for an hour, everything is solved. Everything is fixed. That's me. That's how I do it. Um maybe for a lot of people, they want to go to the gym. That can be a great outlet. So, another thing that I do is that I put hard rules for myself because we humans are very smart at gaslighting ourselves into into doing things that we shouldn't do because we're emotional. It's not rational. And one of the key things that I sometimes catch myself upon is that I say to myself, I'm not emotional, but I'm actually emotional. I don't recognize it. So, one of the key things that I do is I make hard rules. And that is that if I lose three games in a row, I quit my day. Why do I do that? because I know that if I lose three games in a row, probably something wrong with me and I'm not really analyzing the situation correctly. So, three losses in a row, I instantly quit the game for that day and I come back the day after. So, three losses rule, very important one. set hard rules before you even go up in into into a game into a solo Q session because you might run into the position where you're going to gaslight yourself into still playing those nine games saying to yourself you're not build it but you actually are because once again we're all emotional now there's also tool kits that we can have for the actually in game recently with our coaching program we had a boot camp and in this boot camp I had a session with Tommy and Tommy was playing Silus and he was doing a really good job in the early game and then suddenly the game turned to [ __ ] fights everywhere and I saw he was just losing focus. So what I said to him, Tommy, I want you to try this one thing. The only thing I want you to do is try to create a man advantage. And suddenly Tommy calmed him down and the game got in control again because he was taking control because he had a reference point. So my recommendation right is if you are recognizing that you get emotional, you recognize that you get tilted, we got to snap out of it. We got to recalibrate and we got to get into a position where we have something that we can latch on to. So we focus on that other thing instead of the emotions. And what happens is that we can we get a direction point. We can start thinking rationally. We can start solving the puzzle of league. Again, this can be anything. This can be a specific question that you ask yourself. Okay, what's my win condition? Where can I create a man advantage? What is what's the next objective? Or what is my job in the game? These are key things that you can ask yourself. Another thing you can do, and this is something that we widely cover in our coaching program, is when we're behind, a lot of people get tilted. And then we have the mindset of making the enemies sweat for their LP. We're not going to give it to them free. We're going to make them sweat for it. Because the truth is is that a lot of times the enemies will make a lot of mistakes while trying out the game and we can potentially capitalize on that. That's another mindset that I sometimes give in my coaching sessions is play with the mindset of forcing the enemies to make mistakes. push out a wave, sit in a bush. Who knows? They might run into it. They might make a mistake. Especially these supports nowadays. These supports make a lot of mistakes. So, you might just be able to catch one or two of those. One of my favorite things to do is that I like to breathe. And this is one of the things that I had with with my coach like five, six years ago is that we always did breathing exercises. I didn't understand why. And it always I I didn't care about these breeding. What's this guy yapping about, man? It was literally when we were doing the the the warm-up or like for an important professional game. What happened is that we did breathing exercises. Now, I [ __ ] hated them because I didn't understand the value of it until I actually started researching why that's the case. You got two different types of breathing. You can breathe through your chest and breathe through your belly. If you breathe through your chest, you're basically in the fight or flight. You're in the go time breathing. And this creates a stress response equals building. And um you can also breathe to your belly which calms you down and calms down your nervous system. So one of the key things that I like to do if I recognize that I'm building, one of my cues is that I take a few deep breaths through my nose in my belly. And what happens is that you start calming down, start relaxing, and you could start thinking a little bit more rational. Now, of course, we can go to breeding exercises and proper breeding during the game very in depth. We can I can yap about that [ __ ] for years, but I don't think it's it's worth it right now. We're really talking about the tilting part of League. So, these are the main things that I personally do myself. And as a final thing, like as that final ledge on point, think of clear reference points that your champion want to do. If you're absolutely lost, just think, okay, how does my champion want to play out the game? Especially if you play a mage, for example, just play with the mindset of being first at the play. If you're late to the play, call it off, be first at play, and be okay with giving stuff. Just go down with a fight. And then if you're ever emotional after a game, don't instantly review. Don't instantly queue up. Take a break. Give yourself some time to reset.

titre: 3 Minute Poppy Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: Why play Poppy jungle? Well, she counters dash champions. She brings great lockdown. Her ganks are very strong and she deals surprisingly good damage. However, she is reliant on terrain to stun and some champions can easily zone her away. Poppy's passive is periodically her next basic attack is empowered to gain bonus range and deal bonus damage. After using this empowered attack, a circle is left on the floor, which Poppy can walk over to gain a shield. If the attack kills the target, it automatically comes back to Poppy, granting her the shield. However, enemy champions can walk over this first to destroy it. Poppy's Q is a rectangular skill shot which deals damage to enemies hit. It then leaves an area beneath it for 1 second which slows enemies within, and when it expires, it deals damage again to enemies within. Poppy's W has a passive where she gains a large amount of bonus armor and magic resist. And the amount she gains is doubled while below 40% health. The active grants her a large amount of bonus movement speed for 2 seconds and creates a circle around her. If any enemies within the circle try to dash, they take damage, are knocked up, grounded, and slowed. Poppy's E is a point-and-click ability which dashes her to the enemy's location. Upon reaching them, she deals damage to them and carries them along with her another short distance. And if she carries them into terrain, she deals the damage again and stuns them. Poppy's ultimate can be used in two different ways. If tapped, it briefly knocks the target up and deals damage to them. However, if held down, Poppy can charge it up, slowing herself. Upon release, it becomes a long-range rectangular skill shot, which knocks enemies back a massive distance towards their fountain and dealing damage to them. For combos, start W if needed into your E, attack, and Q. From here, attack into tapping your alt before attacking away. And if you don't have terrain nearby, W if needed into E and attack and Q. Tap alt to keep them in your Q and attack away. For matchups, Poppy is amazing against champions who rely on dashes. However, she can struggle against champions who rely more on movement speed rather than dashes to stay on her. For runes, take this. Phase rush allows you to easily stick onto your targets. For build order, start blue monster and pot into a sundered sky and tier 2 boots. From here, a dead man's and force of nature are great pickups before finishing a build with any of these as needed. For skill order, start Q, then E, then W before maxing Q, then E, then W, taking alt whenever you can. For summon the spells, take flash and smite. Starting the game, you have a few different options. The safest option is always to do a full jungle clear to make sure you're as strong as you can be before taking scuttle crab. However, Poppy's early ganks are also incredibly strong. So, clearing one side of the jungle into a cheese gank early is also very viable. When ganking, always consider the three main points of what CC does your team bring, what escapes does the enemy have, and where is the lane push to. Your main focus will be on trying to slam enemies into terrain, and otherwise making sure they can't dash away with them by timing your W. Entering the mid game, you're all about getting picks and converting into objectives. So keep up aggressive wards and look to pick off enemies in their own jungle where you have tons of terrain to slam them into and your CC chain is great enough that your team can easily follow up. Entering late game team fights, either play as the front line or the engage. When engaging, you want to use your ultimate to split up the enemy team, looking to force the other half into a poor fight. Otherwise, simply play the front line, stopping anything from getting onto your back line.

titre: 3 Minute Neeko Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: Why play Nico Mid? Well, she brings great burst damage. She can make incredibly creative plays. She has multiple AOE lockdowns and she's just tricky to play against. However, she is squishy, especially if locked down, and she is skillshot reliant, so can struggle against good mobility. Nico's passive is a collection of disguises. At will, she can turn into her ally champions, copying their basic attack and current health. Also, if she stands near non-epic monsters, minions, traps, wards, pets, or jungle plants, she then puts them in her pocket where she can transform into them. When disguised, she can use basic attacks, but anything else breaks the disguise. Nico's Q throws out a circle on the floor, which deals damage to enemies hit. If this kills an enemy or hits a champion or large monster, it blooms again to deal damage, and this can happen up to twice. Nico's W has a passive where her basic attacks generate a stack. On the third stack, all are consumed to make her next attack deal bonus damage and grant her bonus move speed. The active creates a clone of herself in a current form where they both become invisible for a short amount of time and gain a burst of movement speed. The clone lasts for 3 seconds. However, if you channel back or an emote, it will last longer. Nico's E is a straight line skill shot, which deals damage to enemies hit and roots them. If this passes through an enemy, it grows in size and its speed and route duration is increased. Nico's ultimate creates a large circle around herself, and the enemies won't be able to see the start of this if she's invisible or disguised. After a short delay, Nico then jumps up in the air, knocking enemies up and then crashing them down, dealing damage to them and stunning them. For combos, a good go-to is to start disguise into your W and alt. After your alt goes off, use your E into Q and attack away. And if starting E, use E into Q. Follow up with W into your ultimate and attack away. For matchups, she does very well into scaling or shorter range laners who she can easily bully early. And she can struggle into assassins who will get onto her and blow her up. For runes, take this. Comet is great extra damage. For build order, start ring and pot into rocket belt and tier 2 boots. After this, a storm surge and zas are great pickups before finishing a build with any of these as needed. For skill order, start Q, then E, then W before maxing Q, then E, then W, taking alt whenever you can. For summoner spells, take flash and teleport. Starting the game, you deal great damage early. And not only do your Q and E go through minions, they're also enhanced by minions. So there's nowhere in the lane, your opponents are safe from you. So whenever your opponent goes to briefly stand still to attack, throw some spells at them. When looking to trade, the most important thing is just landing your skill shots. Landing an empowered E will allow you to proc your three hit W passive as well as have your Q detonate on the target multiple times. And then once you hit level six, you now have a ton of extra damage and lock down. Otherwise, the classic Nico thing to do is to disguise yourself as a minion. This is a great way to return back to your own lane, but also gank other lanes early. If your opponent's not constantly counting the minion waves, you will catch them out. Entering the mid game, your bot lane will usually come mid, which means your golden experience is in a side lane. So, push out the side lane to keep your income high while constantly ganking your own lanes disguised as a minion or anything else. Entering team fights, you can play in two different ways. Firstly, you can play as your team's engage. Pretend to be anything else. W towards the enemy team, ultimate, rocket belt, and destroy them all, especially if you can get all your spells out, then follow up with Zas. It's a very strong engage. Otherwise, play the backline mage, spamming out spells, using alt to protect

titre: The ONLY WAVE CONTROL Guide You'll EVER NEED - League of Legends Season 11
transcription: welcome to our sixth of this year's collaborations with riot games for latin america if you want to catch these guides in spanish then be sure to check out their channel here when it comes to learning how to lane effectively most players immediately look for trading tips so that they can kill their opponents to win the matchup that's definitely useful but the best players in the world know that the most consistent way to crush your opponents is to manipulate minion waves so that all situations go in their favor in this guide we'll be going over the three different types of wave manipulation tactics that you need to know by the end of the video you'll know exactly how to execute them when to use them but most importantly when not to use these wave tactics as well everyone wants to know the best way of crushing their opponents every single game we'll definitely be covering those kinds of tips in this guide but what the best players in the world focus on more often is minimizing their mistakes these wave tactics can oftentimes come with big drawbacks so it's very important that you know them ahead of time that way things are much less likely to go wrong in your games okay so the three way manipulation tactics that you need to know are fast pushing freezing and slow pushing now if we were to rank the order on how effective each of these are fast pushing is the weakest freezing is the second strongest and slow pushing is more often than not the strongest wave tactic you can execute so let's break down their strengths and weaknesses starting with fast pushing this tactic is pretty self-explanatory in order to execute it all you have to do is push the wave as quickly as possible that's it there are two cases in which you'll want to fast push the wave the first case is when you want the wave at your opponent's tower for absolutely any reason for example the enemy talon roaming towards bot warrants a fast push so you can try to deny some farm before he gets back to lane and to also try and score a turret plate while his tower is undefended the other major reason that you'd want to fast push is to get yourself out of lane as quickly as possible by pushing the enemy wave into the tower you no longer have any reason to be in lane this is most useful for when you suspect that you're in danger for example your team just got a completely uncontested dragon on the bottom side of the map if you see that as a top laner you should probably wonder about the possibility that the enemy team was trying to make a play on you which is why they weren't in position to contest dragon therefore you'd likely want to fast push the wave to get out of lane as quickly as you can in order to avoid the potential gank coming your way okay so you quickly shove the wave into the enemy's tower what could be so bad about doing that well you try and think about it what downsides can you think of that might make fast pushing not ideal to do all the time well its greatest strength happens to also be its greatest flaw by fast pushing a wave you're generally only getting one minion wave of pressure that doesn't give you much time to do anything as an example anivia this game got to fast push a single wave uncontested into the enemy zed's tower she left lane for a moment but quickly realized that the next wave was coming in soon she almost had zero time to do anything before she had to be back in lane to catch the next wave okay so if the first flaw is that you don't have much time to do anything then the second flaw is that you're just giving your opponent free farm this way imagine a renekton versus nasus lane if all renekton did was fast push waves he'd have no time to impact the rest of the map with such small crashes not only that but he's funneling farm into his opponent uncontested fast pushing waves is incredibly bad to do when your opponent outscales you you need to win the lane and fast pushing doesn't help you do that most of the time which brings us to our second wave tactic that you need to learn freezing freezing is basically allowing your own wave to be at a disadvantage so that the enemy wave overwhelms it it's pretty easy to set up a freeze any time that your own minion wave is at a disadvantage all you have to do is last hit minions that way you're not disrupting the lead the enemy minions have over yours you can also consistently set up your own minions to be at a disadvantage by using what is known as a bounce back crash whenever you crash a wave into the enemy's tower there are two possibilities that can occur one the wave is cleared before their next incoming wave arrives what this will do is reset the lane completely into a neutral position the other possibility is what you want to go for if you're looking to achieve a freeze remember that the incoming wave on both sides are mirrored you want to crash your wave at the enemy's tower when you're sure that the enemy wave will arrive before yours is fully clear basically you just want the enemy wave to stall here a bit killing the minions at their tower after your wave dies a tower the waves will crash like this what comes next is based on the even minion rule what this means is that whenever there is an even creep wave then whichever side of the map it is on will push to the opposite side so in this case in the long run the wave pushed toward the blue side of the map since it was initially closer to the red side's tower this is the most consistent way that you can force a freeze whenever you want the even minion rule does add a layer of complexity to maintaining a freeze you'll soon come to realize that even though your minion wave is at a disadvantage compared to your opponent's wave it won't actually freeze based on what part of the lane that it's in this is because freezes generally occur on your side of the lane which means that your reinforcing wave will arrive first the added minions will overcome any initial disadvantage your wave may have been under this is a very useful visual to help you maintain your freezes when your waves are in the green area you will only need to have your wave be at a 1 minion disadvantage when the waves are fighting in the yellow area your wave should be at a 2 2-3 minion disadvantage otherwise the even minion rule will break the freeze and in the red area your wave should be at least at a 3 to 4 minion disadvantage that may seem overly complicated but it really isn't once you understand it and start applying it in your games initiating and maintaining the freeze will become second nature to you and as a quick reminder if you want to improve fast and get the rank you've always wanted then check out our hyper improvement system at skillcap.com we have professional courses by top players smurf commentaries where a challenger player walks you through how to climb out of every rank from iron to diamond and we upload tons of new exclusive guides to our website each week in fact we're so confident you'll improve using our system that if you don't climb at least five divisions while actively using skill cap you can claim a full refund so there's no risk what are you waiting for check out skillcap.com and get the rank you've always wanted link in the description below what's next though is understanding when you should actually attempt to freeze so before starting a freeze you should always ask yourself is there anywhere else i need to be on the map by freezing waves you're committing to sitting in a lane for a very long time as an example let's say you're a top laner with half health and mana and 2 000 gold in your inventory that is very likely not a time that you should be freezing as you should definitely be looking to visit the shop in the near future or another example let's say the dragon is up as the adc you probably shouldn't be freezing by doing so you're hindering your ability to rotate to the objective if the enemy team begins to threaten it if you've got nowhere to be though then there will be two situations that you'll want to freeze away for number one if you for whatever reason want the wave closer to your side of the lane more often than not this decision revolves around junglers let's say that your jungler is on the bottom side of the map and you suspect the enemy jungler is topside then you may want to be freezing that way it's much harder to gank you and you can remain safe while your team gets something done on the bot side the opposite is just as good a reason to do it as well if you think that the enemy jungler is on the other side of the map and yours is close to your lane then initiating a freeze could be a great way to set up a gank onto your opponents the second and main reason that you want to freeze a wave is when you are comfortably stronger than your opponent and you want to deny them farm sometimes pushing waves isn't the best way to get leads if there's nothing else that you can do on the map then the best thing for you to do to snowball your lead is to freeze versus your opponent normally the player with more minions wins trades that's because fighting in a minion wave will draw agro and during the early stages of the game minions do a ton of damage thus you usually can't zone your opponents when you have a minion deficit but if you have a sure lead you may be able to potentially break that rule the most brutal thing that you can do to your opponent is to freeze and deny them the ability to walk up and farm depending on how far ahead you are you can potentially deny them a huge amount of gold or if you're incredibly ahead you can even go as far as denying your opponent experience entirely because your wave is at a disadvantage the wave will never push toward your opponent if you maintain it properly that is which means you can technically zone someone from gold and experience indefinitely the only way your opponent can break out of this situation would be to call their jungler to come and help them break the freeze with the numbers advantage against you but even if they do that you've still earned a massive lead over them number one they'd be sharing gold and experience as they break the freeze and number two you'd be giving your teammates critical information as to where the enemy jungler is freezing is absolutely brutal when you can pull it off in fact it technically should be the strongest wave tactic in the game in theory with a proper freeze there should be nothing that your opponent can do to stop you unfortunately freezing does come with some pretty hefty downsides which prevent it from being as strong as it should be the biggest one being that your opponent is completely free from being in lane yes they may not be able to walk up to the wave and last hit minions but that means that they can roam around the map since you're not pushing waves into their tower their ability to leave lane for free isn't the problem though it's really your teammates think about things from your allies perspective let's say you're a zed versus a talon in midlane that's a pretty fun matchup and both players are looking to fight and outplay each other to score a kill just as you're hitting six and the lane is becoming even more interesting you notice your bot lane is freezing if zed is smart he will just play safe at his tower his bot lane is getting ahead as long as he doesn't lose the game should be won because of how many resources his own bot lane is tonight but that's not why people queue up to play league of legends that zed wants to have fun and he wants to be the carry he wants to be the one with 12 kills who everyone honors at the end of the game so he'll go in he'll die to the rome and then he'll blame his bot lane for freezing so you have to really think about whether the freeze is worth it before initiating one consider how well your lane opponent can roam around the map if you deem them to not be a threat then it's totally okay to freeze but if they have even the slightest kill pressure on your teammates then a freeze might be too risky to implement since your allies will likely die to their roams here's a decent guideline for when we'd advise you to stop freezing in a game for bot laners stop freezing when your lane opponents are both level 6. a freeze is not worth the risk of two champions with two ultimates roaming around the map and killing your team for mid stop freezing waves at around level 7. at that point most mid laners have long range reliable wave clear anyway it's almost impossible to maintain a freeze in mid after level 7 anyway not only that but once again a mid laner with their ultimate and quick access to the entire map is too risky to be left alone roaming as for top laners you can probably freeze waves all the way up until level 9 or 10. top is so isolated from the rest of the map that it's generally not that scary to allow your lane opponents to roam of course these are just general guidelines it's on you to adapt to your specific game that you're in for example if you're playing against a jinx and a yumi bot lane then it's totally fine to freeze past level six they're not going to have effective roams anyway so who cares if they leave lane or in top if you're against a quinn then you may just want to never freeze a wave ever again after she gets level 6. when she's free to do what she wants quinn will roam around the map killing your whole team regardless of how much you ping her so you really want to be pressuring her tower as much as you can and trying to force her to stay in lane alright on to our final and strongest wave tactic slow pushing this is by far the most utilized wave tactic by high elo players as they all understand how powerful it is all it is is pushing the minion wave slowly then when it's big enough you crash it your end goal is just to create as big of a crash as possible at your opponent's tower the advantages of a slow push are really easy to understand slow pushing is literally just fast pushing but better the problem we had with fast pushing was that a single wave at the enemy tower didn't give you much time to do anything slow pushing fixes that by having a big wave at your opponent's tower you're going to have a lot more time to do things this can be whatever you want for example one of the most common uses of slow pushing is to just recall after the crash because it takes your opponent so long to clear the wave you're able to get back in time before they can crash the wave to your tower oh and remember what we said about bounce backs after a crash if the wave stalls at the tower after you crash a slow push then you can force a freeze by coming back to lane with an item advantage sion doesn't want the wave frozen against him so he tries to contest the irelia unfortunately for him with her item advantage she easily disposes of him this kill was scored not through mechanics but from irelia's previous slow push into the threat of a freeze this was a perfect example of how to use waves to crush your lane opponent the other most devastating tactic off of a slow push is setting up a tower dive on your opponent killing someone after crashing a big wave into their tower is incredibly punishing number one you've scored a kill number two you're denying your opponent a ton of farm while they're respawning and number three you have a massive wave to help you take turret plates to snowball even further ahead coordinating a slow push while the jungler is on your side of the map and pinging them to dive is one of the most abused high elo wave tactics by far you can literally win the game instantly if you manage to pull it off sure in lower elos your junglers may not listen to your call but it's still good to ask in case they do pay attention but as we said you can do anything you want off of a slow push you can roam to another lane invade the enemy jungle set up vision etc anything you attempt to do is far more likely to work out by crashing a decently sized wave at their tower your opponent will be forced to stay there giving you more time for your play to succeed and that's why slow pushing is the strongest wave tactic of the three you get great advantages with very little downside and the one downside it does have is easy to play around the only way a slow push could turn out poorly for you is if you mess up during it it's very important to know that the only difference between a slow push and a freeze is who is in control of the wave for example in this case the tf has a slow push going for him since he's even in health with syndra and he has the wave advantage but let's imagine that syndra randomly landed a crazy good trade onto the twisted fate with him being at such low health and unable to contest the wave anymore his slow push has just become syndra's freeze as we said earlier freezes can be quite devastating so this is a complete disaster for tf so it's important to remember that the advantage of a slow push is all about that crash you don't have to trade with your opponent while you're crashing a big wave if you don't need to it's more important to make sure that you actually crash the wave to ensure your advantage to wrap things up we want to leave you with a clear message you don't always have to use the most optimal wave strategy for every single scenario it's impossible to always do the best play possible every single time the most important thing to focus on is making sure that you don't use these tactics at the wrong time or mess them up either fast pushing and giving a scaling opponent free farm freezing versus a powerful roamer or dying while you're slow pushing can all be game losing mistakes hopefully by knowing them ahead of time you're much less likely to commit those mistakes and make full use of the power of all three strategies and with that we'll end things here hope you enjoyed thanks for watching and we'll see you next time

titre: 3 Minute Qiyana Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: Why play Kiana jungle? Well, she has amazing mobility. Her ganks are surprisingly strong. She has ridiculous outplay potential, and she's a strong late game team fighter. However, she is squishy, especially if locked down, and her initial clears are a little on the slow side. Kiana's passive are her basic attacks and abilities deal bonus damage, but she can only apply this once every 25 seconds to each target. However, the cool down is reset on all targets when she gains a new element. Kiana's Q is a rectangular skill shot, which deals damage to the target hit. Gathering an element resets this cooldown and upgrades the ability. If upgraded with terrain, she deals bonus execute damage. If upgraded with river, the ability roots and then slows. And if upgraded with rush, it creates an area which she can go invisible in and gain bonus move speed. Kiana's W has a passive where when she's holding an element, she gains bonus attack speed, bonus attack range, and bonus damage on hit. And while out of combat, she moves quicker towards that element. The active causes her to dash towards either brush, river, or terrain. And whichever one she chooses, she gets the element of. Kiana's E is a point-and-click ability which causes her to dash a fixed distance in the direction of the target enemy and if she hits them, she deals damage to them. Kiana's ultimate is a rectangular skill shot which knocks back enemies hit and stops when it hits terrain. If it crosses through any river or brush, it creates a cascading shock wave. However, hitting terrain is the real goal where it then sends a shock wave all the way around the terrain. All enemies hit by the shock wave, whether they are pushed into it or hit later, take massive damage and are stunned. For combos, a good go-to is to E into attack and Q. Grab another element with W into a Q and then attack away. And your all-in combo is to use E into ultimate and attack. Q into W and then Q again before attacking away. For matchups, Kiana is great into junglers where she can simply go invisible and constantly outplay them. And she can struggle into junglers who have a faster early clear so you can do more around the map than she can or ones who can reveal her while she's invisible. For runes, take this. Electrocute makes your burst damage incredible. For build order, start blue monster and pot into a ghost blade and tier 2 boots. From here, an opportunity and grudge are great pickups before finishing a build with any of these as needed. For skill order, start W then Q then E before maxing Q, then W then E, taking alt whenever you can. For summoner spells, take flash and smite. Starting the game, you almost always want to go for a full jungle clear. With your first clear being a little on the slow side, it just means any early gank will punish you even harder. However, once you've done your first clear and contested scuttle crab, you can go for a gank or simply back. When ganking, always consider the three main points of what CC does your team bring, what escapes does the enemy have, and where is the lane pushed to. With the river terrain and the fact you have two dashes, your ganks surprisingly strong. So, if you can ever get onto your opponent and use both your Q's, which of course resets your passive, it's just an insane amount of burst. And once you hit level six, throwing some into terrain will simply destroy them. Entering the mid game, play a pick and objective play style. Use your massive burst damage combined with the ultimate to quickly remove enemies from the map and convert into objectives. Entering late game team fights, your main goal is to land a massive ultimate. Fighting in the jungle or around objectives is a great way to do this. Throw a couple of enemies into terrain and watch as their health evaporates. From here, brush element keeps you very safe. So go in with E and use Q after Q for massive damage while staying invisible.

titre: Want to Climb in League of Legends? Do This.
transcription: I probably shouldn't be teaching you this because I am a coach, but if you want to climb a league, you don't need coaching. There's a simple trick that works better than any guide or coaching program, and it's called the single fundamental rule, and it's incredibly effective. Most players think climbing a league means improving everything all at once. They try to improve their laning, their jungle tracking, and their mid game all at once. And because they're trying to improve everything, they end up improving nothing. If you want to climb, follow what I call the single fundamental rule. Pick one fundamental like wave management and all-in on that. Pregame, your only job is to think about what the wave should look like regarding the matchup and the game state. During the game, you only focus on that. If you miss the jungler walking over ward and you get ganked, fine, no problem at all. All we care about is wave management. Postgame, you only review your wave management as well. If you die, ask yourself, "Was my wave in the right spot?" If you take a bad trade, ask, "Was my wave in the correct position?" If the lane feels hard, ask, "Did I manage my wave properly?" Nothing else matters. Here's why this works. League of Legends is a game of habits. It's a game of pattern recognition. 99% of what you do in a fight isn't conscious thinking, it's automatic execution. So, the real question is, how do you build a habit? Your brain's lazy, it wants efficiency, and it doesn't like creating new connections unless it absolutely has to. If you overload it with five different improvement goals all at once, it doesn't know what to prioritize. But if you focus on one specific thing with intention, something different happens. You go into the game knowing exactly what you're training. You review only that. You visualize what you could have done better. Now, the brain has a clear signal, and when the signal is clear, the connections form faster. That's how you turn knowledge into habits. Last week, an Emerald Zener player joined my coaching program and got a coaching session. He told me, "I've tried multiple coaches. They all gave me stuff to work on laning, mid game, macro, positioning. I've worked on it all, but it doesn't work. I'm still stuck." We reviewed his fault together, and those coaches were right. There were five or six things he could work on immediately. Laning mistakes, positioning errors, sloppy ability usage. I could have given him a list, but instead, I told him this. For the next three to four weeks, I only want you to focus on your Syndra E usage in fights. And this is exactly how we're going to do that. In the review, I want you to pick out two fights that felt awkward. I want you to go back into the fight and review it with the mindset as if you have your E up permanently. Now, in this fight, there will be five, six, seven opportunities to use your E. And from all of these opportunities, I want you to pick the best one. This is going to allow you to visualize what the best time is to use your Syndra E in the fight, and that way you can build pattern recognition. Now, if you do this two times 30 games per week, which is 60 reps in a week times three, we're already at 180 reps. At that point, of course your brain's going to build a habit. It's impossible to fail. At the end of the session, he felt a bit confused and said, "Is that it?" And I said, "Yep, that's it." A few days later, he messaged me saying, "Tim, I didn't know improvement could go so fast. I feel so much more confident with my E's." Nothing magical happened. We just removed all of the noise, allowing him to improve. And this works at every rank. Before I made my first climb to Challenger a few years ago, I was stuck at high master for a long time, and I was struggling hard. And what I realized is that my champion pool allowed me to skip some fundamentals. I was playing Anivia back in the day, so I made a decision. I dropped her and I went to Viktor, which is one of the most sound champs in the game, which forced me to learn the fundamentals properly. I dropped all the way back to Diamond II, and instead of trying to improve everything all at once, I focused on one fundamental at a time. Two or three weeks per fundamental, full focus, and then move on to the next one. I did that consistently for a few months, and I climbed from D II to Challenger for the first time ever. Not because I learned more, but because I removed the noise and built habits properly. Improvement isn't about adding more to your gameplay, it's about removing the noise and focusing on what actually matters. You don't climb by overwhelming yourself. You climb by isolating one fundamental, mastering it before moving on to the next. That's how you build habits that wins games. And if you're unsure which fundamental to start with, check out this video right here where I do a full deep dive on the fundamentals and how to investigate which one is your problem.

titre: 3 Minute Alistar Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: Why play Alistister support? Well, he has amazing engage. He's great at protecting his carry. He is a beast at roaming. And he is stupidly tanky. However, he does have long cooldowns to play around and he's very all-in with little escape. Alistar's passive is he gains a stack every time a nearby enemy minion or monster dies. And whenever he stuns or displaces an enemy with an ability at seven stacks, he consumes them all to heal himself and nearby ally champions. Alistar's Q deals damage in a circle underneath him, stunning enemies and knocking them up. Alistister's W is a point-and-click ability which dashes him to an enemy champion, dealing damage to them, knocking them back, and stunning them. This can be combined with his Q while he's dashing, where upon hitting the enemy champion, he'll instantly cast the Q to deal damage to them and knock them up instead. Alistister's E causes him to constantly deal damage in a circle around him. And every time this hits at least one enemy champion, he generates a stack up to five times. At five stacks, his next basic attack is empowered to consume these stacks, deal bonus damage, and stun the target. Alistister's ultimate cleanses himself of all crowd control effects and massively reduces the damage taken for the next 7 seconds. Especially if this ability is used early, he is basically just unkillable. For combos, start with W and press Q while dashing to immediately use it upon arrival. Use E, then attack away until you can stun your opponent. If your opponent near terrain, use E, then go in with W to hit them into the terrain. Follow up with Q to knock them up, then attack them to stun them for a stupidly long CC chain. For matchups, Alli's great into engage support as he's happy to be engaged on and wants to be close to his target. However, he can struggle against champions with strong disengage, especially if they can interrupt his headbutt pulverized combo. For runes, take this. Aftershock makes your engages so much stronger and safer. For build order, start world atlas and pot into bandal pipes, tier 2 boots, and upgrade to a celestial opposition. From here, a locket and zeke are great pickups before finishing a build with any of these as needed. For skill order, start Q then W then E before maxing Q then W then E, taking alt whenever you can. For summoner spells, take flash and ignite. For synergies, Alli is amazing with skill shot reliant champions as you can CC enemies for such a long time. Starting the game, your main focus is going to be on protecting your carry and threatening allins on your opponent. Against another mele support, you really do shine as if they ever try to engage on you, all of your abilities will CC them. And if they ever do this too close to your tower, you can simply flash, knock them up, and headbutt them under it. Otherwise, if you're against a poke lane, your objective is to survive and secure your ADC as much farm as possible until you can threaten allins. In these matchups, you'll often get pushed in anyway. So, buy early boots to avoid skill shots and try to be as healthy as you can be. So, if your jungler does come, you're in a good position to take the fight. And especially in these lanes, brush control is even more important as not only does it keep you safe, but it enables your hex flashes to allins. Entering the mid game, take your tower as soon as possible and rotate mid with your carry. You love this stage in the game, running around the map with your jungler, looking to make picks and plays. Entering late game team fights, either play as your teams engage or protect your backline. When engaging, if something valuable overextends, simply headbutt and pulverize them. Your ultimate keeps you incredibly safe while you're right in all of their faces. Otherwise, if your team's the one being engaged on, sit back with your carry. Position yourself to block abilities and knock enemies

titre: Viego Champion Spotlight | Gameplay - League of Legends
transcription:
Once the young, rash king of an ancient realm,
Viego went mad upon the death of his wife. To bring her back, he’ll stop at nothing—even
if he destroys everything. Now a powerful wraith, Viego can possess the
wills and skills of his adversaries. He’s a cruel skirmishing assassin, and no foe is
safe from his merciless blade. Witness the wrath of The Ruined King. Welcome
to the Viego Champion Spotlight. Viego’s passive, Sovereign Domain, allows
him to claim champions who fall to his blade. Upon a kill or assist, he can possess them,
healing for a percentage of their max health. Possessing a champion allows Viego to become
them for 10 seconds, replicating all of their non-ultimate abilities, attacks, and item
passives. This is Viego’s bread and butter: use your
passive to turn the enemy team’s strengths against them.  Master yourself, master the
enemy. The more you know about your opponents, the
better you can best them. Imitation is the most dangerous form of flattery. Blade of the Ruined King is no longer just
an item. It’s Viego’s Q, and it comes with an active and two passives. His first passive makes every basic attack
deal a small percentage of his target’s current health as bonus damage. Déjà vu. For the active, he stabs forward with his
sword, dealing damage. Damaging an enemy with any spell triggers
the Q’s second passive, which makes his next auto attack strike twice. The second
strike deals significant bonus damage and heals Viego, so be sure to take a STAB at
using your blade for sustain. This passive also works while possessing an enemy champion. Viego’s W is Spectral Maw. Charge this skill
shot to dash forward and hurl a projectile that damages and stuns the first enemy hit.
Just be careful, because you’re charging in whether you hit or you… Mist. His dash range is constant, but the longer
you charge, the longer you stun. Keep in mind, Viego can’t use this to scale
terrain. Looks like he’s hit a wall. With his E, Harrowed Path, Viego fires an
orb that creates a trail of Black Mist where he can hide. Harrowed Path haunts the first wall it hits, covering the area in Mist for about eight seconds. When he’s camouflaged by Mist, Viego gains Movement and Attack Speed. Gotta go fast (and spooky). Use it to spread your dark domain all across
the Rift. But watch out—if your enemies spot the cloud, they might just track your
location. Viego’s ultimate is Heartbreaker. Abandoning
any Possessed souls, Viego teleports to a nearby location, attacking the enemy champion
with the lowest health, applying on-hit effects like his Q passive, and slowing them.  Nearby enemies will also be knocked back, taking damage as well. You can use your ult as a gap closer to teleport
and isolate your target. Call it a hit and run. It’s also a ghastly finisher. The lower
your target’s health, the more Heartbreaker hurts. After learning it, Heartbreaker is always
available to be cast while possessing an enemy champion. Use it to exit your host early and
shift between bodies, controlling the fate of the fight. Playing the Ruined King requires a bit of
patience. Use Harrowed Path away from walls in the early game to keep enemies guessing
while you farm the jungle. Once you’ve hit your stride with your item spikes, it’s
time to look for those big plays. Your Q’s double hit passive has no internal
cooldown, so use your abilities mixed with auto attacks to maximize the damage. This combo lets Viego really burst, giving him the edge in a fight. Stay hidden in the Mist until you’re ready
to blitz, borrowing bodies as you take them down, one by one. When you’re done, just
ghost them. As an assassin, Viego isn’t great when behind,
but if he has a head start, he can conquer the Rift, quickly turning objectives and teamfights
to his favor. Looking to bring his reign to an end? Be aggressive
early game to put this king in his place. Remember, Viego is a conqueror at heart. If you let him collect items, it’ll be a Mist opportunity to keep him contained. When you’re ready to fight, don’t just
jump in. Unlike other melee-assassins, Viego’s only escape is his ultimate, which isn’t
exactly ideal. That crown’s looking a little heavy. You’ll need to build your royal guard with
tanky teammates, then seize your opening. Long live the king. Wreak havoc upon the Rift with the original
heartbreaker. Ready for an era of Ruination? Just Vie-go
to the links below.

titre: 3 Minute Aatrox Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: Why play Hrox jungle? Well, he has a solid healthy clear. He brings massive damage. He's very strong early and mid, and his full combo brings a ton of lockdown. However, he can get kited by champions with good CC, and he is reliant on landing skill shots to pull off his whole combo. Atrox's passive is periodically he empowers his next basic attack to deal bonus damage and heal him. And whenever he hits at least one enemy champion or large monster with a basic attack or ability, it lowers the cool down. And it lowers it even further if he hits the sweet spot on his Q. Atrox's Q has three different activations. All three activations will deal damage to the enemy hit and each have their own unique sweet spot. Enemies hit by the sweet spot take a large amount of bonus damage and are knocked up. And the second activation has 25% bonus damage and the third 50% bonus. The first activation is a rectangular skill shot with a sweet spot on the farthest edge. The second is more of a cone skill shot with a sweet spot also at the edge. And the third is a circular skill shot with a sweet spot in the middle. Atrox's W is a straight line skill shot which deals damage to the first enemy hit and slows them. If this hits an enemy champion or large monster, it forms a tether between them and the floor. They then have to escape an area or they're dealt the same damage again and pulled back to the center. Arox's E has a passive where he heals for a portion of the damage dealt to enemy champions. The active is an auto attack reset which dashes him in a direction. However, he can use any of his other abilities while he dashes. Atrox's ultimate grants himself a large buff for 10 seconds and fears nearby minions and monsters. Upon use, he gains a burst of movement speed as well as bonus attack damage size is ghosted and increased self-healing. And whenever he gets a champion takedown, he extends the duration by 5 seconds. For combos, start alt if needed into W, Q, and E. Attack into Q before attacking into Q again and attacking away. And you can start with E and Q into W. From here, Q again before attacking into your final Q and attacking away. For matchups, Aatrox is great into tanky champions or those who tend to be strong early as he outdoes them. However, he can struggle against pure dualists. For runes, conqueror is always a solid go-to. For build order, start blue monster and pot into a cycllo sword and tier two boots. From here, a sununded sky and shojins are great pickups before finishing a build with any of these as needed. For skill order, start Q, then E, then W before maxing Q, then E, then W, taking alt whenever you can. For summoner spells, take flash and smite. Starting the game, you almost always want to do a full jungle clear. Your half clear into a gank is still very strong, but you clear so quickly, you might as well. After getting scuttle crab, you are a strong early game jungler, so you do want to look for these fights. When ganking, consider the three main points of what CC does your team bring, what escapes does the enemy have, and where is the lane push to. You're all about making sure you can land that W. So, any low mobility is often a good target. And your level six is brilliant with some champions not being able to escape from you. Entering the mid game, keep playing aggressive and looking for picks. Whether this is going for ganks or invading your enemies jungle, you want to look for these kills and then, of course, convert them into objectives. Entering late game team fights, you're almost always playing a front-toback play style. Just keep attacking anything in front of you, life steal tanking as much as you can, and then as soon as you get any chance to land any ability on the backline, go for it. And that's how to play Hatrox Jungle. Leave the video a like and comment below who you want to see
