titre: How to Choose Your MAIN Champion! - Beginner's League of Legends Guide
transcription: there are over 160 champions in league of legends which makes it extremely difficult to choose who you should main in this video our goal is to help combat that and narrow things down to a few champions so that you don't need to play every single champion to find your main this way you can start playing who you really enjoy faster and begin climbing at a quicker pace by being consistent on those picks but before we get into it be sure to check out skill cap if you want to truly get better at league of legends we're the only service that offers a money-back guarantee if you don't climb at least five divisions while actively using our service we do this because our service really does work and if it doesn't work for you you shouldn't pay learn more at the end of this video or click the link in the description below do you enjoy dealing a ton of consistent damage and being a force in the 1v1 if so choosing a champion from the fighter or bruiser class is what we'd recommend to make things even easier for you we'll place these fighters into a few different categories this first category of fighters are ones who can exert a ton of pressure throughout the earlier stages of the game if you like being aggressive right from the jump or being able to punish your opponent's mistakes then playing someone like renekton darius aurelia or rengar is a great idea if you'd rather play a champion who scales super well into being a menace in the side lane then here are a bunch of picks to try out you can still win early with many of these champs but with a couple of items is where they can really shine what if you don't really care as much for the split push power and would rather have a stronger team fight or ability to start fights someone like cled wukong or gangplank are all great options this isn't to say these champions can't be strong in the split it's just they offer more team fight presence than the champs we had in the split push category therefore if you still want the option to split push but also want to be able to team fight well choosing from this category is a great idea if you prefer a champion who can team fight extremely well but doesn't offer very strong side lane power consider maining a tank these tanks have a ton of crowd control and build super durable so you're able to start fights very easily and be the go button for your team this next category is more of a mix between a tank and a fighter the champions in this category are generally a little stronger than the tanks when put into the side lane it's really the champions who can offer a little bit of everything as someone like shen can split push has good teamfight presence with ult and builds very durable and finally we have the ranged top lane champions who every fighter main absolutely despises these are the champs who have the potential to be most dominant in the early game and can snowball harder than most since the majority of champions played top lane are melee you have a pretty big edge up in most games one of the downsides to range tops though is that they are super volatile meaning if you fall behind you're going to be way less useful than fighters or tanks we've divided range top laners into two different subcategories being mages and ad champs mages are a little less risky than the ad champs as they can scale better without items due to their cc 80 range tops on the other hand are super feast or famine as it's really only damage they can provide for their team moving on to the jungle role let's start off by running down the skirmishing fighters for junglers who like being able to duel their opponent in the early game and excel in any fights that break out these are your options if you don't want to rely on the early game as much and would prefer to come online much harder later on than these are the fighters to choose from hard farming and playing more of a pve style until you can reach item or level break points is what you'll be doing on these champs the last category of fighters are ones who are not the most optimal for early skirmishes but have much stronger gank power to compensate if you don't care about invading the enemy jungler or fighting over every crab and instead want to snowball your lanes by spam ganking look into any of these as for tank junglers we'll split them into two categories to make things really simple for you tank junglers are going to dominate come team fights and have excellent engage power if you want someone who can also gank well early on though look into nunu or rammus these next couple of tanks don't have the same kind of early gank potential but they make up for that with even more potent team fight and engage power you can think of them as the better scaling tanks we're putting diana in this category but she could honestly have her own as she's super unique in the sense she can get really tanky team fight well but also has amazing skirmish it's no wonder she's one of the highest playrate junglers right now next up we have assassins so if you enjoy being able to one-shot your opponents with heavy burst potential there's a bunch of options for more early game impact assassins junglers like rengar shaco or nidalee are going to be your best bets assassin junglers who are more reliant on farming levels and hitting item spikes but don't fall off as hard later in the game include the likes of ekko and kha'zix the vast majority of junglers are melee so if you enjoy range champs you can narrow it down quite easily first we have the ap range junglers and if you like playing an early game pick then talia is really your only choice as for scaling ap junglers fiddle lilia and karthus are who you have to choose from for more of a supportive ap jungler ivorn is the only option the ranged ad junglers are graves kindred and if you want to throw a twitch in there you can kindred plays more like a traditional adc than graves does though so it makes it very easy to choose between the two even though graves is ranged he thrives the closer he is to his opponents so it's definitely a very unique play style transitioning over to mid lane there are so many different champs you can bring to the role many of the fighter champions you see played top can actually have great success in mid as well playing a fighter mid can beat out the majority of assassins early on and will provide you with great skirmish and an overall better potential to impact the game than from top mages make up the majority of mid lane champions but we can sort them into subclasses to make it easier to choose from in general mages are very spell reliant are quite vulnerable but can have instant impact with proper spell usage majority of mages like artillery are extremely skill shot reliant so they require more skill expression than the average champ if you like being able to sit back from afar and rain down with heavy damage from spells then ziggs velkas or xerath are your options this next category will feature the burst mages who are champions you will want to choose from if you like being able to insta gib your opponent with one rotation of spells this comes at a cost though as once you've used your rotation of spells burst mages generally have longer cooldowns they have to wait out the battle mages are champions who thrive in extended fights and have stronger consistent dps than burst mages generally the cooldowns of battle mage's spells are much shorter than that of burst mages if you want to be able to one-shot your opponent but don't want to deal with the lower mobility of mages assassins are the way to go roaming and map impact can also be more consistent with assassins which is a big reason why we see most assassins with better win rates than mages in solo queue for more early game impact these are the assassins you should look into it's not to say that these assassins can't win early on but they are much stronger the later games go for example kassadin can win a bunch of his early matchups but his mid to late game is astronomically better most of the scaling assassins also struggle with getting early priority which hurts their ability to roam and impact the map you can bring adc's into the mid lane but generally their success rate in solo queue is quite poor with the exception of action with that said if you enjoy playing adc but would rather be in the center of the map with more impact on other lanes than maining an adc mid is not a bad idea for the bot lane carries we will divide them into four different categories the first will consist of stronger skirmishing or dueling adcs if you like being able to outplay your opponent with flashy plays then choosing one of these is a good idea if you don't care so much about the mechanics of adc and winning out in 1v1s siding with a stronger teamfight champ may be in your best interest every single one of these champions lack a gap closer but to make up for it they provide incredible team fight and majority scale better than the skirmishing adcs the last adc category consists of the catch or pick marksmen these champs excel at making plays happen from longer range and can reliably start fights by themselves if you're tired of your support never going in or your teammates missing out on free engages choosing from these three will suit you well and the final set of champions you can choose from for the bot lane are ap carries are your mechanics absolutely awful but you still want to play bot is your team in need of magic damage after they've locked full ad very few players main mages bot lane but they are insanely underrated solo q picks so if you like shaking things up and going against what everyone else does you can't go wrong with playing a mage bot after all seraphine does hold the highest win rate for any bot lane carry right now for support let's start off by talking about the melee options you have melee picker and gauge supports are going to be up your alley if you like being the one who's making all the plays these champions have really strong crowd control and lockdown which allows them to catch enemies titre: 3 Minute Ekko Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: why play Echo jungle well he brings great ganks he has high burst damage he has very good objective control and he has insane Mobility however if locked down he is much squishier and able to Al and he is weaker early needing time and items to scale ekko's passive is his basic attacks and abilities apply a stack to the Target upon applying the third stack all Stacks consumed to deal bonus damage and if this was used against an enemy Champion Eko then gains a massive burst of movement speed echo's Q is a StraightLine skill shot which upon hitting the end of its range or hitting an enemy becomes a slower wider skill shot this deals damage too and slows all enemies it hits afterwards this comes back to Ekko dealing damage to All Enemies hit ekko's W has a passive where his basic attacks still bonus execute damage to targets below 30% Health ekko's W places a circle on the floor after 2 seconds enemies will be able to see this and after a short time longer it will deploy slowing all enemies within if Ekko enters the circle he gains a shield and stuns all enemies within echo's e causes him to dash in a direction and empowers his next basic attack the empowered attack causes him to Blink to the Target and deal bonus damage this ability can also be used as an auto attack reset and he can use any of his abilities during the dash Echo ultimate has a passive where an after image of him follows him as long as the ability is available to use the active causes him to enter stasis and gain a heal before dashing back to the image upon arriving Back At His Image he deals damage in a circle around him for combos start by throwing out W and then use e to Dash and attack your opponent follow up with q and then spam away with attacks and a great combo if you're being chased is to throw down w and then ult back into it for massive damage and the stun from here Alo attack into q and then use the E for more damage or to chase down your opponent for matchups Echo is great into junglers who he can easily Dodge their skill shots or Al away from their burst and he can struggle against Champions that bring hard Cc or can easily outd him for runes take this dark Harvest makes your scaling and burst even better for build order start red monster and pot into a lich Bane and tier 2 boots after this a Nash's tooth and hat are great pickups before finishing a build with any of these as needed for skill order start W then Q then e perform maxing Q then e w taking alt whenever you can for Summoner spells take Flash and smite starting the game you don't have the fastest clear but you do still have a few options clearing one side of the Jungle to get level three into a gank is a great go-to you bring a very long stun a good slow and two dashes to easily get on your opponent lock them down and blow them up otherwise you can simply do a full jungle clear to fight for Scuttle when gank King Landing your W is going to be the most important thing so predict what your opponents can Retreat to and cut them off and this works even better if you compare it up with a teammate who brings good CC entering the mid game as the jungle your main focus is going to be on objectives bring great burst damage so between farming keep hunting for isolated targets blow them up and convert into objectives entering late game team fights play as the Assassin role wait for the fight to start and then throw your W over their entire team this is a great slow and also allows you to dash onto one of their carries stunning them and if you spend over 4 seconds within the enemy team you can even ultimate on the spot to deal massive damage and that's how to play Echo jungle if this video helped you leave it a like and comment below who you want to see next

titre: 5 Reasons Why YOU SUCK ON ADC! - ADC Guide
transcription: Hello everyone, and welcome back to ADC Diff. This is Violet for those who don't know who I am. I'm a former professional player 5 years and I've competed at Worlds and International multiple times. I've also hit Challenger in Korea and NA Elo's. This video is going to be a video where I talk about the five reasons why you suck at ADC and you can't climb consistently on this role. Um, I'm pretty much just going to be talking about what I think are the most important things that you should be doing and a lot of the mistakes that I see a lot of low Elo players that I coach make. So hopefully you guys are able to apply some of these concepts and fundamentals to your game and check out the coaching in the description down below or at a Patreon be interested, but I hope you guys enjoy the video. The first reason to why you're losing games is because you're always greeting your bases and not recalling at the correct time. It's a mistake that I see a lot of lower Elo players do is that whenever they win a fight or they end up crashing a wave, they always try to get that extra plate or that extra wave or that extra gold that they see right in front of the stream. But it's very, very important that whenever you win a fight, you actually spend your gold, otherwise you haven't you don't actually have an advantage, right? Um, because as I said, if you overstay on the map, for example, one, your condition is probably not the best, right? And two, well, if the enemy AD carry comes back with spent gold and 100% HP and mana versus your condition, which is not so good because you've greeted on the map, just because you have more gold doesn't mean you are actually stronger in the moment, right? Which is why as a general rule of thumb, you should always try to match your base with the enemy AD carry or even base earlier, right? For tempo and spend your gold, especially when you have a lot of gold, right? Or when you've won a team fight or when you've pushed out a wave, very, very important you always think about how much gold you have in the bank and always try to look to spend it so that you can actually capitalize on the advantage that you have over the enemy bot laner. The second mistake that you're making is that you always die to ganks. So, a mistake that I see a lot of low Elo players do or even just all Elo's that I coach is that they almost greed their wards. You do not want to not use a ward and you almost never, ever want to sit on two trinkets, right? So, it's very, very important that you always try to drop your trinket down when you can, right? So, generally speaking, you know, if your jungle is pathing top side, for example, and you see him finish three camps, ideally you should try to ward tri-bush or the enemy tri-bush, depending on what side you're on, and so you're able to spot up the enemy jungler in case he three camps, right? And also, when your jungle finishes the full clear and he's pathing to top side, for example, you ideally want to put your trinket down because you can infer that if the enemy jungler does not show top side, he's most likely bot side, right? So, there's a very high chance that the enemy jungler could be looking to gank bot. So, it's very, very important, you know, you don't die to these really silly ganks, so very, very obvious, right? Because, you know, this is a great way to, you know, lose your lane uh by dying to a gank, and now, you know, your lane is lost, and also making sure you don't throw the game, right? If you're 5-0, for example, and you're super fed, and then you die to a gank, you know, you might have just thrown the game, right, for your teammates there. So, very, very important, you know, it's to always try to use your trinket ward when you see a good opportunity to do so. The third mistake I see a lot of players do is that they always try to save their support or team. Your job as an AD carry is to do three things. Your job is to try not to die, try to deal as much damage as possible, and try to get as much gold as possible. So, your goal is to always try to do something that fulfills those three conditions, right? So, as you love support AD carry players, when they see the support int, they walk up, try to help them, and then they also die. Or let's just say your teammates take a bad fight, right? They blindly join in the fight, and then they just end up dying in the fight, and then, you know, they say, "Oh, um you know, my teammates inted, my support inted." But the most important thing is League is a puzzle, right? You need to figure out how to avoid those how to find a solution in those [ __ ] situations, right? For example, if your jungler dies 40 seconds, or maybe even 10 seconds before dragon or Baron Nashor, and you don't think you can win the 4v5, instead of running to the fight with your team, and fighting a 4 v 5 and dying. You could be asking yourself instead, "Okay, where can I go on the map to farm gold?" You know, that's one of as I said one of the three conditions of playing AD carry is trying to get gold in your pocket, right? Because you can't win the fight. It's very hard to win a 4 v 5. So, ask yourself, "Can I take jungle camps? Can I push out bot lane? Can I push out the top wave? Can I push out the mid wave? Can I break the towers?" So, always try to do something else on the map that allows you to get more gold in your pocket instead of joining your teammates on [ __ ] plays. The fourth mistake that I see a lot of players do is that they refuse to take accountability. You know, a lot of people love blaming external factors, you know, [ __ ] teammates, [ __ ] support, um losers queue. I'm here to tell you the brutal truth is that none of that [ __ ] is real. That's all [ __ ] You know, if you play well consistently over a period of time, you will always improve and climb, right? If you're always trying to get better at the game. So, it's very important that you know, you try to tie back everything that you do towards the three most important things that that you should be focusing on, right? Which is, you know, try to do as much damage as you can, try to die as little as possible, and [music] try to maximize as much gold as possible, you know. You should always try to be tied down all your decision-making towards these three things uh as an AD carry and not just, you know, blaming your [ __ ] support or your [ __ ] teammates every game, right? Very important you actually look back and self-reflect on your own gameplay if you truly want to have success on this role, right? Because as I said, this role is very obviously reliant to your team to a certain degree, but once you figure out how to fulfill these three conditions consistently and take accountability for your actions, you know, you will have a much more enjoyable time playing League and I'm sure you will also improve a lot more in the long run. The fifth mistake that I see a lot of players do is that they always try to chase kills. You know, the thing about AD carry is that your goal isn't necessarily to kill the enemy bot lane. Your goal, once again, is to get as much gold as possible, right? Like obviously, I'm not telling you guys to never look for the kills, but you're kind of only looking for the kills if the opportunities present themselves essentially. You know, I see a lot of players they over chase kills, they flash forwards way too often to try and greed for the kills. You know, they don't think about their positioning in the fights and they just try to once again use their ability to dash forwards right there. Ezreal E forwards, Kalista E forwards, just down the W way too early. Your job is to deal as much damage as you can. You know, your job isn't to get as many kills as possible because that's not the only way to win a game of League of Legends, right? There's so many ways to get gold in lane, whether that be through a farming minion waves, farming jungle camps, farming towers, helping your teammates secure an objective. There is like so many ways to be able to generate a gold lead that, you know, getting kills is not the only way. So, it's very important you reframe your mind to really just be happy with the small wins in game and not always tunnel vision on chasing and trying to get kills.

titre: League of Legends - Mastery Guide // Champion Points farming, and how you can get a S+ in every Game
transcription: what's up guys it's your boy cenron I recently made a community poll and turns out the majority of you want to know more about how the Mastery system works so today we're going to be focusing on the Mastery system in League of Legends and uncovering the secrets to achieving an S Plus performance in every game so without further Ado let's begin what did I do to deserve all of this why me why now first I get fired from my job then my girlfriend left me for a drave in Maine and now my parents think of me as a failure and won't even speak to me and all that because of one stupid thing why did I choose to one trick Jacks I swear I'm not a pedophile I reckon at this point I need a miracle to fix my life Champion Mastery is a progression system that tracks a player's skill and experience with each champion at the end of every PVP game players receive a grade that scores their performance in comparison to the whole player base in that respective champion and position combination the ranks range from S Plus to D minus with S Plus being the highest in the room next to grades players also additionally gain Mastery points which accumulate over time these points eventually unlock new Champion Mastery levels and Flair as the player advances through the system from what I can tell the champion Point calculation is based on a mix of game length game mode win or loss and group size remember this as we'll get into more detail about it in the champion points farming section of this video for now all you need to know is that a 30 minute game rewards roughly 1 000 Champion points for a win and 180 for a loss Champion Mastery is currently distinguished in Seven Levels the first five of which are achieved at Champion Point thresholds unplayed Champions are considered level zero after playing a game which rewards Champion points the Mastery level immediately enters level 1 and starts progression towards level 2. you need a total of 1 800 Champion points for level 2 6 000 Champion points for level 3 12 600 Champion points for level 4 and 21 600 Champion points for level 5. after reaching Mastery level 5 with their Champion the system to further level up changes this is because level 6 and 7 are instead earned through hextech crafting instead of Mastery points earning an S grade with a champion that is level 5 or level 6 will reward you with a Mastery token these tokens can be used in combination with either a champion Shard or blue Essence to permanently unlock the Master Level 6 or Mastery level 7 grade at Mastery level 5 you receive a Mastery level 6 token with a grade of s minus s or S Plus you need a total of two tokens and either the champion Shard or 2450 blue Essence to unlock Champion Mastery level 6. at Champion Master Level 6 you receive a Mastery level 7 token with a grade of s or S Plus this time you need a total of three tokens and either the champion Shard again or 2950 blue Essence to unlock Champion Mastery level 7. foreign the biggest question you might have right now is why why would you spend time grinding the same Champion over and over again just to get a higher number isn't it much more important to go outside meet new people have fun and maybe live life a bit nah Champion Mastery actually has benefits for one it lets you and others know which Champions you can play and like playing some of your Champion Mastery is openly displayed in the overview section of your profile tab displayed here are the three Champions with the highest level and Mastery score if you want to see your progress on all Champions just head on over to the Champions tab on the collection the first three levels of champion Mastery are fairly unimportant as soon as you reach level 4 however you unlock the Mastery Badger mode loading screen display and announcement Banner the look of these changes depending on what champion level you are currently at the Mastery badge emote is kind of a meme or bming tool nowadays it's meant as a way to display your skill and dedication with a champion in the game you can activate it using either the command slash Mastery badge or with the key combination control plus 6. people mostly only use this if they're toxic and are trying to tilt other people I rarely use this myself but I know some yasu and Kali one tricks that spam this [ __ ] every 12 seconds I personally prefer the loading screen display and announcement Banner changes as they are passive effects and don't require any active input to flex on someone meaning you can fully concentrate on performing well without having to worry about compensating with something you don't have much of I'm talking about key fragments I know you answered Perma chat band speaking of which achieving an S grade or being pre-made with someone who achieved one also grants you a hex tech chest if you haven't received one for the champion you played you can earn a chest every 7 days and can store up to a maximum of four you can see how many you have left to earn under your profile tab under the champion section or in champ select you can see which Champions you've already gotten a chest with this resets every season so if there's only a couple of Champions you like playing don't worry about it too much foreign as previously stated the champion Point calculation is based on a mix of game length game mode win or loss and group size it's currently still not known how the calculation is made there are some official values and theories but no secret formula at least I didn't find one anyways let's move on let's start with game length you simply earn more the longer a game goes this could be objective stun CS accomplished or just minutes passed we don't know but all these things just happen naturally as you play so no need to worry about the details here so the longer the game the better no try to keep the game short and sweet and focus on maximizing the other effects a 35 minute game rewards you with roughly 1 100 Champion points a 15 minute game rewards you with roughly 600 Champion points rounded down to points per minute and voila you can see which one's better now on to the best game mode for grinding Champion points I'd recommend normal games for leveling specific Champions and Aram or a rotating game mode for fun and diversity Aram and rotating game mode games usually last a bit shorter than normal games but these game modes can be heavily RNG favored so choose summoner's Rift for consistency in terms of wins speaking of winds go for wins always you'll receive a huge penalty for losses I'm talking about nearly a thousand Mastery points difference Plus winning is more fun and will keep you motivated you also receive Champion Point bonuses depending on the party size these range from nothing up to a 10 bonus 10 doesn't sound like much but it does add up especially because you need to play more than one game to reach the last level threshold plus I mean come on playing League alone in 2023 really first of all find four people with similar goals to you however these people must be somewhat good on the champion slash delay in their playing already either because they know how to play macro or practice the champ they want to level up in custom games Bot games Smurf accounts or maybe even the PBE server next you want to play to end games early this means that the wind cons can be a bit tricky usually you want to get the gold funneled into a champion that will snowball to dominate the mid to late game now you have to funnel into Champions that will dominate the early to mid game this might mean we're jungle pathing weird roams or just genuinely weird macro altogether but your goal is to make the enemy ff15 or to destroy the Nexus 3 minute 25. you'll have to find a specific strategy here yourself simply because I'm not able to tell you one because I don't know where your win conditions are or what you're playing against but I can tell you that an early orientated playstyle requires you to actively do [ __ ] from the get-go either call out opportunities look for catches or roams deep Wards or look for solo bolos if you can if you're a jungler consider dropping Drakes as Drakes are a late game win condition and you don't want the game to last that long instead look to kill Harold and cross map place it when you see the jungler either on Top Lane or bot Lane or uh just Spam gank the [ __ ] out of those enemies yes try Harding is exhausting but this is technically the fastest way to grind those Champion points no one's forcing you to do this strategy either if losing a couple of games is fine for you and you're in a rush to get that Mastery level up then relax a bit chat with the people you're playing about other things and enjoy the titre: 3 Minute Nautilus Guide - A Guide for League of Legends
transcription: Why play Nautilus jungle? Well, he clears quickly and healthily. His ganks are very solid. He deals good damage for a tank, and he has four different lockdown abilities. However, he does lack mobility, and he is reliant on his team to follow up his plays. Nautilus' passive are his basic attacks are empowered to deal bonus damage and route to the target. However, this can only be applied on each target every 6 seconds. Nautilus' Q is a straight line skill shot, which deals damage to the first target hit, stunning them and dragging both them and Nautilus together. However, if it hits terrain instead, Nautilus dashes towards that terrain, and 50% of the cooldown and mana costs are refunded. Nautilus' W is an auto attack reset, which grants him a shield. While the shield lasts, his basic attacks are empowered to deal bonus magic damage over time to the target hit and enemies around them. Nautilus' E sends out three waves of explosions, which deal damage to enemies hit and slow them. Enemies can be hit by multiple of these, and they take reduced damage after the first, and this ability deals massively increased damage against monsters, making him a solid jungler. Nautilus' ultimate is a point-and-click ability which sends out a depth charge towards an enemy champion. It creates explosions on the way there, dealing damage to enemies hit and knocking them up and stunning them. Upon arrival, it deals damage to the target hit, knocks them up, and stuns them. For combos, start with an attack into your W reset and E. Attack into Q before attacking into alt. If starting Q, follow up with an attack into your W reset. From here, E into attack and alt. And if starting alt, use your ultimate into Q. Attack into your W reset and E before attacking away. For matchups, Nautilus is great into champions who usually rely on their mobility as he just keeps them standing still. And he can struggle into champions that are simply happy to run at him and engage on him. For runes, take this. Aftershock makes your engages even stronger. For build order, start blue monster and pot into Leandre's and tier 2 boots. From here, get a Sunfire or hollow radiant into an undying despair before finishing a build with any of these as needed. For skill order, start E, then W, then take a second point in E before taking Q. From here, max E, then Q, then W, taking alt whenever you can. For summoner spells, take flash and smite. Starting the game, you almost always want to do a full clear. You clear very quickly and healthfully, which puts you in a good position to get a scuttle. And if you do ever need to fight for it, you bring so much lockdown that you can easily keep your opponent in danger while your team collapses. Otherwise, you can just clear one side of your jungle as you have access to a ton of lockdown very early. And this works particularly well if you do have a lane, which brings a ton of damage for you. When looking for a gank, always consider the three main points of where is the lane pushed to, what escapes does the enemy have, and what CC does your team bring. You bring a massive amount of lockdown, so you love laners that just bring tons of damage while you keep them still. Enter in the mid game, you're all about picks and objectives. If you have the man advantage, your ultimate is guaranteed lockdown, which guarantees you'll follow up. These become very easy picks, which you want to convert into objectives, whether they be towers or epic monsters. Entering late game team fights play as your teams engage. Starting the fight by queuing or ulting the enemy carry is just a great way to begin. From here, just get involved and be disruptive. Your E can easily slow the entire enemy team while you keep attacking multiple targets to apply many routes. And that's how to play Nautilus jungle. If this video helped you, leave a like and comment below who you want to see

titre: Season 2024 Gameplay Spotlight | League of Legends
transcription:
Out with the old, in with the new!
Welcome one and all to Season 2024! We’ve got quite a few changes to
share, from terrain adjustments to new monsters and items. Ready to
see what’s coming? Let’s get into it! Wait, where are we?! Oh, it’s top lane!
These walls are different. And would you look at that—the brush is now in the middle of
the river. That will make for a trickier gank path from jungle, so you top laners can focus more
on your lane opponent instead of hungry junglers. We’ve got terrain changes
across the entire map, actually. Like in mid-lane. The brush is farther back from
the lane now, and we’ve adjusted the walls to both red and blue side for better symmetry.
Overall, these adjustments should make it a little safer to play a variety of champions in
midlane—especially those mages with zero dashes! Speaking of safety, we’ve also changed
the walls opposite Baron and dragon pit for a bit more security while you’re
moving between lanes through the jungle. Finally in bot lane, there is a new
gank path on Red side to make the map more symmetrical. We’ve also shifted
the brush to the middle of the river so both teams can more reasonably fight
for vision control. Look alive down there! New jungle paths aren’t the only thing
to keep your eye on this season. A new Void monster has found its way to
the Rift. During the early game, a camp of three Voidgrubs will
now spawn inside the Baron pit. When you attack a grub, smaller Voidmites
appear to join the fight. Killing one of the Voidgrubs grants a PERMANENT buff to
your entire team that deals damage over time when attacking turrets. For every grub
you slay, the buff becomes more powerful. After the first three grubs are killed, three
more will spawn a few minutes later. If your team manages to kill ALL SIX, then you unlock
the maximum buff: When you attack a turret, you’ll apply the damage over time AND spawn
some Voidmites of your own to help take it down. Keep in mind that these grubs are an incremental
camp—not all or nothing. So slaying even one can give your team a small boost (and keep
the enemy from collecting all six)! At 14 minutes, any remaining grubs disappear, and the beloved Rift Herald enters the game… with
a fresh new look. Maybe they’re born with it, maybe it’s the hideously vile influence
of the Void from whence they came! If you slay the Herald, you can summon
them as before to march down lane and whack turrets. Or…any member of your team
can hop on board to play Summoner’s Drift, steering the monster as they
charge forward like a battle sled. Ramming Herald into a turret this way deals some
extra damage, and you can run into enemy champions to damage and knock them up. On collision with
terrain or a turret, the driver pops out nearby, and the ability to steer goes on cooldown.
This cooldown resets if you hit a turret, but takes longer if you hit a wall.
All aboard the Shelly demolition train! Now let’s talk about— what was that? Baron Nashor, Voidborn tyrant of the Rift, is back and badder than ever before.
When he enters the game, you’ll know it. Baron takes one of three forms when he
emerges. Each form provides the same classic buff, but alters how he fights
and determines the shape of his pit. But wait there’s more! With Baron’s
entrance, the Void’s influence spreads across the map, infusing the Scuttle Crab,
Brambleback, and Sentinel with dark power. Voidborn Scuttle Crab becomes tankier
and tougher to kill. On death, she sends out a massive burst like Scryer’s Bloom, revealing all enemy champions and wards in a
large area. Bet you didn’t see that coming. Likewise, the Voidborn red and blue buff
require a little more muscle to take down, but in exchange, they grant the buff
to every teammate who’s alive at the time. So no more fighting over
who gets blue buff, alright? Speaking of cool new buffs, let’s talk Infernal
Rift. All versions of the elemental rift have been refreshed to accommodate the new base
terrain, with Mountain Rift losing a few walls that didn’t quite fit anymore. But
we’ve changed up Infernal Rift completely. We don’t have gameplay footage quite ready at
this time, so let me paint you a word picture. Instead of a burned-out firescape, Infernal Rift
will scatter infernal cinders all across the map. Picking up these cinders gives a small burst
of move speed and a stat buff. On death, half your collected cinders explode out
for others to snag for themselves. I mean, you weren’t using them anymore, right? As in years past, we’ve made some item
adjustments for this new season. Like, a lot of adjustments. For one,
mythic items are out! Gone! Poof! In their place, we’re adding,
removing, and updating a bunch of items across several champion classes.
Mages, assassins, tanks, enchanters, fighters, and marksmen will all notice quite a few changes
when they gear up this season. For example: the new Stormsurge for those AP assassins and
burst mages who really want to shock the enemy. In addition, we overhauled the support quest
items. Supports now have a singular item they pick up at the start of the game with minimal
stats. You earn gold with this item by executing minions of any type for a flat amount or hitting
enemy champions with abilities or attacks. At the middle tier, you unlock refilling wards as
before. And when you reach the final tier, you can choose between several upgraded items
to suit your playstyle. Whether you prefer protecting your carry or more… aggressive
support tactics, we’ve got you covered. While you’re running around the new
map with new monsters and new items, you should keep your eye out for old grudges.
We’ve added a handful of Champion Quests that will trigger when certain champs are in the
same game. Defeating your quest rival on the enemy team will grant a tiny buff. While
it won’t significantly impact the game, it will be a nice reward for you Renekton
mains to finally stick it to Nasus. Many of the changes we’ve made this year are
aimed at making gameplay mechanics and systems easier to understand. This goal includes a
lot of the adjustments mentioned previously, or smaller changes like
simplified tooltip language. We also just want to make it feel better to be on the Rift! Listen closely and you might
notice the music changing throughout the game based on where you are or what
you’re doing. Baron boss music anyone? And now you can celebrate (or
commiserate) with your teammates in a language everyone understands: the fist bump. Oooookay that was a LOT to cover! In summary:
new terrain means new gank paths in every lane Voidgrubs are now a thing
you can drive the Rift Herald into battle Baron looks scarier
And the Void is everywhere Infernal Rift is totally changing
there’s a bunch of new items Some champs are bringing their beef to the Rift
and fistbumps explode into sparkles and fireworks—phew!
Got all that? Good. If you want ALL the nitty gritty details,
we unpack everything in the article linked below. So study up, friends, and we’ll see
you in January when Season 2024 kicks off!

titre: WASD, Smurfing & More | Dev Update - League of Legends
transcription: Hello everyone, I'm Paul, aka Pabro And I'm Andre, also known as Meddler Today is July 28th, and as usual, anything that happens after that won't be covered in this video. Now in this dev update, we want to talk a bit about some things we've got coming over the next few patches, and we also want to talk about a key feature we're working on for League's future. But first, Season 3. This season we're going to be pulling characters and storylines from Noxus and Spirit Blossom together. LeBlanc, Yunara, Atakhan, and Xin Zhao will all be playing a vital role as we continue. And we're super excited to return to China this year, where we'll be celebrating 15 years of worlds. We'll also be returning to the base Summoner's Rift map. We've been enjoying seeing all of your reactions to the new maps. And if you're a new map lover, don't worry, we've also still got more in store for the future. For now though, let's talk about some of the things you'll see in-game real soon. Okay, we need to talk about Xin Zhao. Many of you have been wondering why he's been a part of Spirit Blossom without actually having his new looks reflected in-game. And that's a great question. Great question. Xin is one of the most popular jungle champions, and he's also just celebrated his 15th birthday. And while he's gotten some updates to his gameplay over the years, his appearance and animations remain stuck in the past. Now, a lot of older champions are in need of visual updates, and we're working through the roster to update them, while also slotting them into Seasons that make sense. So, we wanted to give Xin Zhao a visual update, similar to what we did with LeBlanc earlier this year. Xin Zhao's gameplay is entirely untouched, as we feel his kit holds up really well. Outside of his base skin, all of the skins have been updated, and his current vaulted skins will be unvaulted for two weeks. So if you want to grab them, they'll be available for patches 25.17 and 25.18. Commando and Imperial Xin Zhao have also gotten additional upgrades, which have pushed them from 5.20 RP to 7.50 RP. You can still pick them both up at the lower price until Xin's update goes live in patch 25.17. And that's it for them. Yep, nothing else. Okay, yeah, yeah, yeah, he's getting a new Epic skin as well. That'll be out later in Act I. Speaking of skins, a few quick updates. Sylas and Mel will be receiving Prestige skins in the Mythic Shop, and J4 and Zoe will get Prestige skins on the Battle Pass in Acts 1 and 2, respectively. Now, we'll also be moving the Exalted Viego skin that we previously mentioned, and passing to next year, since we want to take a bit more time to get that skin right. And finally, for Viktor Nation, we've got an important message. We do indeed. Viktor mains. We've heard you. Machine Herald Viktor will be coming out in patch 25.19, and will be giving it to all of you Viktor lovers for free if you had Mastery 5 or above as of August 4th. Otherwise, for everyone else, he'll be available in the Shop. We also have a small update to Battle Passes. Passes are slightly harder to complete than we intended, so we're going to be lowering the levels on the Battle Pass from 50 to 48 as a result. The amount of time between levels will stay the same, so you should see slightly faster completion. We won't be removing any rewards from the track, just condensing the Pass a little. We're also adding some emotes and icons to the Mythic Shop that were previously only earnable by completing TFT passes. So, you can finally go back and pick up some of your fan favorite emotes from years ago if you never got the chance to get them. So yeah, Doombots is back. Last year, when we released our updated bot AI, we mentioned that this could have other applications that utilize bots. And we, of course, saw many of you ask, what would a certain PvE mode that used bots be like with this? Now, Doombots pits you and four other teammates against a somehow even seriously more evil Veigar, whose armed with some of the meanest bots we've ever created. The goal? Survive and protect your nexus. It's got new bots, a new boss, a new difficulty mode, and new curses and missions. We've even made Blitzcrank bot hook push minions out of the way, so enjoy that one. Now, in the past, he told us that Doombots wasn't hard enough, so he made something we're calling Trials of Doom, a unique challenge each patch with an obstacle that you need to overcome while fighting tougher bots, like what if curses stack up over the game, and what if all the bots had Veigar's passive? Now, we know what you're wondering. At least some of you, hey, will other older modes also get a return? And the answer right now is, aye, maybe. We don't have any immediate plans to bring any other modes back from the past, but we're open to exploring it. In the meantime, though, be on the lookout for some seriously doomed games against these high-powered AI. And if you're looking to learn more, we have a blog out today to cover more about this update to Doombots. So, we have a key update that we want to talk about that we believe will help new players, in particular. WASD controls. Hold on, Andrejondo. Is it WASDee? Is it W-A-S-D? Is it WASD? Is it Wassup I don't know, chat? Let us know. But for now, what do you call it? It's WASD. WASD, alright, he said it. Anyway, we found that a lot of newer returning players struggle a lot with League's controls early on. Click to Move just isn't the sort of control scheme they're used to, and it puts a lot of people off, including many that we believe would otherwise really love the game. So, we're introducing WASD as an alternate control scheme to help get new players to the fun parts of League faster and with less friction. We're going to hand things over to the members of the WASD team to talk more about how and when you can get your hands on it and what you can expect. Hey gamers, I'm Dashiejador, the producer for WASD. And I'm Dean, the designer for WASD. We've been working on WASD for a while now, and we want to get it in your hands to test. Ultimately, our goal with WASD is to make playing League more intuitive for first-time players or those returning, without changing what makes League, League. That said, we're long-time League players ourselves and have been using point-and-click for years. So, we've been working hard to ensure that both control schemes feel great across all ranks, champions, and modes. In order to make sure that's true, we've been gathering tons of feedback from our internal playtest team, from brand new players, and from pro players at MSI. And now, we're ready to get even more feedback from all of you. WASD is going to come to PBE in the next few patches, and it'll stay there for an extended run. We're taking a slower-than-usual rollout, where we'll start on PBE, we'll move to unranked queues, and eventually, into ranked. Our goal is to make sure we have the time to gather feedback and act on it before full release. We're rolling it out this way, because we want to make sure that we can catch any bugs, unintended interactions, and to ensure that your ranked games aren't disrupted by someone WASD-ing into walls. We've discovered and resolved a lot of edge-case scenarios while in development, so we're hopeful we got rid of the most atrocious bugs. One of the earliest bugs led to a few playtests where Nunu could do this sick 180 instantly while snowballing, and that unlocked a new fear for many of us. Now, let's talk about balance. Our goal is to make sure WASD is an awesome way to play League for the players who want it, but no one should feel forced to switch control schemes just to stay competitive. One thing we'll be keeping a close eye on is how WASD affects balance between moving and attacking, especially at high attack speeds. With point-and-click, kiting or chasing someone down while attacking takes serious precision, and WASD controls can make this a lot easier. This is something on everyone's minds, so we're going into PBE very aware of it, and we'll be ready to make adjustments if we need to. Beyond WASD movement and improving the way the champion feels while in WASD, we feel there should be a few other things that can be improved for both control schemes. So we're making some updates to those spaces, like key binding and camera. For key bindings, we're exploring a lot more finite controls for players to be able to set whatever they want. For example, we'll let you bind the left-click on your mouse without having to edit your system files for the first time in League's history. We're also adding a new version of locked camera called Dynamic Locked Camera. It extends your view towards your mouse, and our hope is to get the simplicity of locked camera, but with some flexibility to see more of the action when you need to. Both of these updates are going to be locked to WASD at first, and the only reason we want to do that is to make sure all WASD-related features are in a great state before we roll them out to everybody. While WASD is on PBE, we'll be consistently shipping improvements and tweaks based on your feedback. We're still in an experimental state, so you'll likely see changes patch-to-patch. And that's why we really need your feedback. So when WASD launches in a couple of patches, go to the PBE, try every champion, find the weirdest bugs, and tell us all about it. Seriously, some of you are some of the most creative and wildiest players out there, and we need you. So grab a friend, get to the PBE, and WASD your way down the rift. Make sure to give us feedback and help us make a fun control scheme for new players. That's it from us! If you want to learn more, you can check out the blog. It's out now. We're also working on some smaller stuff aimed at helping new players get into League too. There are some things in League that we believe are unnecessarily hard to track, or just a bit of a chore, that contribute to League being a hard game to learn. So we're going to be adding jungle timers to all camps, as well as making it easier to track Summoner Spell usage in the Death Recap screen. We've also got some additional changes planned in this sort of vein coming later in the year, and more on those in a future dev update. Hey all, I'm Riot Phroxzon And I'm Just A Capybara. We're here to talk more about the ongoing work we've been doing to reduce game-ruining behaviour. Earlier this year, we began issuing LP refunds for only detected cheaters in your League games. While also banning them, of course. After that, we started rolling out improvements to our griefing and inting detection to make sure these sorts of people don't end up in your games. With all the work that we're doing for the future of League, we also know that the element we need to maintain above all else is League's competitive integrity. And in order to keep that reality, we need to give you games with 9 other players of similar skill levels. We've heard your frustrations over the years, and we know that you want us to do more about smurfing. And we agree. It sucks, and it makes your games worse. So starting in patch 25.18, we're going to be taking additional action against accounts that use the two most common smurfing behaviours we see. Boosting and Hitchhiking. Simply put, boosting is when someone else plays on your account to climb to a higher rank, and hitchhiking is when a higher skilled player duos on a fraudulent, usually purchased account with a lower skilled player to artificially increase their rank. We'll be taking action on both accounts in these situations, both the booster and the boostee. One of the reasons we're doing more now is because Vanguard has given us the increased capability and accuracy we need to determine if accounts are being shared or sold, which is what enables most smurfing. Back in 25.13, we added a new Rank Manipulation Report category so you could let us know when you believe someone is boosting, hitchhiking, deranking, or whatever. Your reports were instrumental in helping us gather a bunch of data to improve our detection systems, and we'll be using a combination of our detection systems and reports to punish these accounts. You'll also get a feedback report so you'll know when you've helped us find these accounts. And we're going to be doubling down on banning accounts that are being bought, sold, or shared, regardless of whether or not they're used in ranked smurfing. For those of you who are wondering if this applies to your alts, if you're the only one who's ever played on your alt and you aren't engaging in ranked manipulation, you're fine. So if you've got another account that you created and leveled on your own to learn another role or play norms with lower skilled friends or whatever, don't worry. We're not coming after you. And if we ever do get it wrong, let us know by filing an appeal with our player support team. We are in the process of banning a bunch of accounts that were commercially bought and meant to be resold. We're going to go through and also ban a bunch of accounts that have been sold or shared that people use as part of a roster of alts to do various bad things. Another longer term project we're working on that will also help reduce smurfing is True Skill 2 with its increased MMR detection accuracy. We started testing it in North America in March in normal SR and ARAM. We then fixed some issues and expanded testing to all unranked queues in Korea last month. We're seeing players placed correctly in about five games, which is three to five times faster than our current system. We know League's matchmaking is super important to you and to us. It's important to us that we meet those expectations and we know we have more work to do to get us there. That's it for us today, but we'll be back in October to share another update on this work. If you want to learn more, check out the dev blog that's live now. All right, we know this is a long one. So here are some final updates. We'll kick off World’s with the 2025 Anthem on October 10th, which will honor and celebrate all of the legends of the sport from over the years. Then games will kick off in Beijing on October 14th with finals in Chengdu on November 9th. Now, we'd love your feedback on anything, of course, but we'd especially love to get all of your takes on WASD as soon as that hits PBE soon. We'll work on a number of things we think are really important for the long term future of League. WASD is the first one up and the one we're in a position to talk about right now. So please give us all your takes and we'll talk about some other efforts in a little while. We've got some dev blogs going out right now, so please check those out and let us know your thoughts. And that's it. So until next time, see ya. Thanks, all. WASD.
